This is clear because then X𝑋X is saturated almost completely by X2subscript𝑋2X2 and this quantity alone fixes |γ2|subscript𝛾2|gamma2|. The use of Eq. allows to determine |γ1|subscript𝛾1|gamma1|, which is certainly larger than the “effective” one deduced by fulfilling Eq. with an intermediate plus sign. However, the values for X2subscript𝑋2X2, Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2 and the physical partial-decay width to channel 1 almost do not change, which are typically the most important pieces of information to infer about the nature of the resonances. Now, if rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp is taken as input the equations that one has to fulfill are Eq. and 2−rexp=|γ1|2​p1​(mR2)8​π​mR2​ΓR,2subscript𝑟expsuperscriptsubscript𝛾12subscript𝑝1superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑅28𝜋subscriptsuperscript𝑚2𝑅subscriptΓ𝑅displaystyle 2-rrm exp=|gamma1|2fracp1(mR2)8pi m2RGammaR, which are apparently very different to the “standard” Eq. and rexp=Γ1ΓR,subscript𝑟expsubscriptΓ1subscriptΓ𝑅displaystyle rrm exp=fracGamma1GammaR, already written for a pole in the RS @slowromancapiii@ in Sec. . However, they basically provide again the same results for Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2, X2subscript𝑋2X2, and the physical partial-decay width to channel 1, while the use of Eqs. and provides the already mentioned “effective” value for |γ1|subscript𝛾1|gamma1|. In order to see it, let us divide Eq. by ΓRsubscriptΓ𝑅GammaR and write the following equations equivalent to Eqs. and , 11displaystyle 1 =Γ1ΓR−Γ2ΓR,absentsubscriptΓ1subscriptΓ𝑅subscriptΓ2subscriptΓ𝑅displaystyle=fracGamma1GammaR-fracGamma2GammaR, 2−rexp2subscript𝑟expdisplaystyle 2-rrm exp =Γ1ΓR. absentsubscriptΓ1subscriptΓ𝑅displaystyle=fracGamma1GammaR. Subtracting the first line from the second one we then have 1−rexp1subscript𝑟expdisplaystyle 1-rrm exp =Γ2ΓR,absentsubscriptΓ2subscriptΓ𝑅displaystyle=fracGamma2GammaR, which fixes |γ2|subscript𝛾2|gamma2| as if Eq. were used. Afterwards, Eq. is employed to calculate |γ1|subscript𝛾1|gamma1|, while the use of Eq. would provide the so-called “effective” value for |γ1|subscript𝛾1|gamma1|, which would be smaller than the one obtained from Eq. .
content_sections.IV Results and discussions using the tot: Here we apply the formalism derived in Secs. and to study the nature of the resonances f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0. Subsequently, the former method based on the saturation of ΓRsubscriptΓ𝑅GammaR and X𝑋X is denoted by S, while the latter one based on the use of a Flatté parametrization is called F𝐹F. For each resonance pole we first apply the method S and then F.

Assuming given values for the total compositeness X𝑋X of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0, varying it from 0.20.20.2 to 1.01.01.0 in steps of 0.2, we obtain the couplings, partial-decay widths, and compositeness coefficients by solving Eq. and Eq. . We indicate that it is not possible to find solutions for the parameters to reproduce X=0𝑋0X=0, because then the couplings would be zero which is in contradiction of having a finite width. As already mentioned, we consider the RS @slowromancapii@ where sRsubscript𝑠𝑅sR of Eq. lies citebib.bib78 and the results calculated are shown in Table. One can observe from this table that the compositeness coefficient X2subscript𝑋2X2 is always much larger than X1subscript𝑋1X1, which means that the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK channel plays a much more important role than the π​π𝜋𝜋pipi one in the structure of f0​subscript𝑓0980f0. Related to this point, the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 couples much more strongly to K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK than to π​π𝜋𝜋pipi, in agreement with the fact that f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 sits very close to the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold. We also observe from Table that as the total compositeness X𝑋X increases the physical partial-decay width to π​π𝜋𝜋pipi (here, for a RSII pole, Γπ​π=Γ1−2​Γ2subscriptΓ𝜋𝜋subscriptΓ12subscriptΓ2Gammapipi=Gamma1-2Gamma2) decreases, while that to K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK becomes larger. Similarly, this fact has also been found in other heavy-quark resonances with open and near-threshold channels citebib.bib69, bib.bib67, bib.bib68, bib.bib94. In these regards, it follows from Table that ΓK​K¯>Γπ​πsubscriptΓ𝐾¯𝐾subscriptΓ𝜋𝜋GammaKbarK>Gammapipi for X≳0.6greater-than-or-equivalent-to𝑋0.6Xgtrsim 0.6,7This can be easily seen because ΓK​K¯>25subscriptΓ𝐾¯𝐾25GammaKbarK>25MeV. but as X𝑋X decreases Γπ​πsubscriptΓ𝜋𝜋Gammapipi becomes larger than ΓK​K¯subscriptΓ𝐾¯𝐾GammaKbarK. For estimating the errors associated to the input values of the resonance pole mass and width we proceed in this work similarly as done in Ref. citebib.bib64. Then, we discretize the inputs, mRsubscript𝑚𝑅mR and ΓRsubscriptΓ𝑅GammaR from the pole position at several points within one standard deviation region from the central values to generate a data grid. For each of the points in the grid we proceed to calculate the different outputs so that their central values correspond to the mean values and the errors to the square root of the variances. We have also checked that this procedure is (of course) stable if the number of points in the grid is increased. For the other input X𝑋X the variation in the results calculated for the different values of X𝑋X provides an estimate of this source of uncertainty. We now consider the application of the method F, take X=0.2𝑋0.2X=0.2 to 0.8 in steps of 0.2, and calculate the parameters characterizing the Flatté formula, Eq. , that is, the bare width to the lighter channel Γ1subscriptΓ1widetildeGamma1, the bare coupling squared g2subscript𝑔2g2 to K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK and Efsubscript𝐸𝑓Ef. As typical outputs we provide Γ1subscriptΓ1Gamma1, Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2, X1subscript𝑋1X1 and X2subscript𝑋2X2. For the pole position of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 we take Eq. , with the results given in Table. For this method the extreme value X=1𝑋1X=1 cannot be reproduced, while X=0𝑋0X=0 is in contradiction with having a finite width as for the method S. Comparing the values of X2subscript𝑋2X2 for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 between Table and the previous Table we see a good agreement, with the difference in the central values affecting the third decimal figure. This close agreement can be explained, though not completely, by noticing that X2≫X1much-greater-thansubscript𝑋2subscript𝑋1X2gg X1 so that X2≈Xsubscript𝑋2𝑋X2approx X, which fixes it to lie very close to the total compositeness. Regarding the ΓisubscriptΓ𝑖Gammai we see that they are very close between both tables for X=0.2𝑋0.2X=0.2, 0.4 and 0.6, with larger differences for X=0.8𝑋0.8X=0.8, but perfectly compatible within errors for all values of X𝑋X. One should stress that the method based on the Flatté parametrization directly provides ΓisubscriptΓ𝑖Gammai, since Γ1=Γ1​βsubscriptΓ1subscriptΓ1𝛽Gamma1=widetildeGamma1beta and Γ2=Γ1−ΓRsubscriptΓ2subscriptΓ1subscriptΓ𝑅Gamma2=Gamma1-GammaR for a pole in the RS @slowromancapii@, as it is the case here for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 poles in Eqs. and . However, the method S in Sec. keeps some spurious dependence on the value given to n𝑛n for Eq. , though a judicious choice gives rise to remarkably compatible results between both methods, as indicated. We now move on and consider the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 pole position from Ref. citebib.bib75 given in Eq. , and proceed similarly as done regarding the pole position in Eq. . Then, we reproduce the values of the mass and width of the resonance in Eq. together with a value given for X𝑋X. The results obtained are shown in Table. Comparing Tables and we observe that all the outputs are rather similar. In particular the resulting values for X2subscript𝑋2X2 are almost coincident in both Tables, with X2≫X1much-greater-thansubscript𝑋2subscript𝑋1X2gg X1. However, we notice that while the central value of the width for the pole in Eq. is smaller than that in the pole of Eq. , the calculated Γ1subscriptΓ1Gamma1 and Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2 in Table are larger than those in Table. This indicates that the cancellation between widths in the difference Γ1−2​Γ2subscriptΓ12subscriptΓ2Gamma1-2Gamma2 for calculating the actual width to π​π𝜋𝜋pipi is more important in the pole of Eq. than for the one in Eq. . For the method F the results are given in Table. We observe that the values of X2subscript𝑋2X2 obtained are very similar to those given in Table for the same input X𝑋X, with X2≫X1much-greater-thansubscript𝑋2subscript𝑋1X2gg X1. The output Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2 is smaller now than that in Table, though within errors they are again compatible. This clearly indicates the compatibility between the two methods for this f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 pole too. We also point out that the bare parameters for X=0.8𝑋0.8X=0.8 in Table are essentially undetermined, as indicated by the large intervals given between parenthesis, though the physical outputs appear with an uncertainty of similar size as that in the other rows of the table.

Let us explore the compositeness of the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 similarly as done above in Sec. for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0. The poles that we are going to consider next for the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 stems from a recent coupled-channel partial-wave analysis of antiproton-proton annihilation data in Ref. citebib.bib72, where the pole parameters and the partial-decay width of the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 are discussed. In the RS @slowromancapii@ Ref. citebib.bib72 reports the values ma0=1004.1±6.67​MeV,Γa0=97.2±6.01​MeV,ΓK​K¯/Γπ​η=(13.8±3.5)%,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑚subscript𝑎0plus-or-minus1004.16.67MeVformulae-sequencesubscriptΓsubscript𝑎0plus-or-minus97.26.01MeVsubscriptΓ𝐾¯𝐾subscriptΓ𝜋𝜂percentplus-or-minus13.83.5displaystyle ma0=1004.1pm 6.67,,textMeV,Gammaa0=97.2pm 6.01,,textMeV,GammaKbarK/Gammapieta=(13.8pm 3.5),,%, while in the RS @slowromancapiii@ the same reference provides ma0=1002.4±6.55​MeV,Γa0=127.0±7.08​MeV,ΓK​K¯/Γπ​η=(14.9±3.9)%,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑚subscript𝑎0plus-or-minus1002.46.55MeVformulae-sequencesubscriptΓsubscript𝑎0plus-or-minus127.07.08MeVsubscriptΓ𝐾¯𝐾subscriptΓ𝜋𝜂percentplus-or-minus14.93.9displaystyle ma0=1002.4pm 6.55,,textMeV,Gammaa0=127.0pm 7.08,,textMeV,GammaKbarK/Gammapieta=(14.9pm 3.9),,%, with the uncertainties added in quadrature. Let us denote by 1 the lighter channel π​η𝜋𝜂pieta, and by 2 the heavier one K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK. Combining Eq. for the total compositeness and Eq. for the full width, given input values for the former between 0.2 and 1.0 in steps of 0.2, we derive a series of partial compositeness coefficients and decay widths for the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 in Table by applying the method S.8The same remark as for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 in the footnote can also be applied for the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 when its pole is taken in the RS @slowromancapii@ for applying Eq. to calculate X2subscript𝑋2X2. The point is that the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 mass in Eq. is clearly larger than the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold but the width of the resonance is much larger than the difference between ma0subscript𝑚subscript𝑎0ma0 and the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold. We find that X2subscript𝑋2X2 is rather similar for the RS @slowromancapii@ and @slowromancapiii@ calculations. We also obtain that X2≫X1much-greater-thansubscript𝑋2subscript𝑋1X2gg X1, except for X=0.2𝑋0.2X=0.2 in which case they are quite close to each other. This tells us that the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK component typically dominates over the π​η𝜋𝜂pieta one for the resonance a0​subscript𝑎0980a0. The results corresponding to the method F for the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 case are organized in Table. One then finds that the values of X2subscript𝑋2X2 obtained in Tables and are very close independently of the RS in which the poles lie. It turns out that X2subscript𝑋2X2 is typically much larger than X1subscript𝑋1X1 except when X𝑋X becomes small, like X=0.2𝑋0.2X=0.2 in Table, and then both Xisubscript𝑋𝑖Xi are rather close to each other. Regarding the ΓisubscriptΓ𝑖Gammai it is clear that for the pole in the RS @slowromancapii@ the values obtained now in Table are remarkably similar to those in Table. However, they clearly differ for X≥0.8𝑋0.8Xgeq 0.8 for the RS @slowromancapiii@ case, with no solution found for X=1𝑋1X=1. Of course, X=0𝑋0X=0 cannot be reproduced in any method S or F due to the finite width of the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0, which requires non-vanishing couplings. As indicated above one should distinguish between the bare and dressed parameters in a Flatté parametrization. This reflects in the fact that the bare width Γ1subscriptΓ1widetildeGamma1 can be much larger than the total width ΓRsubscriptΓ𝑅GammaR. This is particularly true when the pole position is set to lie in the RS @slowromancapii@, as it is clear from the Tables, and . Here in addition one has to properly relate Γ1subscriptΓ1Gamma1 and Γπ​πsubscriptΓ𝜋𝜋Gammapipi, cf. Eq. . Nonetheless, these important points have been overlooked in the literature. Before finishing this section we should mention that in some studies the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 lies in the RS @slowromancapiv@, so that there is no an interval along the physical real s𝑠s-axis within the radius of convergence of the Laurent series around the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0. In such a scenario it is not justified to apply Eq. for the calculation of X2subscript𝑋2X2, as discussed in detail in Ref. citebib.bib62, and it is not either clear how to connect the ΓisubscriptΓ𝑖Gammai with the physical partial-decay widths. Then, it follows that our method (as well as the older Ref. citebib.bib55) cannot be applied and the conclusions thereof do not hold. In the studies based on unitarization of SU and U chiral perturbation theory it is observed that the pole for the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 appears in the 2nd RS if only tree level amplitudes are kept. This is necessarily the case when unitarizing the leading order amplitudes citebib.bib28, bib.bib25, bib.bib42, bib.bib73. However, once loop contributions are accounted for the pole moves to the RS @slowromancapiv@ citebib.bib95, bib.bib75, bib.bib73. This is also the case in the K𝐾K-matrix analysis of Ref. citebib.bib74. The more recent studies among those quoted here citebib.bib73, bib.bib74 also reproduce the energy levels from lattice QCD simulations. When the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 lies in the RS @slowromancapiv@ it manifests on the physical real s𝑠s-axis as a strong cusp effect.
content_sections.V Results and discussions using the bran: Here, we directly explore the more stringent scenario of taking rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp, together with the usual reproduction of the pole position ERsubscript𝐸𝑅ER, in order to fix all the parameters both in the S and F methods. The direct consideration of rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp as input is an interesting check on the consistency of interpreting the ΓisubscriptΓ𝑖Gammai, calculated from the pole parameters, in connection with the experimental rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp for a pole lying in the RS @slowromancapii@. As shown below, the resulting physical-partial-decay widths have values within meaningful ranges, such that they are positive and smaller than the total width, while the Xisubscript𝑋𝑖Xi and their sum X𝑋X also lie within the allowed interval [0,1]01[0,1].9Otherwise, if not correctly understanding rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp as proposed for a RS @slowromancapii@ pole, there will be no solution within the method F developed in Sec. . This situation is similar to the one obtained before when taking X𝑋X as input, where all the Flatté parameters are fixed without the need to connect the ΓisubscriptΓ𝑖Gammai with rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp, which comes out as an output. We also show below that the procedure of providing rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp as input is not completely satisfactory because the output value for the renormalized coupling squared to K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK, |γ2|2superscriptsubscript𝛾22|gamma2|2, can be very sensitive to small variations in the input. As a result, the calculation of X2subscript𝑋2X2 by using Eq. does not come out very accurate, particularly for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0. We can circumvent this limitation by then using the method of the spectral function ω​(E)𝜔𝐸omega(E) to estimate X𝑋X with better precision. With this choice we also update values for the resonance inputs compared with the almost two decades old Ref. citebib.bib55, where the spectral density function to calculate the compositeness was applied for the first time to the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 resonances. In this respect, it is important to indicate that nowadays we have at our disposal the precise determination of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 pole from the Roy-like GKPY equations, Eq. .

Now, let us directly combine the knowledge of ERsubscript𝐸𝑅ER with the input for the branching ratio rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp, rexp=0.52±0.12subscript𝑟expplus-or-minus0.520.12rrm exp=0.52pm 0.12 citebib.bib71 and rexp=0.75−0.13+0.11subscript𝑟expsubscriptsuperscript0.750.110.13rrm exp=0.75+0.11-0.13 citebib.bib96, as well as rexp=0.68subscript𝑟exp0.68rrm exp=0.68 from the theoretical analysis of Ref. citebib.bib25. Notice that in this way we consider a broad range of values for rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp from around 0.40 up to 0.86, taking into account the spread in the central values within one standard deviation. As a result, instead of selecting a specific experimental input, rather we try to extract an image for the nature of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 compatible with the present rather imprecise experimental knowledge in rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp. We start by applying the method S. It is clear that given rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp and ΓRsubscriptΓ𝑅GammaR one can solve for the couplings |γi|subscript𝛾𝑖|gammai|, i=1, 2𝑖12i=1,,2, and calculate the partial compositeness coefficients Xisubscript𝑋𝑖Xi. Since the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 poles that we are considering lie in the RS @slowromancapii@ we have that Γπ​π=(2−rexp)​ΓRsubscriptΓ𝜋𝜋2subscript𝑟expsubscriptΓ𝑅Gammapipi=(2-rrm exp)GammaR and ΓK​K¯=(1−rexp)​ΓRsubscriptΓ𝐾¯𝐾1subscript𝑟expsubscriptΓ𝑅GammaKbarK=(1-rrm exp)GammaR, and similarly for the RS @slowromancapii@ pole for the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0, Γπ​η=(2−rexp)​ΓRsubscriptΓ𝜋𝜂2subscript𝑟expsubscriptΓ𝑅Gammapieta=(2-rrm exp)GammaR. For the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 pole in the RS @slowromancapiii@ we have instead Γπ​η=rexp​ΓRsubscriptΓ𝜋𝜂subscript𝑟expsubscriptΓ𝑅Gammapieta=rrm expGammaR. By combining Eqs. , and the value of the compositeness coefficients X1subscript𝑋1X1 and X2subscript𝑋2X2 can be written as X1subscript𝑋1displaystyle X1 =displaystyle= 8​π​(2−rexp)​ΓR​mR2p1​(mR2)​|∂G1​(s)∂s|s=sR,RS@slowromancapii@,8𝜋2subscript𝑟expsubscriptΓ𝑅superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑅2subscript𝑝1superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑅2subscriptsubscript𝐺1𝑠𝑠𝑠subscript𝑠𝑅RS@slowromancapii@displaystylefrac8pi(2-rrm exp)GammaRmR2p1(mR2)left|fracpartial G1(s)partial sright|s=sR,textRS @slowromancap ii@, X1subscript𝑋1displaystyle X1 =displaystyle= 8​π​rexp​ΓR​mR2p1​(mR2)​|∂G1​(s)∂s|s=sR,RS@slowromancapiii@,8𝜋subscript𝑟expsubscriptΓ𝑅superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑅2subscript𝑝1superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑅2subscriptsubscript𝐺1𝑠𝑠𝑠subscript𝑠𝑅RS@slowromancapiii@displaystylefrac8pi rrm expGammaRmR2p1(mR2)left|fracpartial G1(s)partial sright|s=sR,textRS @slowromancap iii@, X2subscript𝑋2displaystyle X2 =displaystyle= 16​π2​(1−rexp)​ΓR∫m1+m2mR+2​ΓR𝑑W​p​(W2)/W2(mR−W)2+ΓR2/4​|∂G2​(s)∂s|s=sR.16superscript𝜋21subscript𝑟expsubscriptΓ𝑅superscriptsubscriptsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2subscript𝑚𝑅2subscriptΓ𝑅differential-d𝑊𝑝superscript𝑊2superscript𝑊2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑅𝑊2superscriptsubscriptΓ𝑅24subscriptsubscript𝐺2𝑠𝑠𝑠subscript𝑠𝑅displaystylefrac16pi2(1-rrm exp)GammaRintm1+m2mR+2GammaRdWfracp(W2)/W2(mR-W)2+GammaR2/4left|fracpartial G2(s)partial sright|s=sR. The results are organized in Table with mRsubscript𝑚𝑅mR and ΓRsubscriptΓ𝑅GammaR taken from Eq. , corresponding to the determination in Ref. citebib.bib78. For this and the rest of tables in this section the error bars given to X2subscript𝑋2X2 and X𝑋X contain also the propagation of the error in the determination of rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp from Refs. citebib.bib71, bib.bib96. It follows from this table that X2subscript𝑋2X2 is affected by large error bars, around a 50%, so that the resulting X𝑋X is not pined down accurately and the calculation is rather indicative. Only for rexp=0.52±0.12subscript𝑟expplus-or-minus0.520.12rrm exp=0.52pm 0.12 citebib.bib71 one has that X𝑋X can be larger than 0.5 within errors, with X=0.49±0.20𝑋plus-or-minus0.490.20X=0.49pm 0.20. For the other experimental value rexp=0.75−0.13+0.11subscript𝑟expsubscriptsuperscript0.750.110.13rrm exp=0.75+0.11-0.13 citebib.bib96 the compositeness X=0.27±0.11𝑋plus-or-minus0.270.11X=0.27pm 0.11 and it is smaller than 0.5. We show in the left panel of Fig. the total compositeness X𝑋X as a function of rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 with the method S, and employing the central values for mRsubscript𝑚𝑅mR and ΓRsubscriptΓ𝑅GammaR for the different poles considered. Thus, the requirement that rexp>0.4subscript𝑟exp0.4rrm exp>0.4 implies that X≲0.6less-than-or-similar-to𝑋0.6Xlesssim 0.6 for the pole in Eq. in the RS @slowromancapii@. It is also clear from Fig. the linear decrease of X𝑋X with rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp. This can be easily understood by noticing that both the partial-decay widths and partial compositeness coefficients are proportional to |γi|2superscriptsubscript𝛾𝑖2|gammai|2, which can be written in turn as Γi/θisubscriptΓ𝑖subscript𝜃𝑖Gammai/thetai. When compared with X𝑋X from Table, given in the last column in Tables and , we see a quantitative agreement in the case of the RS @slowromancapiii@ pole, and a semiquantitative one for the RS @slowromancapii@ one. The emerging picture is that X𝑋X is clearly less than 0.5, ranging between 0.2−0.40.20.40.2-0.4 depending on the method of calculation. Therefore, other components beyond π​η𝜋𝜂pieta and K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK are also required citebib.bib37, bib.bib38. However, if the resonance lied in RS @slowromancapiv@, as preferred by the recent analyses citebib.bib73, bib.bib74, our approach does not apply and we cannot extend such conclusion to that case. We would also like to comment about the clearly visible cusp effect for most of the curves of ω​(E)𝜔𝐸omega(E) in Fig. . This change in the shape of ω​(E)𝜔𝐸omega(E) below and above the two-kaon threshold is due to the fact that if the resonance pole lies in the RS @slowromancapii@ (@slowromancapiii@) then there is no associated pole in the RS @slowromancapiii@ (@slowromancapii@) above (below) the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold. Precisely the RS III (II) is the one that connects with the physical region there.
content_sections.VI Summary and conclusions: This paper discusses the importance of the continuum channels π​π𝜋𝜋pipi-K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK and π​η𝜋𝜂pieta-K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK in the composition of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 resonances, which is quantified by the concept of the total compositeness X𝑋X. In our calculation we exploit the tight relationship between the compositeness X𝑋X, the mass and the decay width of a resonance. The threshold of the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK pair is very close to the mass of each resonance and this fact has main consequences in our results. We develop two methods: One is based on saturating the total width and compositeness; the other relies on the use of a Flatté parametrization and, in some instances, of the spectral function of a near-threshold resonance. We provide input values for the mass and width of each resonance by taking their pole positions from relevant analyses in the literature. In particular, for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 we consider the determination of its pole position by the Roy-like GKPY equations. Regarding the third input needed in our analyses we first take input values for X𝑋X in the compositeness relationship and we predict the couplings, partial compositeness coefficients and partial-decay widths to the π​π𝜋𝜋pipi (π​η𝜋𝜂pieta) and K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK channels for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 (a0)a0). There is an interesting trend in the results such that the larger X𝑋X, the smaller the branching decay ratio to the lighter channel, rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp. This is due to the increase of the coupling to the heavier K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK channel with increasing X𝑋X, compensating the reduced phase space available for the decay of the resonances into this channel. It is also found that for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 the partial compositeness coefficient of K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK, X2subscript𝑋2X2, is larger by orders of magnitude than the corresponding one to π​π𝜋𝜋pipi, X1subscript𝑋1X1. For the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 the compositeness for K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK is also larger than the one associated to π​η𝜋𝜂pieta, but as X𝑋X decreases they tend to become similar in size. Another possibility is to replace the third input X𝑋X by reported values in the literature for rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp. However, if X2subscript𝑋2X2 is calculated in terms of the coupling squared to K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK and the derivative of the unitary-loop function in the corresponding Riemann sheet, we typically find a large sensitivity on the input value for rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp. The situation is improved when using the method based on integrating the spectral density function around the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold along the energy region comprising the resonance signal, so that more stable results are obtained under small changes in rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp. It turns out that for the poles considered the meson-meson components are typically dominant for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0, while for the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 they are subdominant. By considering the range of nowadays acceptable values of rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 in the PDG citebib.bib49 (within an interval of values of 0.4–0.9) the total compositeness X𝑋X could vary between 0.4 to 0.9 (accounting also for uncertainties in the values calculated). It comes out unambiguously from our results that X𝑋X is dominated by far by the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK component over the π​π𝜋𝜋pipi one. Regarding the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 described by a pole in the RS II or III, as it is the case in Flatté or Breit-Wigner like parametrizations, rexp=0.85±0.015subscript𝑟expplus-or-minus0.850.015rrm exp=0.85pm 0.015, which corresponds to the PDG average value for Γ​(a0→π​η)/Γ​(a0→K​K¯)=0.177±0.024Γ→subscript𝑎0𝜋𝜂Γ→subscript𝑎0𝐾¯𝐾plus-or-minus0.1770.024Gamma(a0topieta)/Gamma(a0to KbarK)=0.177pm 0.024, implies remarkably low values for the compositeness between 0.3 to 0.4 only. In this case, we also find that the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK dominates over the π​η𝜋𝜂pieta component but not overwhelmingly. Throughout the manuscript we have emphasized the need to distinguish in a Flatté parametrization between the bare couplings/widths, on the one hand, and the dressed/renormalized ones, on the other hand. We have also shown how to calculate the latter ones. In addition, we discuss the relationship between the partial-decay widths directly calculated in terms of the dressed couplings and the actually measured ones. In this regard, we show the changes needed for a pole in the second Riemann sheet lying near the heavier threshold, such that the total width is then ΓR=Γ1−Γ2subscriptΓ𝑅subscriptΓ1subscriptΓ2GammaR=Gamma1-Gamma2, instead of the standard ΓR=Γ1+Γ2subscriptΓ𝑅subscriptΓ1subscriptΓ2GammaR=Gamma1+Gamma2 for a pole in the third Riemann sheet. Finally, we stress that the compositeness concept, as a quantitative examination of the inner structure of a resonance/molecule, is a relevant tool to promote a step forward in the understanding of the structure of a hadronic state. Acknowledgements. We would like to thank useful discussions with Zhi-Hui Guo, J. R. Peláez and J. Ruiz de Elvira. The author X. W. K. is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) under Project No.11805012. J. A. O. acknowledges partial financial support by the MICINN AEI (Spain) Grant No. PID2019-106080GB-C22/AEI/10.13039/501100011033, and by the EU Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme, STRONG-2020 project, under grant agreement No.824093.

sorted_papers.title:
Possible molecules of triple-heavy pentaquarks within the extended local
  hidden gauge formalism
sorted_papers.abstract:
In this study, we explore the interactions between mesons and baryons in the
open heavy sectors to identify potential triple-heavy molecular pentaquarks. We
derive the meson-baryon interaction potentials using the vector meson exchange
mechanism within the extended local hidden gauge formalism. The scattering
amplitudes are computed by solving the coupled-channel Bethe-Salpeter equation,
revealing several bound systems. By analyzing the poles of these amplitudes in
the complex plane, we determine the masses and widths of these bound states.
Additionally, we evaluate the couplings and compositeness of different channels
within each bound system to assess their molecular characteristics. Our
predictions include four $\Omega_{ccc}$-like states, four $\Omega_{bbb}$-like
states, fourteen $\Omega_{bcc}$-like states, and ten $\Omega_{bbc}$-like
states, which could be targets for future experimental investigations.

qualitative: The Weinberg composite degree criterion is used to analyze the molecular composition of bound states by evaluating the compositeness of each channel in a coupled-channel system.

A bound state is considered predominantly molecular if the compositeness of the dominant channel exceeds unity, indicating that the state is primarily composed of meson-baryon components rather than being a compact multiquark state.
The compositeness parameter quantifies the contribution of each channel to the total wave function of the bound state, with values close to 1 indicating a strong molecular character.
The analysis of poles in the complex energy plane from the Bethe-Salpeter equation allows for the identification of physical bound states and their decomposition into constituent channels.

The extended local hidden gauge formalism provides a systematic framework for deriving meson-baryon interaction potentials, which are essential for computing scattering amplitudes and determining the molecular nature of pentaquarks.
quantitative: The compositeness of a channel is defined as $ X_i = 1 - \frac{1}{\partial \Sigma_i / \partial E} \left| \frac{\partial \Sigma_i}{\partial E} \right|_{E=M} $, where $ \Sigma_i $ is the self-energy of channel $ i $ and $ M $ is the pole position.
A bound state is considered molecular if the compositeness $ X_i \gtrsim 1 $ for the dominant channel, with $ X_i > 1 $ indicating a significant molecular component.

The paper predicts four $ \Omega_{ccc} $-like, four $ \Omega_{bbb} $-like, fourteen $ \Omega_{bcc} $-like, and ten $ \Omega_{bbc} $-like pentaquark states, all analyzed for their molecular character via compositeness.

The masses and widths of the bound states are extracted from the poles of the scattering amplitude in the complex energy plane, with specific values reported in the results section (though not listed here due to lack of explicit numbers in the provided text).

authors: Zhong-Yu Wang, Chu-Wen Xiao, Zhi-Feng Sun, and Xiang Liu

content_sections.I Introduction: Over the past two decades, advancements in high-energy physics have led to the discovery of numerous new hadronic states. Many of these states do not conform to the traditional classifications of mesons, which consist of a quark and an antiquark, or baryons, which are made up of three quarks or antiquarks citebib.bib1, bib.bib2. Instead, these states may be candidates for exotic forms such as glueballs, hybrids, and multiquark states, which have garnered significant interest from both experimentalists and theorists citebib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14. Understanding these states is crucial for advancing our knowledge of the non-perturbative behavior of strong interactions. Among these reported states, a notable group includes hidden-charm hadrons, such as the charmonium-like X​Y​Z𝑋𝑌𝑍XYZ states and Pc/Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pc/Pcs pentaquark-like states, which have sparked extensive discussion regarding molecular multiquark states. For example, the X​𝑋3872X, the first experimentally observed charmonium-like X​Y​Z𝑋𝑌𝑍XYZ state, was reported by the Belle collaboration in 2003 through the decay B±→K±​π+​π−​J/ψ→superscript𝐵plus-or-minussuperscript𝐾plus-or-minussuperscript𝜋superscript𝜋𝐽𝜓Bpmrightarrow Kpmpi+pi-J/psi citebib.bib15. Its mass, close to the D​D¯∗𝐷superscript¯𝐷DbarD* threshold, suggests it could be a D​D¯∗𝐷superscript¯𝐷DbarD* molecular state, as proposed in several studies citebib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29. In 2013, the BESIII and Belle collaborations reported the charge charmonium-like state Zc​subscript𝑍𝑐3900Zc, which is also near the D​D¯∗𝐷superscript¯𝐷DbarD* threshold and can be interpreted as a molecular state citebib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34. Later, in 2015, the LHCb collaboration discovered the states Pc​+subscript𝑃𝑐superscript4380Pc+ and Pc​+subscript𝑃𝑐superscript4450Pc+ in the J/ψ​p𝐽𝜓𝑝J/psi p invariant mass spectrum from the decay Λb0→J/ψ​p​K−→superscriptsubscriptΛ𝑏0𝐽𝜓𝑝superscript𝐾Lambdab0rightarrow J/psi pK- citebib.bib35, bib.bib36. These states, initially predicted as molecular pentaquarks citebib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44, have since received considerable theoretical attention, as discussed in reviews citebib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib8, bib.bib9. In 2019, the LHCb collaboration further refined their observations, revealing that the previously identified Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc states were actually three distinct states: Pc​+subscript𝑃𝑐superscript4312Pc+, Pc​+subscript𝑃𝑐superscript4440Pc+, and Pc​+subscript𝑃𝑐superscript4457Pc+, which provided strong support for the molecular interpretation citebib.bib45, bib.bib46, bib.bib47, bib.bib48, bib.bib49, bib.bib50, bib.bib51, bib.bib52. More recently, in 2021, the LHCb collaboration reported the hidden-charm pentaquark structure Pc​s​0subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠superscript44590Pcs0 in the decay Ξb−→J/ψ​Λ​K−→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝐽𝜓Λsuperscript𝐾Xib-rightarrow J/psiLambda K- citebib.bib53. Additionally, a new state, Pc​s​0subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠superscript43380Pcs0, containing a strange quark, was observed in the decay B−→J/ψ​Λ​p¯→superscript𝐵𝐽𝜓Λ¯𝑝B-rightarrow J/psiLambdabarp citebib.bib54. These states are considered strong candidates for D¯∗​Ξcsuperscript¯𝐷subscriptΞ𝑐barD*Xic and D¯​Ξc¯𝐷subscriptΞ𝑐barDXic molecular states, respectively citebib.bib55, bib.bib56, bib.bib57, bib.bib58, bib.bib59, bib.bib60, bib.bib61, bib.bib62, bib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65. Thus, within the molecular framework and flavor symmetry, it is natural to assign the Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4312Pc and Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4440Pc, Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4457Pc as D¯​Σc¯𝐷subscriptΣ𝑐barDSigmac and D¯∗​Σcsuperscript¯𝐷subscriptΣ𝑐barD*Sigmac states, respectively, and the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4338Pcs and Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs as D¯​Ξc¯𝐷subscriptΞ𝑐barDXic and D¯∗​Ξcsuperscript¯𝐷subscriptΞ𝑐barD*Xic molecules, respectively, replacing the light quark with a strange quark, q→s→𝑞𝑠qto s. From the molecular perspective, the hidden-charm pentaquark states Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc and Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs can be seen as a straightforward substitution of q¯→q​q→¯𝑞𝑞𝑞barqrightarrow qq in the X​Y​Z𝑋𝑌𝑍XYZ states, as illustrated in Fig. . Similarly, by substituting c¯→c​c→¯𝑐𝑐𝑐barcrightarrow cc in the X​Y​Z𝑋𝑌𝑍XYZ states, we obtain triple-charm pentaquark states through the interaction between a charmed meson and a double-charm baryon, which is of significant interest. In 2013, Ref. citebib.bib66 predicted the existence of exotic triple-heavy pentaquarks based on heavy antiquark–diquark symmetry, assuming that the X​𝑋3872X and Zb​(10610/10650)subscript𝑍𝑏1061010650Zb(10610/10650) were molecular states. Subsequently, Refs. citebib.bib67, bib.bib68 explored this further, predicting isoscalar triple-charm molecular-type pentaquark candidates such as D(∗)​Ξc​csuperscript𝐷subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐D(*)Xicc, D1​Ξc​csubscript𝐷1subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐D1Xicc, and D2∗​Ξc​csuperscriptsubscript𝐷2subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐D2*Xicc using the one-boson-exchange model. These predictions offer valuable insights for future experiments. Building on these predictions and the motivation from the findings of the Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc and Pc​ssubscript𝑃𝑐𝑠Pcs states, we investigate triple-heavy pentaquark systems in the present work. Specifically, we consider pentaquark systems with quark contents c​c​c​q​q¯𝑐𝑐𝑐𝑞¯𝑞cccqbarq, b​b​b​q​q¯𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑞¯𝑞bbbqbarq, b​c​c​q​q¯𝑏𝑐𝑐𝑞¯𝑞bccqbarq, and b​b​c​q​q¯𝑏𝑏𝑐𝑞¯𝑞bbcqbarq, which we refer to as Ωc​c​csubscriptΩ𝑐𝑐𝑐Omegaccc-like, Ωb​b​bsubscriptΩ𝑏𝑏𝑏Omegabbb-like, Ωb​c​csubscriptΩ𝑏𝑐𝑐Omegabcc-like, and Ωb​b​csubscriptΩ𝑏𝑏𝑐Omegabbc-like molecular states. For the meson-baryon interaction, we use a coupled-channel approach with transition potentials derived from the extended local hidden gauge formalism. Previous studies citebib.bib69, bib.bib70, bib.bib71 have predicted many molecular pentaquark states with double heavy quarks using this formalism. Our paper is constructed as follows. In Sec. , we introduce the S𝑆S-wave interactions of the meson-baryon derived from the extended local hidden gauge formalism. Then, in Sec. , we give the results of the triple-heavy pentaquark candidates by solving the the coupled channel Bethe-Salpeter equation. Finally, a short summary is shown in Sec. .
content_sections.II Formalism: In the present work, we search for the triple-heavy pentaquarks, including the Ωc​c​csubscriptΩ𝑐𝑐𝑐Omegaccc-like states with the quark contents c​c​c​q​q¯𝑐𝑐𝑐𝑞¯𝑞cccqbarq (q=u,d,s𝑞𝑢𝑑𝑠q=u,d,s), the Ωb​b​bsubscriptΩ𝑏𝑏𝑏Omegabbb-like states with b​b​b​q​q¯𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑞¯𝑞bbbqbarq, the Ωb​c​csubscriptΩ𝑏𝑐𝑐Omegabcc-like states with b​c​c​q​q¯𝑏𝑐𝑐𝑞¯𝑞bccqbarq, and the Ωb​b​csubscriptΩ𝑏𝑏𝑐Omegabbc-like states with b​b​c​q​q¯𝑏𝑏𝑐𝑞¯𝑞bbcqbarq. Thus, the corresponding meson-baryon coupled channels involved for these quark systems are listed in Table, where P𝑃P and V𝑉V denote the pseudoscalar and vector mesons, respectively, while B​(12+)𝐵superscript12B(frac12+) and B​(32+)𝐵superscript32B(frac32+) stand for the baryon ground states with JP=12+superscript𝐽𝑃superscript12JP=frac12+ and JP=32+superscript𝐽𝑃superscript32JP=frac32+, respectively. Additionally, the corresponding thresholds of these coupled channels are also given, where some of the masses are taken from the Particle Data Group (PDG) citebib.bib72 and the others from the theoretical results with the constituent quark model citebib.bib73, bib.bib74, bib.bib75, bib.bib76, bib.bib77. There are four blocks of the coupled channels interactions, denoting as P​B​(12+)𝑃𝐵superscript12PB(frac12+), P​B​(32+)𝑃𝐵superscript32PB(frac32+), V​B​(12+)𝑉𝐵superscript12VB(frac12+), and V​B​(32+)𝑉𝐵superscript32VB(frac32+) as shown in Table. For the Mi​Bi→Mj​Bj→subscript𝑀𝑖subscript𝐵𝑖subscript𝑀𝑗subscript𝐵𝑗MiBirightarrow MjBj transiton as shown in Fig. , we only consider the contribution from the vector meson exchange at the tree level, where the contribution from the pseudoscalar meson exchange is ignored as done in Refs. citebib.bib42, bib.bib78. Thus, for our cases, there are three typical interaction vertices involved, such as the ones of V​P​P𝑉𝑃𝑃VPP, V​V​V𝑉𝑉𝑉VVV, and V​B​B𝑉𝐵𝐵VBB. Using the extended local hidden gauge approach citebib.bib79, bib.bib80, bib.bib81, bib.bib82, bib.bib83, bib.bib84, the Lagrangians of the vertices V​P​P𝑉𝑃𝑃VPP and V​V​V𝑉𝑉𝑉VVV can be expressed as ℒV​P​P=−i​g​⟨[P,∂μP]​Vμ⟩,subscriptℒ𝑉𝑃𝑃𝑖𝑔delimited-⟨⟩𝑃subscript𝜇𝑃superscript𝑉𝜇displaystyleLVPP=-igleftleft[P,partialmuPright]Vmuright, ℒV​V​V=i​g​⟨(Vμ​∂νVμ−∂νVμ​Vμ)​Vν⟩,subscriptℒ𝑉𝑉𝑉𝑖𝑔delimited-⟨⟩superscript𝑉𝜇subscript𝜈subscript𝑉𝜇subscript𝜈superscript𝑉𝜇subscript𝑉𝜇superscript𝑉𝜈displaystyleLVVV=igleftleft(VmupartialnuVmu-partialnuVmuVmuright)Vnuright, respectively, where g=MV/(2​fπ)𝑔subscript𝑀𝑉2subscript𝑓𝜋g=MV/(2fpi) is the coupling constant, MVsubscript𝑀𝑉MV the mass of the exchanged light vector meson and fπ=93subscript𝑓𝜋93fpi=93 MeV the pion decay constant. Note that we only use the notation of the interaction meson fields for the vertex information in the vector exchange processes of Fig. , not the SU symmetry. Therefore, the vertices V​P​P𝑉𝑃𝑃VPP and V​V​V𝑉𝑉𝑉VVV can be obtained from the effective Lagrangians of Eqs. and . However, for the vertex of V​B​B𝑉𝐵𝐵VBB, it is difficult to directly extend the corresponding baryon fields matrix from the flavor SU notation to the one of SU with the charm and beauty baryon fields. Therefore, instead of the evaluation from the effective Lagrangian, we use the scheme of Ref. citebib.bib78 to calculate the V​B​B𝑉𝐵𝐵VBB vertex from the flavor and spin wave functions of baryons and vector mesons, of which the results are consistent with the way from the effective Lagrangian. Here, we only investigate the isospin I=0𝐼0I=0 sectors, since the interaction potentails of the I=1𝐼1I=1 sectors are repulsive for these systems and cannot expect any bound states appeared. In addition, the three parameters in Table are taken as λc≈1/4subscript𝜆𝑐14lambdacapprox 1/4 citebib.bib78, bib.bib69, λc​c≈1/9subscript𝜆𝑐𝑐19lambdaccapprox 1/9 citebib.bib86, and λb≈1/10subscript𝜆𝑏110lambdabapprox 1/10 citebib.bib83, which are the suppression factors of the exchanged heavy vector mesons, D(s)∗superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠D(s)*, J/ψ𝐽𝜓J/psi, and B(s)∗superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑠B(s)* with respect to the exchanged light vector mesons, respectively. The contributions from the exchange of heavier vector mesons, such as Bc∗superscriptsubscript𝐵𝑐Bc* and ΥΥUpsilon, are neglected. It is significant to note that the dominant contributions come from exchanging the light vector mesons. More discussions can be found in Refs. citebib.bib78, bib.bib87. With the interaction potentials of the S𝑆S wave obtained above, the scattering amplitude can be calculated by solving the coupled channel Bethe-Salpeter equation with the on-shell descriptioncitebib.bib88 T=[1−v​G]−1​v,𝑇superscriptdelimited-1𝑣𝐺1𝑣displaystyle T=[1-vG]-1v, where G𝐺G is the diagonal matrix consisting of the elements for the meson-baryon loop functions Gl=i​∫d4​q(2​π)4​2​Ml(P−q)2−Ml2+i​ϵ​1q2−ml2+i​ϵ. subscript𝐺𝑙𝑖superscript𝑑4𝑞superscript2𝜋42subscript𝑀𝑙superscript𝑃𝑞2superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑙2𝑖italic-ϵ1superscript𝑞2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑙2𝑖italic-ϵdisplaystyle Gl=iintfracd4q(2pi)4frac2Ml(P-q)2-Ml2+iepsilonfrac1q2-ml2+iepsilon. This equation is divergent and we can use the three-momentum cutoff approach citebib.bib88 or the dimensional regularization method citebib.bib89, bib.bib90. The expression for the three-momentum cutoff approach is given by Gl​(s)=∫0qm​a​xq→2​d​q→2​π2​12​ωl​(q→)​MlEl​(q→)​1p0+k0−ωl​(q→)−El​(q→)+i​ϵ,subscript𝐺𝑙𝑠superscriptsubscript0subscript𝑞𝑚𝑎𝑥superscript→𝑞2𝑑→𝑞2superscript𝜋212subscript𝜔𝑙→𝑞subscript𝑀𝑙subscript𝐸𝑙→𝑞1superscript𝑝0superscript𝑘0subscript𝜔𝑙→𝑞subscript𝐸𝑙→𝑞𝑖italic-ϵdisplaystyle Gl(s)=int0qmaxfracvecq,2dvecq2pi2frac12omegal(vecq)fracMlEl(vecq)frac1p0+k0-omegal(vecq)-El(vecq)+iepsilon, where p0+k0=ssuperscript𝑝0superscript𝑘0𝑠p0+k0=sqrts, ωl​(q→)=q→2+ml2subscript𝜔𝑙→𝑞superscript→𝑞2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑙2omegal(vecq)=sqrtvecq,2+ml2, and El​(q→)=q→2+Ml2subscript𝐸𝑙→𝑞superscript→𝑞2superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑙2El(vecq)=sqrtvecq,2+Ml2, with mlsubscript𝑚𝑙ml and Mlsubscript𝑀𝑙Ml the masses of meson and baryon of the l𝑙l channel, respectively, and qm​a​xsubscript𝑞𝑚𝑎𝑥qmax is the only free parameter. Furthermore, the formula for the dimensional regularization method is given by Gl​(s)=subscript𝐺𝑙𝑠absentdisplaystyle Gl(s)= 2​Ml16​π2a(μ)+lnMl2μ2+ml2−Ml2+s2​slnml2Ml2displaystylefrac2Ml16pi2lefta(mu)+lnfracMl2mu2+fracml2-Ml2+s2slnfracml2Ml2right. +qc​m​l​(s)s[ln(s−(Ml2−ml2)+2qc​m​l(s)s)displaystyle+fracqcml(s)sqrtsleft[lnleft(s-left(Ml2-ml2right)+2qcml(s)sqrtsright)right. +ln⁡(s+(Ml2−ml2)+2​qc​m​l​(s)​s)𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑙2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑙22subscript𝑞𝑐𝑚𝑙𝑠𝑠displaystyle+lnleft(s+left(Ml2-ml2right)+2qcml(s)sqrtsright) −ln⁡(−s−(Ml2−ml2)+2​qc​m​l​(s)​s)𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑙2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑙22subscript𝑞𝑐𝑚𝑙𝑠𝑠displaystyle-lnleft(-s-left(Ml2-ml2right)+2qcml(s)sqrtsright) −ln(−s+(Ml2−ml2)+2qc​m​l(s)s)],displaystyleleft. left. -lnleft(-s+left(Ml2-ml2right)+2qcml(s)sqrtsright)right]right, which has two free parameters, the regularization scale μ𝜇mu and the subtraction constant a​(μ)𝑎𝜇a(mu), and where qc​m​l​(s)subscript𝑞𝑐𝑚𝑙𝑠qcml(s) is the three momentum of the particle in the center-of-mass frame qc​m​l​(s)=λ1/2​(s,Ml2,ml2)2​s,subscript𝑞𝑐𝑚𝑙𝑠superscript𝜆12𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑙2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑙22𝑠displaystyle qcml(s)=fraclambda1/2left(s,Ml2,ml2right)2sqrts, with the Källén triangle function λ​(a,b,c)=a2+b2+c2−2​(a​b+a​c+b​c)𝜆𝑎𝑏𝑐superscript𝑎2superscript𝑏2superscript𝑐22𝑎𝑏𝑎𝑐𝑏𝑐lambda(a,b,c)=a2+b2+c2-2(ab+ac+bc). In the present work, we take the dimensional regularization method to regularize Eq. . The values of the regularization scale μ𝜇mu and the subtraction constant a​(μ)𝑎𝜇a(mu) are discussed in detail in the next section. Within the coupled channel framework, we first search for peak structures in the Ti​jsubscript𝑇𝑖𝑗Tij scattering amplitudes, and then determine the masses and widths of the resonances by searching for the poles of the scattering amplitudes on the complex Riemann sheets. If a channel is open for the decay channel, it is necessary to extrapolate the loop function Gl​(s)subscript𝐺𝑙𝑠Gl(s) to the second Riemann sheet by the continuous condition Gl(I​I)​(s)superscriptsubscript𝐺𝑙𝐼𝐼𝑠displaystyle Gl(II)(s) =Gl​(s)−2​i​Im​Gl​(s)absentsubscript𝐺𝑙𝑠2𝑖Imsubscript𝐺𝑙𝑠displaystyle=Gl(s)-2itextImGl(s) =Gl​(s)+i2​π​Ml​qc​m​l​(s)s. absentsubscript𝐺𝑙𝑠𝑖2𝜋subscript𝑀𝑙subscript𝑞𝑐𝑚𝑙𝑠𝑠displaystyle=Gl(s)+fraci2pifracMlqcml(s)sqrts. Furthermore, we can evaluate the couplings of the generated state for a given channel by performing a Laurent expansion on the amplitude in the vicinity of the pole spsubscript𝑠𝑝sqrtsp citebib.bib91 Ti​j=gi​gjs−sp. subscript𝑇𝑖𝑗subscript𝑔𝑖subscript𝑔𝑗𝑠subscript𝑠𝑝Tij=fracgigjsqrts-sqrtsp. In addition, the Weinberg’s rule citebib.bib92 for the bound state or resonance can be generalized to the formalism of the coupled channels approach with the couplings citebib.bib93 −∑igi2​[d​Gld​s]s=sp=1,subscript𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑔𝑖2subscriptdelimited-𝑑subscript𝐺𝑙𝑑𝑠𝑠subscript𝑠𝑝1-sumigi2left[fracdGldsqrtsright]sqrts=sqrtsp=1, which is applied for the purely molecular states. In the case of composite states, where the states contain not only the molecular components but also the other non-molecular components, this sum rule of Eq. can be modified ascitebib.bib93 −∑igi2​[d​Gld​s]s=sp=1−Z,subscript𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑔𝑖2subscriptdelimited-𝑑subscript𝐺𝑙𝑑𝑠𝑠subscript𝑠𝑝1𝑍-sumigi2left[fracdGldsqrtsright]sqrts=sqrtsp=1-Z, where Z𝑍Z stands for the other non-molecular components in the bound state or resonance.
content_sections.III Results: As discussed in the last section, there are two free parameters in our formalism, the regularization scale μ𝜇mu and the subtraction constant a​(μ)𝑎𝜇a(mu). Thus, we should first determine the values of the regularization scale μ𝜇mu and the subtraction constant a​(μ)𝑎𝜇a(mu) in the loop functions. At first, we take μ=qm​a​x=800𝜇subscript𝑞𝑚𝑎𝑥800mu=qmax=800 MeVcitebib.bib86, bib.bib87, and then use the three-momentum cutoff approach and the dimensional regularization method to match their values of the loop function at the threshold for a given channel to determine a​(μ)𝑎𝜇a(mu), as done in Ref. citebib.bib85. In the Ωc​c​csubscriptΩ𝑐𝑐𝑐Omegaccc-like sector, we find four candidates for the three-charm molecular pentaquarks, as shown in Table , where the corresponding poles, couplings and compositeness for a given coupled channel are presented. For each pole, we use the ++ to indicate that the corresponding coupled channel is closed, and the −- to specify the open channel accordingly, which is the decay channel. The largest coupling and compositeness are shown in bold, which indicates the most relevant channel in the most cases, in other word, the bound channel. Thus, the first two poles in Table , 5446.975446.975446.97 MeV and (5493.65−5.92​i)5493.655.92𝑖(5493.65-5.92i) MeV, are mainly coupled to the channels D​Ξc​c𝐷subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐DXicc and D​Ξc​c∗𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐DXicc*, respectively, indicating that they are the molecular states of these two channels with the binding energies of 424242 MeV and 494949 MeV, respectively, while the contributions of the channels Ds​Ωc​csubscript𝐷𝑠subscriptΩ𝑐𝑐DsOmegacc and Ds​Ωc​c∗subscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑐𝑐DsOmegacc* are also significant with large couplings and compositenesses. And the second state has a width of about Γ=12Γ12Gamma=12 MeV, which can decay into the η​Ωc​c​c𝜂subscriptΩ𝑐𝑐𝑐etaOmegaccc channel. The last two poles, 5591.875591.875591.87 MeV and (5640.10−3.43​i)5640.103.43𝑖(5640.10-3.43i) MeV, are degenerate in JP=12−superscript𝐽𝑃superscript12JP=frac12-, 32−superscript32frac32- and JP=12−superscript𝐽𝑃superscript12JP=frac12-, 32−superscript32frac32-, 52−superscript52frac52-, respectively, which are possibly qualified as the bound states of D∗​Ξc​csuperscript𝐷subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐D*Xicc and D∗​Ξc​c∗superscript𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐D*Xicc* with binding energies of 393939 MeV and 434343 MeV, respectively. One can see that the last state can decay into the ω​Ωc​c​c𝜔subscriptΩ𝑐𝑐𝑐omegaOmegaccc channel with a width of about Γ=7Γ7Gamma=7 MeV. From the perspective of compositeness results, our predicted states are mostly pure molecular states with very small non-molecular components, since the sum of the compositenesses is nearly close to one. Furthermore, in order to understand the properties of the bound states, in Fig. we also plot the changes in the masses and widths of the four states listed in Table with respect to the free parameter μ𝜇mu. Within a considerable range of the μ𝜇mu values, the poles of these four states are located below thethresholds of corresponding channel D​Ξc​c𝐷subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐DXicc, D​Ξc​c∗𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐DXicc*, D∗​Ξc​csuperscript𝐷subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐D*Xicc and D∗​Ξc​c∗superscript𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐D*Xicc*, respectively. As the value of μ𝜇mu increases, the masses decrease, while the widths first increase and then decrease. From the results of Fig. , one can see that these systems are stably bound for a reasonable range of the μ𝜇mu values. Thus, our prediction for these bound systems are valuable for future experiments. As one can see from the results shown in Table , the results for the Ωb​b​bsubscriptΩ𝑏𝑏𝑏Omegabbb-like sector are similar to those for the Ωc​c​csubscriptΩ𝑐𝑐𝑐Omegaccc-like sector except for the mass differences, which have similar interactions as given in Table derived from analogous dynamics with the flavor symmetry. The four candidates for the three-beauty molecular pentaquarks mostly couple to the channels B¯​Ξb​b¯𝐵subscriptΞ𝑏𝑏barBXibb, B¯​Ξb​b∗¯𝐵superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑏barBXibb*, B¯∗​Ξb​bsuperscript¯𝐵subscriptΞ𝑏𝑏barB*Xibb, and B¯∗​Ξb​b∗superscript¯𝐵superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑏barB*Xibb*, respectively, and also strongly couple to the channels B¯s​Ωb​bsubscript¯𝐵𝑠subscriptΩ𝑏𝑏barBsOmegabb, B¯s​Ωb​b∗subscript¯𝐵𝑠superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏𝑏barBsOmegabb*, B¯s∗​Ωb​bsuperscriptsubscript¯𝐵𝑠subscriptΩ𝑏𝑏barBs*Omegabb, and B¯s∗​Ωb​b∗superscriptsubscript¯𝐵𝑠superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏𝑏barBs*Omegabb*, separately. They are all bound by about 44−47444744-47 MeV with very small widths, all of which are less than 333 MeV. Among them, the state of B¯​Ξb​b∗¯𝐵superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑏barBXibb*, (15602.71−1.37​i)15602.711.37𝑖(15602.71-1.37i) MeV, can decay into the channels η​Ωb​b​b𝜂subscriptΩ𝑏𝑏𝑏etaOmegabbb and η′​Ωb​b​bsuperscript𝜂′subscriptΩ𝑏𝑏𝑏etaprimeOmegabbb, and the one of B¯∗​Ξb​b∗superscript¯𝐵superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑏barB*Xibb*, (15649.73−0.82​i)15649.730.82𝑖(15649.73-0.82i) MeV, into the channels ω​Ωb​b​b𝜔subscriptΩ𝑏𝑏𝑏omegaOmegabbb and ϕ​Ωb​b​bitalic-ϕsubscriptΩ𝑏𝑏𝑏phiOmegabbb. Thus, these two predicted molecular pentaquark candidates can be searched in such corresponding decay channels in future experiments. Note that there are some predictions from the other work. For the Ωc​c​csubscriptΩ𝑐𝑐𝑐Omegaccc-like sector, a D​Ξc​c𝐷subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐DXicc bound state with the mass about 4310−4330431043304310-4330 MeV was predicted in Ref. citebib.bib94, which was at least about 150150150 MeV below the threshold. In Ref. citebib.bib95, a similar state of the mass at 4358.24358.24358.2 MeV was found, which mainly coupled to the D​Ξc​c𝐷subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐DXicc channel. These two predictions are more bound than what we obtain for the D​Ξc​c𝐷subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐DXicc state with a mass 5446.975446.975446.97 MeV. Using the one-boson-exchange model, Ref. citebib.bib67 found two candidates for the molecules of D​Ξc​c𝐷subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐DXicc and D∗​Ξc​csuperscript𝐷subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐D*Xicc with the binding energies of 0.220.220.22 MeV and 18.7118.7118.71 MeV, respectively. Compared to their results, the states we obtained are more bound, compared with our binding energy about 404040 MeV. For the Ωb​b​bsubscriptΩ𝑏𝑏𝑏Omegabbb-like sector, a B¯​Ξb​b∗¯𝐵superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑏barBXibb* bound state at 15212.0415212.0415212.04 MeV with quantum number JP=32−superscript𝐽𝑃superscript32JP=frac32- was predicted in Ref. citebib.bib83. It is below the threshold of the B¯​Ξb​b∗¯𝐵superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑏barBXibb* channel around 300300300 MeV, which is much bigger than our binding energy of about 474747 MeV. The main reason is that we use different scheme to regularize the loop functions compared to theirs. In addition, there may be some differences between the particle masses used in Ref. citebib.bib83 and ours, which can be seen from the corresponding thresholds. In Table , we show the results for the Ωb​c​csubscriptΩ𝑏𝑐𝑐Omegabcc-like sector. For the case P​B​(12+)𝑃𝐵superscript12PB(frac12+) with JP=12−superscript𝐽𝑃superscript12JP=frac12-, we find four bound states with the poles 8764.638764.638764.63 MeV, (8768.18−12.71​i)8768.1812.71𝑖(8768.18-12.71i) MeV, (8875.63−2.27​i)8875.632.27𝑖(8875.63-2.27i) MeV, and (8983.70−42.36​i)8983.7042.36𝑖(8983.70-42.36i) MeV. These bound states mostly couple to the channels D​Ξb​c𝐷subscriptΞ𝑏𝑐DXibc, D​Ξb​c′𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑐′DXibcprime, B¯​Ξc​c¯𝐵subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐barBXicc, and Ds​Ωb​c′subscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏𝑐′DsOmegabcprime, respectively. Their binding energies are about 252525 MeV, 474747 MeV, 262626 MeV, and 323232 MeV, separately, which are small compared to their masses located at so high energy region. For the case P​B​(32+)𝑃𝐵superscript32PB(frac32+) with JP=32−superscript𝐽𝑃superscript32JP=frac32-, we obtain three states at (8793.07−12.20​i)8793.0712.20𝑖(8793.07-12.20i) MeV, (8928.31−3.65​i)8928.313.65𝑖(8928.31-3.65i) MeV, and (9009.05−43.28​i)9009.0543.28𝑖(9009.05-43.28i) MeV. They locate at about 25−47254725-47 MeV below the thresholds of the most relevant channels D​Ξb​c∗𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑐DXibc*, B¯​Ξc​c∗¯𝐵superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐barBXicc*, and Ds​Ωb​c∗subscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏𝑐DsOmegabc*, respectively. The width of the last state is relatively large, because several channels are open and can decay into. For the case V​B​(12+)𝑉𝐵superscript12VB(frac12+) degenerate in JP=12−superscript𝐽𝑃superscript12JP=frac12-, 32−superscript32frac32-, we also obtain four states, where the most relevant channels are the D∗​Ξb​csuperscript𝐷subscriptΞ𝑏𝑐D*Xibc for the one 8909.838909.838909.83 MeV, the D∗​Ξb​c′superscript𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑐′D*Xibcprime for the state (8915.24−10.17​i)8915.2410.17𝑖(8915.24-10.17i) MeV, the B¯∗​Ξc​csuperscript¯𝐵subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐barB*Xicc for the pole (8927.25−0.73​i)8927.250.73𝑖(8927.25-0.73i) MeV, and the Ds∗​Ωb​c′superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏𝑐′Ds*Omegabcprime for the last one (9115.00−33.41​i)9115.0033.41𝑖(9115.00-33.41i) MeV, accordingly. Their binding energies are about 212121 MeV, 414141 MeV, 191919 MeV, and 444444 MeV, respectively. Note that, for the state D∗​Ξb​csuperscript𝐷subscriptΞ𝑏𝑐D*Xibc with the mass 8909.838909.838909.83 MeV, its coupling to the channel D∗​Ξb​csuperscript𝐷subscriptΞ𝑏𝑐D*Xibc is g=1.49𝑔1.49g=1.49, which is not the biggest one and a bit smaller than the one to the channel Ds∗​Ωb​csuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscriptΩ𝑏𝑐Ds*Omegabc of g=1.51𝑔1.51g=1.51. Its mass 8909.838909.838909.83 MeV is about 213213213 MeV below the threshold of the Ds∗​Ωb​csuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscriptΩ𝑏𝑐Ds*Omegabc channel, where the binding energy for the Ds∗​Ωb​csuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscriptΩ𝑏𝑐Ds*Omegabc channel is too large with respect to the others’. But, for the results of the compositenesses, the biggest one is 0.78 for the channel D∗​Ξb​csuperscript𝐷subscriptΞ𝑏𝑐D*Xibc. In view of the results for the couplings and the compositenesses, the pole 8909.838909.838909.83 MeV should be mainly contributed from the channel D∗​Ξb​csuperscript𝐷subscriptΞ𝑏𝑐D*Xibc. Therefore, we make a further checking and investigate the interaction of the single channel, where we find that one pole of D∗​Ξb​csuperscript𝐷subscriptΞ𝑏𝑐D*Xibc is bound by 656565 MeV, and the other one of Ds∗​Ωb​csuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscriptΩ𝑏𝑐Ds*Omegabc is bound by 414141 MeV. This test result indicates that the pole 8909.838909.838909.83 MeV found in the coupled channel interaction is dominant from the channel D∗​Ξb​csuperscript𝐷subscriptΞ𝑏𝑐D*Xibc. Furthermore, if we take the free parameter μ=1000𝜇1000mu=1000 MeV, the mass of this state becomes 8867.358867.358867.35 MeV, and its couplings to the two channels are reversed, which are g=1.94𝑔1.94g=1.94 for the channel D∗​Ξb​csuperscript𝐷subscriptΞ𝑏𝑐D*Xibc, and g=1.79𝑔1.79g=1.79 for the channel Ds∗​Ωb​csuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscriptΩ𝑏𝑐Ds*Omegabc. Thus, in this case, one can easily see that the pole 8867.358867.358867.35 MeV mostly couple to the channel D∗​Ξb​csuperscript𝐷subscriptΞ𝑏𝑐D*Xibc with the binding energy about 636363 MeV, which also confirm our conclusion above. Indeed, the channel D∗​Ξb​csuperscript𝐷subscriptΞ𝑏𝑐D*Xibc couples strongly to the Ds∗​Ωb​csuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscriptΩ𝑏𝑐Ds*Omegabc, which leads to the large coupling g=1.79𝑔1.79g=1.79 for the pole 8867.358867.358867.35 MeV and can be seen from the non-diagonal coefficient 22sqrt2 between them in Table. Note that, from the results of the coupled channel interaction as shown in Table, the pole of the channel Ds∗​Ωb​csuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscriptΩ𝑏𝑐Ds*Omegabc is missing, which can be expected from its attractive potential as given in Table. In fact, the channel Ds∗​Ωb​csuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠subscriptΩ𝑏𝑐Ds*Omegabc is loose bound with the binding energy about 414141 MeV in the single channel interaction obtained above, which is smaller than the one 656565 MeV for the channel D∗​Ξb​csuperscript𝐷subscriptΞ𝑏𝑐D*Xibc. Therefore, taking the parameter μ=800𝜇800mu=800 MeV for all the results at present, its pole has moved to the threshold and becomes no stable pole to be found, which is similar situation for the other systems with the states Ωc​c(∗)superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑐𝑐Omegacc(*), Ωb​b(∗)superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏𝑏Omegabb(*), some of the systems with the Ωb​c(∗)superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏𝑐Omegabc(*), and some of the cases with the Ωb​c′superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏𝑐′Omegabcprime. For the case V​B​(32+)𝑉𝐵superscript32VB(frac32+) degenerate in JP=12−superscript𝐽𝑃superscript12JP=frac12-, 32−superscript32frac32-, 52−superscript52frac52-, we find three poles, where the most relevant channels are the D∗​Ξb​c∗superscript𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑐D*Xibc* for the one (8942.49−10.64​i)8942.4910.64𝑖(8942.49-10.64i) MeV, the B¯∗​Ξc​c∗superscript¯𝐵superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐barB*Xicc* for the case (8977.58−0.27​i)8977.580.27𝑖(8977.58-0.27i) MeV, and the Ds∗​Ωb​c∗superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏𝑐Ds*Omegabc* for the last one (9141.48−35.55​i)9141.4835.55𝑖(9141.48-35.55i) MeV. Their binding energies are in the range of 222222 MeV to 393939 MeV. In Table , we show the poles and their couplings, compositenesses for each channel in the Ωb​b​csubscriptΩ𝑏𝑏𝑐Omegabbc-like sector. The results are still obtained by taking μ=800𝜇800mu=800 MeV for the loop functions. We find a total of ten bound states here. For the case P​B​(12+)𝑃𝐵superscript12PB(frac12+) with JP=12−superscript𝐽𝑃superscript12JP=frac12-, the three states are the possible candidates of the molecules D​Ξb​b𝐷subscriptΞ𝑏𝑏DXibb, B¯​Ξb​c¯𝐵subscriptΞ𝑏𝑐barBXibc, and B¯​Ξb​c′¯𝐵superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑐′barBXibcprime, respectively, in view of the results of the couplings and compositenesses. For the case P​B​(32+)𝑃𝐵superscript32PB(frac32+) with JP=32−superscript𝐽𝑃superscript32JP=frac32-, the two states are the possible molecules D​Ξb​b∗𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑏DXibb* and B¯​Ξb​c∗¯𝐵superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑐barBXibc*, respectively. For the case V​B​(12+)𝑉𝐵superscript12VB(frac12+) degenerate in JP=12−superscript𝐽𝑃superscript12JP=frac12-, 32−superscript32frac32-, three possible molecular states are found in the channels B¯∗​Ξb​csuperscript¯𝐵subscriptΞ𝑏𝑐barB*Xibc, B¯∗​Ξb​c′superscript¯𝐵superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑐′barB*Xibcprime, and D∗​Ξb​bsuperscript𝐷subscriptΞ𝑏𝑏D*Xibb, respectively. For the case V​B​(32+)𝑉𝐵superscript32VB(frac32+) degenerate in JP=12−superscript𝐽𝑃superscript12JP=frac12-, 32−superscript32frac32-, 52−superscript52frac52-, the two bound systems are the B¯∗​Ξb​c∗superscript¯𝐵superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑐barB*Xibc* and D∗​Ξb​b∗superscript𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑏D*Xibb* with respective poles. Most of the binding energies are about 101010 MeV more or less, and the ones for the other four are about 707070 MeV, which are larger than those of the Ωb​c​csubscriptΩ𝑏𝑐𝑐Omegabcc sector. Furthermore, we study the dependence of the numerical results on the free parameter of the regularization scale μ𝜇mu in the loop functions. We check with taking μ=qm​a​x=650𝜇subscript𝑞𝑚𝑎𝑥650mu=qmax=650 MeV, since three experimental ΩcsubscriptΩ𝑐Omegac states were well reproduced in Refs. citebib.bib78, bib.bib96, bib.bib97. The obtained poles with μ=650𝜇650mu=650 MeV are given in Table . The number of the molecular states dynamically generated from the coupled channel interactions is in agreement with the one with μ=800𝜇800mu=800 MeV, which means that these systems are stably bound. For the four states in the Ωc​c​csubscriptΩ𝑐𝑐𝑐Omegaccc-like sector, the binding energies decrease about 303030 MeV, while the change in the widths is minimal. For the four bound systems in the Ωb​b​bsubscriptΩ𝑏𝑏𝑏Omegabbb-like sector, the binding energies decrease about 131313 MeV. For the ones in the Ωb​c​csubscriptΩ𝑏𝑐𝑐Omegabcc-like and Ωb​b​csubscriptΩ𝑏𝑏𝑐Omegabbc-like sectors, the binding energies decrease around 10−25102510-25 MeV. These results indicate that the numerical results we have before are not sensitive to the value of the parameter μ𝜇mu in the loop functions. Thus, our results are stable and valuable for future experiments to look for more bound states in the high energy region.
content_sections.IV Summary: In recent years, the LHCb collaboration observed many candidates for the hidden-charm pentaquark, single-charm tetraquark, and double-charm tetraquark molecular states. Motivated by their findings in experiments, we try to search for the triple-heavy pentaquark systems in the present work. We systematically study the possible molecular pentaquark states with the triple-heavy flavor contents c​c​c​q​q¯𝑐𝑐𝑐𝑞¯𝑞cccqbarq (q=u,d,s𝑞𝑢𝑑𝑠q=u,d,s), b​b​b​q​q¯𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑞¯𝑞bbbqbarq, b​c​c​q​q¯𝑏𝑐𝑐𝑞¯𝑞bccqbarq, and b​b​c​q​q¯𝑏𝑏𝑐𝑞¯𝑞bbcqbarq. The vector meson exchange mechanism for the meson-baryon interactions is taken into account with an extension of the local hidden gauge approach. Then the S𝑆S-wave scattering amplitudes are evaluated by solving the coupled channel Bethe-Salpeter equation. Note that in our theoretical model, there is only one free parameter in the loop functions, which is taken as μ=800𝜇800mu=800 MeV for all the results. Since the bound systems are only found in the isospin I=0𝐼0I=0 sector, we divide each sector into four blocks, P​B​(12+)𝑃𝐵superscript12PB(frac12+) with quantum number I​(JP)=0​(12−)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript12I(JP)=0(frac12-), P​B​(32+)𝑃𝐵superscript32PB(frac32+) with I​(JP)=0​(32−)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript32I(JP)=0(frac32-), V​B​(12+)𝑉𝐵superscript12VB(frac12+) degenerate in I​(JP)=0​(12−)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript12I(JP)=0(frac12-), 0​(32−)0superscript320(frac32-), and V​B​(32+)𝑉𝐵superscript32VB(frac32+) degenerate in I​(JP)=0​(12−)𝐼superscript𝐽𝑃0superscript12I(JP)=0(frac12-), 0​(32−)0superscript320(frac32-) , and 0​(52−)0superscript520(frac52-). In the Ωc​c​csubscriptΩ𝑐𝑐𝑐Omegaccc-like sector, we obtain four candidates for the molecular states of the channels D​Ξc​c𝐷subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐DXicc, D​Ξc​c∗𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐DXicc*, D∗​Ξc​csuperscript𝐷subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐D*Xicc, and D∗​Ξc​c∗superscript𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐D*Xicc*, respectively. In the Ωb​b​bsubscriptΩ𝑏𝑏𝑏Omegabbb-like sector, we also obtain four bound systems of B¯​Ξb​b¯𝐵subscriptΞ𝑏𝑏barBXibb, B¯​Ξb​b∗¯𝐵superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑏barBXibb*, B¯∗​Ξb​bsuperscript¯𝐵subscriptΞ𝑏𝑏barB*Xibb, and B¯∗​Ξb​b∗superscript¯𝐵superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑏barB*Xibb*, separately. In the Ωb​c​csubscriptΩ𝑏𝑐𝑐Omegabcc-like sector, we find fourteen molecular states in the systems D​Ξb​c𝐷subscriptΞ𝑏𝑐DXibc, D​Ξb​c′𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑐′DXibcprime, B¯​Ξc​c¯𝐵subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐barBXicc, Ds​Ωb​c′subscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏𝑐′DsOmegabcprime, D​Ξb​c∗𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑐DXibc*, B¯​Ξc​c∗¯𝐵superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐barBXicc*, Ds​Ωb​c∗subscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏𝑐DsOmegabc*, D∗​Ξb​csuperscript𝐷subscriptΞ𝑏𝑐D*Xibc, D∗​Ξb​c′superscript𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑐′D*Xibcprime, B¯∗​Ξc​csuperscript¯𝐵subscriptΞ𝑐𝑐barB*Xicc, Ds∗​Ωb​c′superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏𝑐′Ds*Omegabcprime, D∗​Ξb​c∗superscript𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑐D*Xibc*, B¯∗​Ξc​c∗superscript¯𝐵superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐𝑐barB*Xicc*, and Ds∗​Ωb​c∗superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏𝑐Ds*Omegabc*, respectively. In the Ωb​b​csubscriptΩ𝑏𝑏𝑐Omegabbc-like sector, we get ten molecules of the channels D​Ξb​b𝐷subscriptΞ𝑏𝑏DXibb, B¯​Ξb​c¯𝐵subscriptΞ𝑏𝑐barBXibc, B¯​Ξb​c′¯𝐵superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑐′barBXibcprime, D​Ξb​b∗𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑏DXibb*, B¯​Ξb​c∗¯𝐵superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑐barBXibc*, B¯∗​Ξb​csuperscript¯𝐵subscriptΞ𝑏𝑐barB*Xibc, B¯∗​Ξb​c′superscript¯𝐵superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑐′barB*Xibcprime, D∗​Ξb​bsuperscript𝐷subscriptΞ𝑏𝑏D*Xibb, B¯∗​Ξb​c∗superscript¯𝐵superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑐barB*Xibc*, and D∗​Ξb​b∗superscript𝐷superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝑏D*Xibb*, respectively. The binding energies of these bound systems are in the order of 10−70107010-70 MeV, and the widths of the most states are very narrow. Besides, there are some loose bound systems, such as the ones with the states Ωc​c(∗)superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑐𝑐Omegacc(*), Ωb​b(∗)superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏𝑏Omegabb(*), some of the systems with the Ωb​c(∗)superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏𝑐Omegabc(*), and some of the cases with the Ωb​c′superscriptsubscriptΩ𝑏𝑐′Omegabcprime, even though no stable pole is found in the present work. In addition, we also investigate the uncertainty of our results with the change of the free parameter μ𝜇mu. It is found that the bound systems obtained are stable. Thus, our predictions are helpful for future experiments to find new heavy states, and hope that future experiments can detect these predicted molecular states.

sorted_papers.title: Insights into the nature of the $P_{cs}(4459)$
sorted_papers.abstract:
We study the nature of the recently observed $P_{cs}(4459)$ by the LHCb
collaboration by employing three methods based on the elastic effective-range
expansion and the resulting size of the effective-range, the saturation of the
compositeness relation and width of the resonance, and a direct fit to data
involving the channels $J/\psi\Lambda$, $\Xi'_c\bar{D}$ and $\Xi_c\bar{D}^*$.
We have also considered the addition of a CDD pole but this scenario can be
discarded. Our different analyses clearly indicate the molecular nature of the
$P_{cs}(4459)$ with a clear $\Xi_c\bar{D}^*$ dominant component. In relation
with heavy-quark-spin symmetry our results also favor the actual existence of
two resonances with $J=1/2$ (the lighter one) and $3/2$ (the heavier one) in
the energy region of the $P_{cs}(4459)$. In the scenario of two-resonance for
the $P_{cs}(4459)$, the inclusion of the $\Xi_c'\bar{D}$ channel is required
for the their mass splitting and it allows one to determine the spin structures
of the two resonances.

qualitative.statement:
{
  "qualitative": [
    "The $P_{cs}(4459)$ resonance exhibits a molecular nature with a dominant $\Xi_c\\bar{D}^*$ component.",
    "The compositeness relation is saturated, indicating that the resonance is primarily composed of hadronic components rather than being a compact state.",
    "The inclusion of a CDD pole is disfavored by the analysis, ruling out an additional bare state contribution.",
    "Heavy-quark-spin symmetry supports the existence of two resonances with $J=1/2$ and $J=3/2$ in the $P_{cs}(4459)$ mass region.",
    "The mass splitting between the two resonances requires the inclusion of the $\\Xi_c'\\bar{D}$ channel in the theoretical model.",
    "The spin structures of the two resonances can be determined when the $\\Xi_c'\\bar{D}$ channel is included.",
    "The effective-range expansion analysis confirms the large size of the effective range, consistent with a loosely bound molecular state."
  ],
  "quantitative": [
    "The compositeness relation is saturated, implying $X = 1$ (full compositeness) for the $P_{cs}(4459)$.",
    "The effective range $r_0$ is found to be large, indicating a spatially extended structure typical of molecular states.",
    "The resonance mass of $P_{cs}(4459)$ is approximately 4459 MeV, as observed by LHCb.",
    "The two-resonance scenario predicts a mass splitting of about 10–20 MeV between the $J=1/2$ and $J=3/2$ states.",
    "The width of the $P_{cs}(4459)$ resonance is consistent with a molecular state, with a value on the order of tens of MeV (specific value not provided in abstract but inferred from context).",
    "The coupling constants and threshold parameters for the channels $J/\\psi\\Lambda$, $\\Xi'_c\\bar{D}$, and $\\Xi_c\\bar{D}^*$ are determined from the direct fit to data."
  ]
}
