authors: Meng-Lin Du, Zhi-Hui Guo and J. A. Oller

content_sections.1 Introduction: The study of exotic hadrons beyond the conventional quark-antiquark (q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq) configurations for mesons and the three-quark (3​q3𝑞3q) ones for baryons has been an interesting topic in strong interactions since long time ago, e. g. one could mention the discussions on the lightest scalar resonances f0​subscript𝑓0500f0, f0​subscript𝑓0980f0, a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 and κ​𝜅900kappa citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, the case of the Λ​Λ1405Lambda resonances citebib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, the Roper citebib.bib9, etc. The interest on this topic has received a great boost since the discoveries in 2003 of the Ds​0∗​superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠02317Ds0* citebib.bib10 and the χc​1​subscript𝜒𝑐13872chic1 citebib.bib11, aka X​𝑋3872X, whose properties cannot be accommodated within the conventional quark model expectations. As a result a new era has developed in hadron spectroscopy based on the detection of numerous multiquark states with heavy quarks, particularly the charm quark, and more recently the bottom quark as well. An important outcome in this fruitful field of research was the discovery by the LHCb in 2015 of the charmonium pentaquarks Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4450Pc and Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4380Pc decaying into J/ψ​p𝐽𝜓𝑝J/psi p citebib.bib12. With higher statistics in 2019 citebib.bib13 the same collaboration in the same decay process determined the existence of the pentaquarks Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4457Pc, Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4440Pc (instead of just one resonance Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4450Pc) and the lighter and narrow Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4312Pc. In Refs. citebib.bib14, bib.bib15 a through analysis of the experimental data employing coupled-channel dynamics, heavy-quark-spin symmetry (HQSS) and one-pion exchange provided a clear hint for the existence of a narrow Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4380Pc. Very recently the LHCb collaboration citebib.bib16 has reported the first evidence of a charmonium pentaquark resonance with strangeness, the so called Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs appearing as a peak in the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda event distribution from an amplitude analysis of the Ξb−→J/ψ​Λ​K−→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝐽𝜓Λsuperscript𝐾Xib-to J/psiLambda K- decay. Ref. citebib.bib16 also concludes that this structure is consistent with two resonances. Indeed strange pentaquark resonances were already predicted theoretically in several references citebib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, being also suggested to look for them in the decay process Ξb−→J/ψ​Λ​K−→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝐽𝜓Λsuperscript𝐾Xib-to J/psiLambda K- by the Refs. citebib.bib25, bib.bib19. The discovery of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs by the LHCb collaboration has prompted numerous studies of this resonance citebib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31. These studies typically favor a molecular-type picture with a dominant component of the channel Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, whose threshold lies only about 19MeV above the nominal mass of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. However, there is no such a general consensus regarding the spin of the resonance between the two values J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 and 3/2323/2 and, indeed, some studies point out towards the existence of two resonances around 4.45GeV in the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda event distributions. In this work we intent to scrutinize the nature of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs without ascribing to a specific dynamical model, by making use of general arguments based on unitarity and analyticity in S𝑆S-matrix theory. We exploit three general methods, recently applied in a similar way by two of us in the study of the X​𝑋6900X citebib.bib32, all of which come to the conclusion of the molecular nature of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs with a clearly dominant Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* component. The first method is based on the fact that a CDD pole citebib.bib33 in the near-threshold region gives rise to a strong impact citebib.bib34 in the values of the scattering length a𝑎a and effective range r𝑟r. Particularly relevant is to focus on r𝑟r because a priori its absolute value is given by the typical range of strong interactions around 1fm citebib.bib35, while the presence of a nearby CDD pole would typically imply much larger values citebib.bib34. This signature could then be taken as favoring the presence in the state of interest of a large bare component, or of large components from other channels, to which the CDD pole would be associated citebib.bib36. However, we find here that r𝑟r has a natural value around −11-1fm by applying the elastic effective-range-expansion (ERE) study to Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, suggesting the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* molecular nature of this resonance. We also observe that when this method is applied to the Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD channel (whose threshold is around 13MeV below the resonance mass) the resulting r𝑟r has instead a much larger modulus indicating that the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs is not dynamically generated by this channel. The second method is a coupled-channel generalization that relies on the saturation of the width ΓΓGamma and compositeness X𝑋X of the resonance in terms of the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*(Ξc′D¯)XicprimebarD) channels. The latter concept refers to the weight of the baryon-meson components in the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. For its calculation we follow Ref. citebib.bib37 that provides a probabilistic interpretation for the compositeness of a resonance. The optimal way to calculate the compositeness is still under debate citebib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib37, bib.bib41, bib.bib41. By following this method we have at our disposal two equations, though the outcome depends on the value assumed for the total compositeness X𝑋X, while ΓΓGamma is taken from the LHCb collaboration Ref. citebib.bib16. Nonetheless, given a value of X𝑋X the method provides us with the partial decay widths and the partial compositeness coefficients for each channel. The third method allows one to also include coupled channels and avoid having to take for granted a value for X𝑋X. We proceed with a direct fit to the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda event distribution around the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs resonance energy region explicitly taking into account the three coupled channels J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda, Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, together with constraints from HQSS. The lightest channel accounts in an effective way of those channels whose thresholds are relatively far from the resonance mass, in particular this is the case for the ηc​Λsubscript𝜂𝑐ΛetacLambda which is degenerate to the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda in the heavy-quark limit. Our analysis also favors the presence of two resonances (with J=12𝐽12J=frac12 and J=32𝐽32J=frac32 respectively) in the peak of the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda event distribution associated to the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. Since the S𝑆S-wave Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD only couples to Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* in J=12𝐽12J=frac12, it could affect the dynamics of this channel and leave significant impact on the line shape. As a result, it leads to the mass split of the two resonances and allows one to determine their spins. We stress that the HQSS breaking of the potential, which is of order 𝒪​(ΛQCDmc)𝒪subscriptΛQCDsubscript𝑚𝑐O(fracLambdatextQCDmc), cannot account for the mass splitting of the two resonances, cf. Sec. . In other words, the inclusion of the Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD channel provides an extra mechanism which is responsible for the mass splitting of the two resonances while keeping the HQSS constraints on the interacting potential. In addition, we have also allowed the presence of a CDD pole in the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* or Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD channel but no acceptable fits result. We find that the methods 1 and 3 agree in the basic fact that the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs is mainly a Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* molecular resonance, while the methods 2 and 3 provide also very compatible values for the couplings of the resonance to the different channels when the same value for X𝑋X is used in the second method. The contents of the manuscript are structured as follows. After this introduction we describe and apply the methods 1, 2 and 3 to the study the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs in the Secs. , and , respectively. In the latter section we also compare between several types of fits with different number of channels and partial waves involved, as well as with different degree of imposition of the HQSS constraints. The conclusions are gathered in Sec. .
content_sections.2 Effective range expansion for the elas: First we rely on the ERE method to perform an exploratory study of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs near the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* and Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD thresholds. The elastic S𝑆S-wave ERE formula reads T​(E)=1−1a+12​r​k2−i​k,𝑇𝐸11𝑎12𝑟superscript𝑘2𝑖𝑘displaystyle T(E)=frac1-frac1a+frac12r,k2-i,k,, where a𝑎a and r𝑟r are the scattering length and the effective range in order, and the non-relativistic relation between the three-momentum k𝑘k and the energy E𝐸E in the center of mass (CM) frame is k=2​μm​(E−mth),𝑘2subscript𝜇𝑚𝐸subscript𝑚thdisplaystyle k=sqrt2mum(E-mrm th),, being μm=m1​m2/(m1+m2)subscript𝜇𝑚subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2mum=m1m2/(m1+m2) the reduced mass and mth=m1+m2subscript𝑚thsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2mrm th=m1+m2 the threshold of the two states in the scattering. Above the threshold, the unitarity relation Im​T​(E)−1=−k,(E>mth),Im𝑇superscript𝐸1𝑘𝐸subscript𝑚thrm Im,T(E)-1=-k,,quad(E>mrm th),, is automatically satisfied by the amplitude in Eq. . There are two caveats when applying the ERE formalism. First the left hand cuts originated from the particle exchanges in the crossed channels are neglected. This assumption is consistent with the pionless effective filed theory description, which turns out to be able to explain the various pentaquark candidatescitebib.bib42, bib.bib43. Nevertheless, further studies on the inclusion of the light-meson exchanges reveal possibly noticeable effects on the assignments of the spin for the different pentaquark statescitebib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib15, bib.bib14. The other caveat is about the possible underlying CDD pole near the threshold. It is rather challenging to infer the existence of the near-threshold CDD pole a priori. In Refs. citebib.bib46, bib.bib34, a practicable criteria is provided to discern the appearance of a near-threshold CDD pole based on the large magnitude of the effective range r𝑟r. This is because the effective range is found to scale as r∝1(MCDD−mth)2,proportional-to𝑟1superscriptsubscript𝑀CDDsubscript𝑚th2displaystyle rproptofrac1(MtextCDD-mrm th)2,, when the CDD position in energy, MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD, approaches to the threshold. To be more specific, if the resulting magnitude of r𝑟r in the ERE study is large, as compared to the typical range around 1fm of strong interactions, it would be a clear hint for the existence of a CDD pole near threshold, indicating as well that the ERE expansion is not suitable to handle such systems. On the contrary, if the resulting |r|𝑟|r| has a value less than 1∼2similar-to121sim 2fm, a near-threshold CDD pole is not necessary and the ERE would not be spoiled by its presence, and it could be used to describe such scattering processes.4Note that a large value of |r|𝑟|r| can also be caused by a near coupled-channel effect. However, this effect could also be related to the presence of a CDD pole in the elastic scattering of the open channel. Regarding the previous discussion, by identifying the observed pentaquark candidate Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs as the resonance pole in the elastic scattering amplitude of Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* or Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD, one can then fix in every case the corresponding scattering length and effective range via the mass and width of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. The size obtained for the effective range would be an indication of whether the resonance can be considered as “molecular” in terms of the channel considered or it better corresponds to a bare state (preexisting resonance). The resonance pole lies in the second Riemann sheet (RS) in the elastic scattering case and the amplitude TI​I​(E)subscript𝑇𝐼𝐼𝐸TII(E) in this RS has the form TI​I​(E)=1−1a+12​r​k2+i​k. subscript𝑇𝐼𝐼𝐸11𝑎12𝑟superscript𝑘2𝑖𝑘displaystyle TII(E)=frac1-frac1a+frac12r,k2,+i,k,. In this convention, the imaginary part of k𝑘k is understood to be positively taken, namely Im​k>0Im𝑘0rm Imk>0, in Eqs. and . The solution of TI​I​(ER)−1=0subscript𝑇𝐼𝐼superscriptsubscript𝐸𝑅10TII(ER)-1=0 gives the resonance pole position ER=MR−i​ΓR/2subscript𝐸𝑅subscript𝑀𝑅𝑖subscriptΓ𝑅2ER=MR-iGammaR/2, being MRsubscript𝑀𝑅MR and ΓRsubscriptΓ𝑅GammaR the resonance mass and width, respectively. Alternatively, one could also determine a𝑎a and r𝑟r in terms of the resonance pole position ERsubscript𝐸𝑅ER and their explicit relations turn out to becitebib.bib46 a=−2​ki|kr|2+|ki|2,r=−1ki,formulae-sequence𝑎2subscript𝑘𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑘𝑟2superscriptsubscript𝑘𝑖2𝑟1subscript𝑘𝑖displaystyle a=-frac2ki|kr|2+|ki|2,,quad r=-frac1ki,, where krsubscript𝑘𝑟kr and kisubscript𝑘𝑖ki stand for the real and imaginary parts of the three-momentum kRsubscript𝑘𝑅kR evaluated at the pole, namely, kR=2​μm​(ER−mth)subscript𝑘𝑅2subscript𝜇𝑚subscript𝐸𝑅subscript𝑚thkR=sqrt2mum(ER-mrm th). For clarity, let us point out again that the convention ki>0subscript𝑘𝑖0ki>0 is taken. The ERE scattering amplitude is completely fixed after a𝑎a and r𝑟r are known, and with it an important quantity, i. e. the residue, which can then be inferred by taking the Laurent expansion of TI​I​(E​(k))subscript𝑇𝐼𝐼𝐸𝑘TII(E(k)) around the pole position TI​I​(k)=−ki/krk−kR+𝒪​[(k−kR)0]. subscript𝑇𝐼𝐼𝑘subscript𝑘𝑖subscript𝑘𝑟𝑘subscript𝑘𝑅𝒪delimited-superscript𝑘subscript𝑘𝑅0displaystyle TII(k)=frac-ki/krk-kR+cal O[(k-kR)0],. In our previous studiescitebib.bib37, bib.bib34, this residue −ki/krsubscript𝑘𝑖subscript𝑘𝑟-ki/kr has been demonstrated to be equal to the compositeness coefficient X𝑋X citebib.bib37, which corresponds to the probability of the two-body component in the resonance, i. e. , X=−kikr. 𝑋subscript𝑘𝑖subscript𝑘𝑟displaystyle X=-frackikr,. As proven in Ref. citebib.bib34 when MR−mth>0subscript𝑀𝑅subscript𝑚th0MR-mrm th>0 it follows that the compositeness X=−ki/kr𝑋subscript𝑘𝑖subscript𝑘𝑟X=-ki/kr lies within the range [0,1]01[0,1], a result that is also consistent with the working condition of the probabilistic interpretation revealed in Ref. citebib.bib37. It should be stressed that the expression of the compositeness coefficient X𝑋X in Eq. is only valid in the context of the single-channel ERE when there is a resonance pole in the amplitude. General discussions on the compositeness relations for resonances will be given in the next section. The mass and width of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs from the single-resonance fit of the LHCb collaboration citebib.bib16 read MR=4458.8±2.9−1.1+4.7​MeV,ΓR=17.3±6.5−5.7+8.0​MeV,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑀𝑅plus-or-minus4458.8subscriptsuperscript2.94.71.1MeVsubscriptΓ𝑅plus-or-minus17.3subscriptsuperscript6.58.05.7MeVdisplaystyle MR=4458.8pm 2.9+4.7-1.1textMeV,,qquadGammaR=17.3pm 6.5+8.0-5.7textMeV,, where the first errors are statistics and the second ones are systematic. We will take a conservative estimation and combine the two types of uncertainties by adding in quadrature the statistic error and the largest of the two systematic errors. The explicit values of the mass and width used in our analyses are given in the second and third columns of Table, respectively, together with the thresholds of Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* in the next column. As a tentative study, let us now focus on the two elastic scattering cases to address the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs, i. e. those stemming from taking separately the Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, whose thresholds lie near the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs peak observed by the LHCb collaborationcitebib.bib16. Therefore, we separately consider in the ERE analysis that the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs corresponds to the poles in the elastic Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* scattering processes. The coupled-channel studies will be postponed until the next section. The determination of the scattering length and effective range for each case is summarized in the columns fifth and sixth of Table. In the elastic Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD scattering, the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs pole meets the working condition raised in Ref. citebib.bib37, i. e. , its mass is above the Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD threshold, so that the prescription in Eq. is eligible for the probabilistic interpretation. The small value of the compositeness X𝑋X of the Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD component, shown in the last column, indicates that other components than the Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD, play more important roles in the constitution of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. The small value of X𝑋X, whose central value is 31%percent3131%, is also consistent with the rather large magnitude of r𝑟r, as the latter hints towards the appearance (within this simplistic elastic picture of Ξc′​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD scattering) of an underlying CDD pole near threshold, see Eq. . For the case of the elastic Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* scattering, the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs pole does not meet the requirement of the working condition in Ref. citebib.bib37, since the mass of the pole is below threshold. As a result, the prescription of Eq. loses the probabilistic interpretation in the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* case. Nevertheless, the magnitudes of r𝑟r and a𝑎a all correspond to the conventional strong interactions, which hint the natural explanation of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs as a molecular of the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* system. This conclusion is further corroborated when discussing the coupled-channel case. Note that the total width of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs is assumed to be saturated by only one channel, either the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* or Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD, in the elastic ERE framework. This assumption should be revisited since the partial decay width to the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda channel is likely non-negligible, which is indeed the channel that is used to measure the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs in experimentcitebib.bib16. As a result, a more realistic study would require the information of the partial decay width to Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* and Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD, which will be investigated in the next section within a coupled-channel analysis. Later on various coupled-channel fits to the experimental event distributions of J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda will be also considered.
content_sections.3 Coupled-channel study of J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/\\p: There are intensive discussions in the literature to extend the Weinberg’s compositeness relation for the bound states citebib.bib47, bib.bib48 to the resonance casescitebib.bib49, bib.bib50, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib37, bib.bib51, bib.bib41. The key obstacle in the latter situation is that the compositeness coefficient X𝑋X usually becomes complex citebib.bib52. Different proposals have been given in Refs. citebib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib37, bib.bib41 to cure this problem. In Ref. citebib.bib37 the Weinberg’s compositeness relation for bound sates is generalized to resonances by performing a phase transformation of the S𝑆S matrix, so that the compositeness coefficient X𝑋X corresponding to the resonance state turns to be a positive number between 0 and 1, which can be interpreted as the probability to find the two-particle component in a resonance. It is demonstrated in Ref. citebib.bib37 that the compositeness coefficient Xjsubscript𝑋𝑗Xj of a specific channel j𝑗j reduces to taking the absolute value of the product of the complex residue/coupling squared gj2superscriptsubscript𝑔𝑗2gj2 and the derivative of the two-point Green function at the resonance pole position sR=(MR−i​ΓR/2)2subscript𝑠𝑅superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑅𝑖subscriptΓ𝑅22sR=(MR-iGammaR/2)2citebib.bib37, i. e. Xjsubscript𝑋𝑗displaystyle Xj =displaystyle= |gj|2​|d​Gj​(sR)d​s|,superscriptsubscript𝑔𝑗2𝑑subscript𝐺𝑗subscript𝑠𝑅𝑑𝑠displaystyle|gj|2left|fracdGj(sR)dsright|,, where Gj​(s)subscript𝐺𝑗𝑠Gj(s) is the one-loop two-point Green function of the j𝑗jth channel. The expression given in Eq. represents Gj​(s)subscript𝐺𝑗𝑠Gj(s) in the first or physical RS, and its corresponding formula in the unphysical RS reads citebib.bib4 Gj​(s)II=Gj​(s)−i​qj​(s)4​π​s,subscript𝐺𝑗superscript𝑠IIsubscript𝐺𝑗𝑠𝑖subscript𝑞𝑗𝑠4𝜋𝑠displaystyle Gj(s)rm II=Gj(s)-ifracqj(s)4pisqrts,, where qj​(s)subscript𝑞𝑗𝑠qj(s) denotes the relativistic CM three-momentum of the j𝑗jth channel qj​(s)=[s−(m1+m2)2]​[s−(m1−m2)2]2​s,subscript𝑞𝑗𝑠delimited-𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚22delimited-𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚222𝑠displaystyle qj(s)=fracsqrt[s-(m1+m2)2][s-(m1-m2)2]2sqrts,, with m1subscript𝑚1m1 and m2subscript𝑚2m2 the masses of the two particles in that channel. The imaginary part of Gj​(s)IIsubscript𝐺𝑗superscript𝑠IIGj(s)rm II is opposite with the one of Gj​(s)subscript𝐺𝑗𝑠Gj(s) along the real s𝑠s axis above the threshold. The explicit μ𝜇mu dependences in the first two terms in Eq. cancel each other, which implies that the Gj​(s)subscript𝐺𝑗𝑠Gj(s) function is actually independent on the scale μ𝜇mu. For the practical purpose, we will set μ=770𝜇770mu=770MeV throughout. The natural value of the subtraction constant a​(μ)𝑎𝜇a(mu) can be estimated by matching the functions Gj​(s)subscript𝐺𝑗𝑠Gj(s) calculated in dimensional regularization and with a three-momentum cut-off qmaxsubscript𝑞maxqtextmax at threshold, as explained in Refs. citebib.bib7, bib.bib53, which leads to a=−2m1+m2​[m1​log⁡(1+1+m12qmax2)+m2​log⁡(1+1+m22qmax2)]≃−2.5,𝑎2subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2delimited-subscript𝑚111superscriptsubscript𝑚12superscriptsubscript𝑞max2subscript𝑚211superscriptsubscript𝑚22superscriptsubscript𝑞max2similar-to-or-equals2.5displaystyle a=-frac2m1+m2left[m1logleft(1+sqrt1+fracm12qrm max2right)+m2logleft(1+sqrt1+fracm22qrm max2right)right]simeq-2.5,, by taking qmax=1.0subscript𝑞max1.0qrm max=1.0GeV, m1=mΞcsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚subscriptΞ𝑐m1=mXic and m2=mD∗subscript𝑚2subscript𝑚superscript𝐷m2=mD*. We will take a universal value for the subtraction constants in the two channels (since the variation in a𝑎a from Eq. by taking the masses of the particles in the first channel is small) and fix it to the one given in Eq. . It is worth noticing that the derivatives of the Gj​(s)subscript𝐺𝑗𝑠Gj(s) and Gj​(s)IIsubscript𝐺𝑗superscript𝑠IIGj(s)rm II functions appearing in the partial compositeness coefficient in Eq. do not depend on the subtraction constant a​(μ)𝑎𝜇a(mu) nor on μ𝜇mu. We introduce the subscript j=1𝑗1j=1 and 2 to distinguish between the channels J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξc​D∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript𝐷XicD*, respectively. The saturation of the compositeness relation can be written as X=X1+X2=|g1|2​|d​G1II​(sR)d​s|+|g2|2​|d​G2​(sR)d​s|,𝑋subscript𝑋1subscript𝑋2superscriptsubscript𝑔12𝑑superscriptsubscript𝐺1IIsubscript𝑠𝑅𝑑𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑔22𝑑subscript𝐺2subscript𝑠𝑅𝑑𝑠X=X1+X2=|g1|2left|fracdG1rm II(sR)dsright|+|g2|2left|fracdG2(sR)dsright|,, where X𝑋X in the left-hand side is the total compositeness coefficient contributed by the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* channels. Regarding the saturation of the decay width of Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs, we also assume that it is exclusively given by these two channels. For the lighter case with J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda, since its threshold is clearly below the resonance mass, one can safely use the standard two-body decay width formulacitebib.bib54 Γ1=|g1|2​q1​(MR2)8​π​MR2. subscriptΓ1superscriptsubscript𝑔12subscript𝑞1superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑅28𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑅2displaystyleGamma1=|g1|2fracq1(MR2)8pi MR2,. While for the heavier channel Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, its threshold is slightly above the resonance mass and the standard decay width formula can not be applied. One way to estimate its partial decay width is to introduce the spectral distribution of the resonance taking into account its finite-width citebib.bib4, bib.bib55, bib.bib34 Γ2=|g2|2​∫mth,2MR+n​ΓR𝑑w​q2​(w2)16​π2​w2​ΓR(MR−w)2+ΓR2/4. subscriptΓ2superscriptsubscript𝑔22superscriptsubscriptsubscript𝑚th2subscript𝑀𝑅𝑛subscriptΓ𝑅differential-d𝑤subscript𝑞2superscript𝑤216superscript𝜋2superscript𝑤2subscriptΓ𝑅superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑅𝑤2superscriptsubscriptΓ𝑅24displaystyleGamma2=|g2|2intmrm th,2MR+nGammaRdw,fracq2(w2)16pi2,w2fracGammaR(MR-w)2+GammaR2/4,. Due to the long tail of the Lorentzian spectral distribution in the integrand of the above equation, the upper integration limit should be taken with care to guarantee the coverage of the resonance peak in Eq. . For the partial decay width of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs into the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* channel, the distance of its threshold to the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs mass is slightly larger than the somewhat narrow width of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. Therefore, somewhat large values of n𝑛n in the upper integration limit of Eq. turn out to be required. For example, it is found that when taking n=8𝑛8n=8, as addressed in detail in the next section, the decay width formulas for the near-threshold channels Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* and Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD, calculated using Eq. , together with the standard formula of Eq. for J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda, lead to consistent results from the pole determinations of both the total width and the couplings fixed from the residues of the pole position. Consequently, we will take this value of n=8𝑛8n=8 in the following analyses. The width saturation is then given by Γ=Γ1+Γ2=|g1|2​q1​(MR2)8​π​MR2+|g2|2​∫mth,2MR+n​ΓR𝑑w​q2​(w2)16​π2​w2​ΓR(MR−w)2+ΓR2/4. ΓsubscriptΓ1subscriptΓ2superscriptsubscript𝑔12subscript𝑞1superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑅28𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑅2superscriptsubscript𝑔22superscriptsubscriptsubscript𝑚th2subscript𝑀𝑅𝑛subscriptΓ𝑅differential-d𝑤subscript𝑞2superscript𝑤216superscript𝜋2superscript𝑤2subscriptΓ𝑅superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑅𝑤2superscriptsubscriptΓ𝑅24displaystyleGamma=Gamma1+Gamma2=|g1|2fracq1(MR2)8pi MR2+|g2|2intmrm th,2MR+nGammaRdw,fracq2(w2)16pi2,w2fracGammaR(MR-w)2+GammaR2/4,. Let us stress that we have also explicitly tried to set n𝑛n at larger values, say up to 50, and the results are found to be quantitatively similar to n=8𝑛8n=8. Therefore we conclude that the values presented in Tables and are robust under the mild changes of n𝑛n. The equations that represent the saturation of the compositeness coefficient X𝑋X and the decay width ΓΓGamma can be used to determine the couplings g1subscript𝑔1g1 and g2subscript𝑔2g2, which in turn allow us to further analyze the partial compositeness factors and the partial decay widths. This approach based on saturating given values of X𝑋X and ΓΓGamma is not suitable to distinguish between the different total angular momenta J𝐽J. The point is that the coupling strengths |gj|2superscriptsubscript𝑔𝑗2|gj|2 would appear in the same way for any total angular momentum J𝐽J. Possible contributions from lighter channels, such as the ηc​Λsubscript𝜂𝑐ΛetacLambda channel, which threshold is rather distant from the interested energy region around 4.46GeV, is effectively reabsorbed in the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda channel. To solve Eqs. and , one must first provide the value of the total compositeness X𝑋X, which is however typically unknown a priori. Here we carry out exploring studies by giving some tentative values to X𝑋X, and the results are summarized in Table for the situation in which the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* coupled channels are included, and in Table for the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD case. Three different values of X𝑋X, namely X=0.1,0.5𝑋0.10.5X=0.1,0.5 and 1.0, are used to analyze the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* system. It turns out that the coupling strengths of |g1|subscript𝑔1|g1| and |g2|subscript𝑔2|g2| tends to decrease and increase, respectively, when increasing the values of X𝑋X, so do the partial decay widths of Γ1subscriptΓ1Gamma1 and Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2. This trend is typical in this type of studies as previously observed citebib.bib56, bib.bib32. In all the cases, the partial compositeness factor X2subscript𝑋2X2 of the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* is always much larger than the compositeness coefficient X1subscript𝑋1X1 contributed by the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda channel. Even when taking X=1.0𝑋1.0X=1.0, the magnitudes of the two partial widths of the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* channels are similar because |g2|≫|g1|much-greater-thansubscript𝑔2subscript𝑔1|g2|gg|g1|. While, when taking smaller values for the total compositeness coefficient X𝑋X, the widths of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs are found to be dominated by the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda channel. For the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD coupled-channel system the maximum value of X𝑋X that results by solving Eqs. and turns out to be around 0.3. According to the results shown in Table, the partial compositeness factor X2subscript𝑋2X2 is always much larger than the value of X1subscript𝑋1X1, regardless of the total compositeness coefficient X𝑋X taken in the study. In contrast, the magnitudes of the two partial widths are rather sensitive to the values of X𝑋X. Larger values of X𝑋X lead to bigger partial widths Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2 of the Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD channel, and smaller Γ1subscriptΓ1Gamma1 for J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda. It is clear that definite values of X𝑋X will be obviously helpful to give more concrete conclusions of the properties of Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. In the following section, we perform the fits within dynamical unitarized models to the experimental event distributions of the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda from the LHCb collaborationcitebib.bib16. In this way, we are able to obtain more precise values of the coupling strengths gisubscript𝑔𝑖gi, which in turn allow us to perform predictions for the partial decay widths and compositeness coefficients contributed from different channels.
content_sections.4 Fits to the experimental event distrib: In the following, we perform fits to the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda invariant-mass distributions from the LHCbcitebib.bib16 data on the decay Ξb−→J/ψ​Λ​K−→subscriptsuperscriptΞ𝑏𝐽𝜓Λsuperscript𝐾Xi-bto J/psiLambda K-, in order to obtain the couplings and the resonance pole position. In this way one can further constrain the coupling strengths which will in turn give more definite predictions for X𝑋X. We consider first the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* channel, and later also include the Ξc′​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD one, as these are the two-body channels whose thresholds, lying around 4478 and 4446MeV, respectively, are very close to the resonance region of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. Because of this fact we study their interaction in S𝑆S wave only. In addition, we also include the much lighter channel J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda, whose event distribution will be fitted. In principle one could also include the ηc​Λsubscript𝜂𝑐ΛetacLambda since it is related to J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda by the HQSS, in which limit the mass of the J/ψ𝐽𝜓J/psi and ηcsubscript𝜂𝑐etac are equal. As we are interested only in the energy region around the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs resonance it is then legitimate to characterize both in terms of just one effective channel, namely the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda (more detailed discussions on this point are given below). It is illustrative to consider the decomposition of the two-body states in the basis of HQSS eigenstates |sH⊗jℓ⟩kettensor-productsubscript𝑠𝐻subscript𝑗ℓ|sHotimes jell, where sHsubscript𝑠𝐻sH and jℓsubscript𝑗ℓjell are the total spin and angular momentum of the heavy and light quarks, respectively. The |Ξc⟩ketsubscriptΞ𝑐|Xic and |D¯∗⟩ketsuperscript¯𝐷|barD* spin multiplets are |12⊗0⟩1/2subscriptkettensor-product12012|frac12otimes 01/2 and |12⊗12⟩1subscriptkettensor-product12121|frac12otimesfrac121 in this basis, where the subscript indicates the spin jisubscript𝑗𝑖ji of each state. The latter arises from the angular momentum combination of sHi⊗jℓitensor-productsubscript𝑠subscript𝐻𝑖subscript𝑗subscriptℓ𝑖sHiotimes jelli, with the index i=1𝑖1i=1 referring to ΞcsubscriptΞ𝑐Xic and i=2𝑖2i=2 to D¯∗superscript¯𝐷barD*. One can then combine j1⊗j2tensor-productsubscript𝑗1subscript𝑗2j1otimes j2 in states of definite total angular momentum J𝐽J, |sH1​jℓ1​j1;sH2​jℓ2​j2;J⟩ketsubscript𝑠subscript𝐻1subscript𝑗subscriptℓ1subscript𝑗1subscript𝑠subscript𝐻2subscript𝑗subscriptℓ2subscript𝑗2𝐽|sH1jell1j1;sH2jell2j2;J, which we simply designate in the following by |Ξc​D¯∗⟩JsubscriptketsubscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷𝐽|XicbarD*J. Another way is to combine first the heavy spins sH1⊗sH2tensor-productsubscript𝑠subscript𝐻1subscript𝑠subscript𝐻2sH1otimes sH2 and the light angular momenta jℓ1⊗jℓ2tensor-productsubscript𝑗subscriptℓ1subscript𝑗subscriptℓ2jell1otimes jell2 separately into states with well defined total heavy spin sHsubscript𝑠𝐻sH and total light angular momentum jLsubscript𝑗𝐿jL, respectively. Then, they are composed to give states of the form |sH1​sH2​sH;jℓ1​jℓ2​jL;J⟩ketsubscript𝑠subscript𝐻1subscript𝑠subscript𝐻2subscript𝑠𝐻subscript𝑗subscriptℓ1subscript𝑗subscriptℓ2subscript𝑗𝐿𝐽|sH1sH2sH;jell1jell2jL;J, that we simply designate in the following by |sH⊗jL⟩Jsubscriptkettensor-productsubscript𝑠𝐻subscript𝑗𝐿𝐽|sHotimes jLJ, with definite total angular momentum J𝐽J. The relation between the two sets of basis vectors with total angular momentum well defined is through the Wigner 9​j9𝑗9j symbols. See e. g. Appendix A of Ref. citebib.bib57 for further details in this formalism. In this way, it follows that |Ξc​D¯∗⟩12subscriptketsubscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷12displaystyle|XicbarD*frac12 =32​|0⊗12⟩12−12​|1⊗12⟩12,absent32subscriptkettensor-product0121212subscriptkettensor-product11212displaystyle=fracsqrt32|0otimesfrac12frac12-frac12|1otimesfrac12frac12, |Ξc​D¯∗⟩32subscriptketsubscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷32displaystyle|XicbarD*frac32 =|1⊗12⟩32. absentsubscriptkettensor-product11232displaystyle=|1otimesfrac12frac32. The states |J/ψ⟩ket𝐽𝜓|J/psi, |ηc⟩ketsubscript𝜂𝑐|etac and |Λ⟩ketΛ|Lambda are |1⊗0⟩kettensor-product10|1otimes 0, |0⊗0⟩kettensor-product00|0otimes 0 and |0⊗12⟩kettensor-product012|0otimesfrac12, in this order. Therefore, |ηc​Λ⟩=|0⊗12⟩12ketsubscript𝜂𝑐Λsubscriptkettensor-product01212|etacLambda=|0otimesfrac12frac12, while |J/ψ​Λ⟩J=|1⊗12⟩Jsubscriptket𝐽𝜓Λ𝐽subscriptkettensor-product112𝐽|J/psiLambdaJ=|1otimesfrac12J has both J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 and 3/2323/2. However, the transformation matrix for the |Ξc​D¯∗⟩3/2subscriptketsubscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷32|XicbarD*3/2 states is just the identity, as it is clear from Eq. . We then obtain the fit 320. Even though we now impose that C12=C32subscript𝐶12subscript𝐶32Cfrac12=Cfrac32, the χ2=3.06superscript𝜒23.06chi2=3.06 is almost equal to that for the fit 220, and the fit reproduces very well the data as shown in the last panel of Fig. . The values for the χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 and the fit parameters are given in Tables and . In addition we also notice that C12=C32subscript𝐶12subscript𝐶32Cfrac12=Cfrac32 is almost identical to the values for C12subscript𝐶12Cfrac12 registered for the fits 210 and 220, which then indicates that this parameter is well determined within our approach. Related to this, we conclude then that our hypothesis that the lack of the channel Ξc′​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD is the reason for the HQSS breakdown in the relation C12=C32subscript𝐶12subscript𝐶32Cfrac12=Cfrac32, as obtained in the fit , is well confronted to phenomenology. The nomenclature for the RSs with three channels is arranged in increasing values for the thresholds. Namely, the RSII (−++)(-++) is the one connecting with the RSI (+++)(+++) above the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and below the Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD thresholds, the RSIII (−−+)(--+) connects with the physical sheet between the Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD and the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* thresholds, and the RSIV connects with RSI above the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* threshold. In this case the scattering in the J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 and J=3/2𝐽32J=3/2 angular momenta is not interchangeable and we can give the corresponding spin of the resonances found in Table. The two poles observed for the fit 320, collected in Table, one with J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 (first peak in the last panel of Fig. ) and another with J=3/2𝐽32J=3/2 (the higher one in the same plot), have each of them an imaginary part that is smaller in absolute value than the one in the first two poles of Table. This is indeed related with the size of the coupling g𝑔g which is also smaller for the 320 fits as compared with the 220. Since this coupling controls how strongly the relatively-light channel J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda couples to Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, and the resonances are strongly affected by the latter channel, the smaller the value of g𝑔g the smaller the resulting widths because the residue g1subscript𝑔1g1 is proportional to it. We have also explored new fits in which g′superscript𝑔′gprime, C12′subscriptsuperscript𝐶′12Cprimefrac12 and/or d12′subscriptsuperscript𝑑′12dprimefrac12 are allowed to float. However, we do not observe any significant change, either qualitative or quantitative, in the description of the data. For instance, the resulting χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 in every case barely improves compared to the fit 320. Of course, the resulting fits are typically over-determined so that some of the parameters are completely undetermined (having errorbars that are much bigger than their central values). Let us discuss two of such fits. If we still fix C12′=0subscriptsuperscript𝐶′120Cprimefrac12=0 but release g′superscript𝑔′gprime we have the fit 1subscript32011 in Tables and . We observe only a slight improvement in the χ2superscript𝜒2chi2, g′=59.6−249.2+160.7superscript𝑔′subscriptsuperscript59.6160.7249.2gprime=59.6+160.7-249.2 is compatible with zero and basically undetermined, while the rest of fit parameters are very similar to the reference fit with three channels 320. However, if we fix g′=0superscript𝑔′0gprime=0 and release C12′subscriptsuperscript𝐶′12Cprimefrac12 the resulting fit 2subscript32022 in Tables and , gives rise to a very uncertain coupling g𝑔g. Though, it is still compatible within errors with the determinations from the other three-channel fits, its central value is very different, while for the other fits 320 and 1subscript32011 their central values are quite similar. Notice also that again the χ2=2.81superscript𝜒22.81chi2=2.81 has improved only by a basically negligible amount compared to 320. The pole content for these fits is not shown because it is very similar to the one already given in Table. In summary, our reference fit for the three-channel case is the 320 one, in which the Ξc′​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD channel is introduced perturbatively (g′=C12′=d12′=0superscript𝑔′subscriptsuperscript𝐶′12subscriptsuperscript𝑑′120gprime=Cprimefrac12=dprimefrac12=0) and we also take into account both J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 and J=3/2𝐽32J=3/2. Releasing either g′superscript𝑔′gprime or C12′subscriptsuperscript𝐶′12Cprimefrac12 does not really provide better fits and the values for the fit parameters are compatible with those from 320. This indicates a perturbative role of the Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD channel, which is also in agreement with our previous results in Sec. when considering only the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξc′​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD channels in J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2. Let us recall that in that case it was not possible to account for the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs resonance signal.

With the determined pole parameters in Table for the fit involving the three-coupled channels J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda, Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, we can revise the discussions of the compositeness and partial decay widths, and make comparisons with the studies based on the saturation of the compositeness coefficient and decay width in the Sec. . For the partial decay width of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs into the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda channel, one can safely use the standard decay width formula in Eq. . For the partial widths into the near-threshold channels Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, we will rely on the Lorentzian spectral integration formula in Eq. . By taking n=8𝑛8n=8, the partial decay widths for the lighter resonance pole with J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 in Table are found to be Γ1=0.5−0.5+1.9​MeV,Γ2=4.3−1.4+1.2​MeV,Γ3=0.9−0.6+1.2​MeV,formulae-sequencesubscriptΓ1superscriptsubscript0.50.51.9MeVformulae-sequencesubscriptΓ2superscriptsubscript4.31.41.2MeVsubscriptΓ3superscriptsubscript0.90.61.2MeVdisplaystyleGamma1=0.5-0.5+1.9rm MeV,,quadGamma2=4.3-1.4+1.2rm MeV,,quadGamma3=0.9-0.6+1.2rm MeV,, and the sum of the three widths agrees nicely with the width of the corresponding resonance pole, i. e. minus twice the imaginary part of the pole. This is also the main reason to take n=8𝑛8n=8 here as well as in Sec. . The partial compositeness coefficients contributed by the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD based on the pole parameters in Table are X1=0.0±0.0,X2=0.15±0.05. formulae-sequencesubscript𝑋1plus-or-minus0.00.0subscript𝑋2plus-or-minus0.150.05displaystyle X1=0.0pm 0.0,,quad X2=0.15pm 0.05,. It should be noted that for the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* channel the pole position of the lighter resonance state with J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 does not meet the working condition of Ref. citebib.bib37, and as a result it is not very meaningful to interpret the partial X2subscript𝑋2X2 (corresponding to X3subscript𝑋3X3 in the three-channel case) calculated in Eq. as the probability of the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* component in that resonance pole. In fact, had we blindly used Eq. to calculate X2subscript𝑋2X2 its value found would be 0.94, so that the sum of compositeness for the three channels would turn out to be slightly larger than 1. This is a manifestation that one should follow the working conditions argued in Ref. citebib.bib37 for giving a probabilistic interpretation of the compositeness X𝑋X of a resonance. For the heavier resonance pole with J=3/2𝐽32J=3/2 in Table , the partial widths calculated for the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* are Γ1=2.6−2.6+8.2​MeV,Γ3=0.4−0.4+2.5​MeV,formulae-sequencesubscriptΓ1superscriptsubscript2.62.68.2MeVsubscriptΓ3superscriptsubscript0.40.42.5MeVdisplaystyleGamma1=2.6-2.6+8.2rm MeV,,quadGamma3=0.4-0.4+2.5rm MeV,, and the resulting partial compositeness coefficients turn out to be X1=0.0±0.0,X3=1.0−0.2+0.2. formulae-sequencesubscript𝑋1plus-or-minus0.00.0subscript𝑋3superscriptsubscript1.00.20.2displaystyle X1=0.0pm 0.0,,quad X3=1.0-0.2+0.2,. Despite that the mass of the J=3/2𝐽32J=3/2 pole lies clearly below the threshold of Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* we have given X3subscript𝑋3X3 calculated from Eq. as an indication that this resonance is clearly dominated by the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* channel component. A similar comment could be also made in relation to X3subscript𝑋3X3 for the J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 pole. Indeed, we also find for both poles that the coupling |g3|subscript𝑔3|g3| is huge in comparison with |g1|subscript𝑔1|g1| and much larger, by around a factor 7/2, than |g2|subscript𝑔2|g2| for J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2. We point out that a direct comparison of the results for the partial widths and compositeness coefficients in this section and the ones in Sec. is not straightforward. The point is that in Sec. the discussions are based on the masses and widths of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs from the single-state determination of the LHCb collaborationcitebib.bib16. However, in the present section our reference fit prefers the two-resonance solutions and the widths of the two resonance poles taking separately are found to be much smaller than the single-state determinations from the LHCb, though the combination of the two nearby peaks, convoluted with the experimental resolution in energy, gives rise to a total signal as a double bump with a width in agreement with the one pole determination for the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs citebib.bib16. Nonetheless, both the results here and in Sec. are essentially compatible, as one concludes by comparing the couplings |gi|subscript𝑔𝑖|gi| in the last and first columns of Tables and , respectively, with those determined by fitting directly the experimental data in Table.
content_sections.5 Conclusions: In this work we focus on the narrow peak around 4459MeV observed in the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda event distributions from the LHCb measurementscitebib.bib16, which is the first discovered charmonium pentaquark with strangeness. We have considered three different methods, based on the elastic effective-range expansion, the saturation of the width and the compositeness of the resonance, and a fit to data employing unitarized phenomenological amplitudes with constraints from heavy-quark-spin symmetry. It is important to notice that the different methods provide compatible results for those magnitudes to which they can be applied for their calculation. In this sense, our analyses clearly favor a dominant role of the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* state in the composition of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. For instance, the elastic effective-range analysis gives an effective range of −0.94±0.13plus-or-minus0.940.13-0.94pm 0.13fm when considering only the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, while it is much larger in absolute value, around −44-4fm, when taking only the Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD channel. When considering the fits to data we then obtain that the compositeness factor X𝑋X of the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* channel is around one, implying that it is saturated almost entirely by the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* channel alone. Finally, even if allowing the presence of a CDD pole in the two-channel case, with J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and either Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* or Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD, no acceptable fits result, which is also a clear indication of the molecular scenario. Another important outcome of our fits is that when requiring heavy-quark-spin symmetry, such that the J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 and 3/2 couplings of the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* are equal, our analysis requires to include also the Ξc′​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD channel to fit data properly. Furthermore, in such circumstances, the resulting fit contains two poles in the energy region of interest around the resonant peak of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs, a J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 pole with mass 4453.8−3.3+2.4subscriptsuperscript4453.82.43.34453.8+2.4-3.3MeV, and a J=3/2𝐽32J=3/2 one with mass 4466.6−2.7+1.9subscriptsuperscript4466.61.92.74466.6+1.9-2.7MeV. These two poles are narrow, the half-width obtained for the lighter one is 2.8−0.8+0.9subscriptsuperscript2.80.90.82.8+0.9-0.8MeV, and that for the heavier one is determined much less precisely as 1.3−3.7+1.3subscriptsuperscript1.31.33.71.3+1.3-3.7MeV. With the present energy resolution and statistics of the LHCb collaboration citebib.bib16 there is only a small hint that there are actually two resonances involved, though this could be disentangled in the future by experiments with improved statistics.

sorted_papers.title:
Structure of exotic hadrons by a weak-binding relation with finite-range
  correction
sorted_papers.abstract:
The composite nature of a shallow bound state is studied by using the
weak-binding relation, which connects the compositeness of the bound state with
observables. We first show that the previous weak-binding relation cannot be
applied to the system with a large effective range. To overcome this
difficulty, we introduce the finite-range correction by redefining the typical
length scale in the weak-binding relation. A method to estimate the uncertainty
of the compositeness is proposed. It is numerically demonstrated that the range
correction enlarges the applicable region of the weak-binding relation.
Finally, we apply the improved weak-binding relation to the actual hadrons,
nuclei, and atomic systems [deuteron, $X(3872)$, $D^{*}_{s0}(2317)$,
$D_{s1}(2460)$, $N\Omega$ dibaryon, $\Omega\Omega$ dibaryon,
${}^{3}_{\Lambda}{\rm H}$, and ${}^{4}{\rm He}$ dimer] to discuss their
internal structure from the compositeness. We present a reasonable estimation
of the compositeness of the deuteron by properly taking into account the
uncertainty. The results of $X(3872)$ and the $N\Omega$ dibaryon show that the
range correction is important to estimate the compositeness of physical states.

sections.7: Weinberg, Evidence That the Deuteron Is Not an Elementary Particle, Phys
sections.9: Hyodo, Structure and compositeness of hadron resonances, Int
sections.18: Hyodo, Hadron mass scaling near the s-wave threshold, Phys
sections.27: Juric et al., A new determination of the binding-energy values of the light hypernuclei (15>>=a), Nucl
sections.35: Machleidt, The High precision, charge dependent Bonn nucleon-nucleon potential (CD-Bonn), Phys
sections.37: Gongyo et al., Most Strange Dibaryon from Lattice QCD, Phys
sections.43: Weinberg, Evidence That the Deuteron Is Not an Elementary Particle, Phys

qualitative: The weak-binding relation provides a model-independent method to estimate the compositeness X of a shallow bound state, defined as the probability of finding the hadronic molecular component in the physical state's wavefunction.
The standard weak-binding relation fails for systems with a large effective range, indicating the need for a finite-range correction to maintain accuracy.
The range correction is introduced by redefining the typical length scale R_typ in the weak-binding relation to account for non-negligible effective range effects.

The compositeness X is a key indicator of exotic hadron structure, with values close to 1 suggesting a dominant molecular (exotic) component, while values near 0 indicate a compact quark-based structure.

Low-energy universality holds when the scattering length a_0 is much larger than other length scales, but deviations occur when the effective range r_e is significant, necessitating range corrections.
The improved weak-binding relation with range correction extends the applicability to systems with large effective ranges, such as X(3872), NΩ dibaryon, and the deuteron.
The uncertainty in compositeness estimation is quantified through a proposed method, enhancing the reliability of model-independent analyses.
quantitative: Weak-binding relation: a_0 = R^2 * X / (1 + X) + O(R_typ / R)
Binding energy and radius relation: R = 1 / sqrt(2 * μ * B)
Scattering length a_0 ≈ R in the low-energy universal limit (a_0 >> r_e, R_typ)
Effective range r_e is a key parameter determining the necessity of range correction; large r_e invalidates the standard weak-binding relation
Compositeness X = |⟨molecule|Ψ⟩|^2, where |Ψ⟩ is the normalized bound state wavefunction
Numerical demonstration shows that the range-corrected weak-binding relation improves accuracy for systems with r_e comparable to a_0
Application to deuteron yields a reasonable compositeness estimate with proper uncertainty treatment
For X(3872) and NΩ dibaryon, the range correction significantly alters the compositeness estimate, highlighting its physical importance

约束项检查：

- C2：方法为“Weinberg复合度准则” — 满足。证据：“weak-binding relation”即Weinberg复合度判据的实现形式，用于计算复合度  
- C3：研究对象包含“X(3872)”等实际强子 — 满足。证据：“X(3872)”, “D*_{s0}(2317)”等明确列出

authors: Tomona Kinugawa and Tetsuo Hyodo

content_sections.I Introduction: Constituent quark models describe hadrons as the q​q​q𝑞𝑞𝑞qqq or q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq states, and they have succeeded in explaining the spectrum of most of observed hadronscitebib.bib1, bib.bib2. However, recent experiments imply that some states in the heavy quark sector have an exotic internal structure, other than q​q​q𝑞𝑞𝑞qqq or q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarqcitebib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5. It is considered that those states are, for instance, the multiquark states which are the compact states with at least four quarks, hadronic molecular states which are the weakly bound states of hadrons, or gluon hybrid states which contain the constituent gluons. The intensive studies with both the theoretical and experimental approaches have been performed to clarify the nature of the candidates for exotic hadrons. One of the most actively studied candidates for exotic hadrons is X​𝑋3872X, observed in the invariant mass spectrum of π+​π−​J/ψsuperscript𝜋superscript𝜋𝐽𝜓pi+pi-J/psi in the B±→K±​π+​π−​J/ψ→superscript𝐵plus-or-minussuperscript𝐾plus-or-minussuperscript𝜋superscript𝜋𝐽𝜓Bpmto Kpmpi+pi-J/psi decay in 2003 by the Belle Collaborationcitebib.bib6. Because X​𝑋3872X was observed in the lower energy region than χc​1′superscriptsubscript𝜒𝑐1′chic1prime predicted by the quark model, it is expected to have an exotic structure. In addition to X​𝑋3872X, many states have been observed in the higher energy regions than the D​D¯𝐷¯𝐷DbarD threshold. These states do not fit well in the prediction by the quark model, and are called X​Y​Z𝑋𝑌𝑍XYZ mesonscitebib.bib5. In 2021, Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc was observed in the invariant mass spectrum of D0​D0​π+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐷0superscript𝜋D0D0pi+ in the p​p𝑝𝑝pp collision by the LHCb Collaborationcitebib.bib7, bib.bib8. Because Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc has the charm C=+2𝐶2C=+2 and the minimal quark content is c​c​u¯​d¯𝑐𝑐¯𝑢¯𝑑ccbarubard, Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc is an exotic state with at least four quarks. To study the internal structure of the candidates for exotic hadrons theoretically, we employ the weak-binding relationcitebib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11 as a model-independent approach. For this purpose, we introduce the compositeness X𝑋Xcitebib.bib12 which is the quantitative weight of the hadronic molecular component in the physical hadron wavefunction. Denoting the hadronic molecular component schematically as |molecule⟩ketmoleculeketrm molecule, we can express X𝑋X as the probability to find the hadronic molecular component in the normalized wavefunction of the bound state |Ψ⟩ketΨketPsi: X𝑋displaystyle X =|⟨molecule|Ψ⟩|2. absentsuperscriptinner-productmoleculeΨ2displaystyle=|braketrm moleculePsi|2. It was shown in Refs. citebib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11 that X𝑋X and the observables satisfy the weak-binding relation: a0=R​2​X1+X+𝒪​(RtypR). subscript𝑎0𝑅2𝑋1𝑋𝒪subscript𝑅typ𝑅displaystyle a0=Rleftfrac2X1+X+Oleft(fracRrm typRright)right. Here a0subscript𝑎0a0 is the scattering length, R≡1/2​μ​B𝑅12𝜇𝐵Requiv 1/sqrt2mu B is the radius of the bound state, determined by the binding energy B𝐵B and the reduced mass μ𝜇mu, and Rtypsubscript𝑅typRrm typ is the typical length scale of the interaction, for instance, the range of the interaction. When B𝐵B is sufficiently small and R≫Rtypmuch-greater-than𝑅subscript𝑅typRgg Rrm typ, we can neglect the correction terms 𝒪​(Rtyp/R)𝒪subscript𝑅typ𝑅O(Rrm typ/R) in Eq. . Then, the weak-binding relation reduces to a0=R​2​X/(1+X)subscript𝑎0𝑅2𝑋1𝑋a0=R2X/(1+X), and X𝑋X is model-independently estimated only by the observables a0subscript𝑎0a0 and B𝐵B for the weakly bound states. In general, the low-energy universality holdscitebib.bib13, bib.bib14 when the scattering length a0subscript𝑎0a0 is sufficiently larger than other length scales. If a system has a large scattering length, the microscopic details of the system can be neglected, and the different systems follow the same universal laws. For example, for two-body systems with a shallow bound state, the low-energy universality implies the relation a0=Rsubscript𝑎0𝑅a0=R. For three-body systems, the Efimov effectcitebib.bib15, bib.bib13, bib.bib14 is known. In practice, when the effective range resubscript𝑟𝑒re is not negligible in comparison with a0subscript𝑎0a0, there arises the deviation from the consequence of the universality, called the range correction. In this paper, we discuss the range correction in the weak-binding relation. We first show that Eq. does not hold when the effective range resubscript𝑟𝑒re is large which indicates the necessity of the range correction. We then propose the improvement of the weak-binding relation with the range correction so that it becomes appricable to the system with a large resubscript𝑟𝑒re. Finally, we discuss the internal structure of the actual physical states using the improved weak-binding relation with the range correction. The compositeness and the weak-binding relation were proposed by Weinberg to analyze the nature of the deuteroncitebib.bib9, and have been applied to many hadron systems since around 2000. The model-independent estimations of the compositeness from the weak-binding relation are proposed in Refs. citebib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib19, and the calculations of the compositeness from the residue of the poles are also performed in Refs. citebib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26. Some recent studies discuss the range corrections in the weak-binding relation focusing on the compositeness of the deuteron. In Ref. citebib.bib27, the authors introduce a general form factor of the bound state and discuss the range correction through the form factor. The model with a finite cutoff is considered in Ref. citebib.bib28. By comparing the effective range in the model with the experimental value to determine the value of the cutoff, they estimate the compositeness. In Ref. citebib.bib29, the authors introduce the effects of Rtypsubscript𝑅typRrm typ to a0subscript𝑎0a0 and resubscript𝑟𝑒re as the phenomenological term, and estimate the likelihood of the compositeness model-independently. This paper is organized as follows. For later discussions, in Sec. , we introduce the nonrelativistic effective field theory which has the derivative-coupling interaction and the coupling with the discrete state. In Sec. , we discuss the weak-binding relation in the system with a non-negligible effective range, and propose the range correction by modifying the correction terms. We perform the numerical calculations to check the validity of the range correction in Sec. . In Sec. , the improved weak-binding relation is applied to the actual hadrons, hypernuclei, and atomic systems to discuss their internal structure by focusing on the significance of the range correction. Section is devoted to summary. The preliminary results of Secs. , , and are partly reported in Refs. citebib.bib30, citebib.bib31, and citebib.bib32, respectively.
content_sections.II Effective field theory: We introduce a nonrelativistic effective field theory to discuss the low-energy limit of two-hadron-scattering systems with a weakly bound state in this section. In general, effective field theories describe the low-energy phenomena of some microscopic theoriescitebib.bib33. Here, we consider the scattering of two identical bosons with the following Hamiltonian: ℋℋdisplaystyleH =12​m​∇ψ†⋅∇ψ+14​m​∇ϕ†⋅∇ϕ+ν0​ϕ†​ϕabsent⋅12𝑚∇superscript𝜓†∇𝜓⋅14𝑚∇superscriptitalic-ϕ†∇italic-ϕsubscript𝜈0superscriptitalic-ϕ†italic-ϕdisplaystyle=frac12mnablapsidaggercdotnablapsi+frac14mnablaphidaggercdotnablaphi+nu0phidaggerphi +14​λ0​(ψ†​ψ)2+14​ρ0​∇(ψ†​ψ)⋅∇(ψ†​ψ)14subscript𝜆0superscriptsuperscript𝜓†𝜓2⋅14subscript𝜌0∇superscript𝜓†𝜓∇superscript𝜓†𝜓displaystylequad+frac14lambda0(psidaggerpsi)2+frac14rho0nabla(psidaggerpsi)cdotnabla(psidaggerpsi) +12​g0​(ϕ†​ψ2+ψ†2​ϕ),12subscript𝑔0superscriptitalic-ϕ†superscript𝜓2superscript𝜓†absent2italic-ϕdisplaystylequad+frac12g0(phidaggerpsi2+psidagger 2phi), where ψ𝜓psi is the boson field, ϕitalic-ϕphi is the discrete state coupled with the two-boson scattering, m𝑚m is the boson mass, and ν0subscript𝜈0nu0 is the energy of ϕitalic-ϕphi measured from the two-ψ𝜓psi threshold. λ0subscript𝜆0lambda0 and ρ0subscript𝜌0rho0 are the bare coupling constants of the two-ψ𝜓psi contact interaction and the derivative coupling term, respectively. g0subscript𝑔0g0 is the coupling constant of the two-body ψ𝜓psi scattering and the discrete state ϕitalic-ϕphi. We introduce the momentum cutoff ΛΛLambda to remove the divergence of the momentum integration of the intermediate bosons in the Lippmann-Schwinger equation, which stems from the contact interactions in Hamiltonian. This theory is renormalizable; we can take the limit Λ→∞→ΛLambdatoinfty with keeping the observables finite. This corresponds to the zero-range limit, because the inverse of the cutoff is regarded as the interaction range. Here, we leave ΛΛLambda finite so that the interaction range is also finite. Because the interaction terms of the Hamiltonian have no angular dependence, the scattering occurs only in the s𝑠s wave. The two-ψ𝜓psi scattering amplitude f​(k)𝑓𝑘f(k) at momentum k𝑘k is obtained as f​(k)=[−8​πm​(1+m12​π2​ρ0​Λ3)2(λ0+g02E−ν0−m20​π2​ρ02​Λ5)+2​ρ0​(1+m24​π2​ρ0​Λ3)​k2−2​Λπ−i​k]−1,𝑓𝑘superscriptdelimited-8𝜋𝑚superscript1𝑚12superscript𝜋2subscript𝜌0superscriptΛ32subscript𝜆0superscriptsubscript𝑔02𝐸subscript𝜈0𝑚20superscript𝜋2superscriptsubscript𝜌02superscriptΛ52subscript𝜌01𝑚24superscript𝜋2subscript𝜌0superscriptΛ3superscript𝑘22Λ𝜋𝑖𝑘1displaystyle f(k)=left[-frac8pimfrac(1+fracm12pi2rho0Lambda3)2(lambda0+fracg02E-nu0-fracm20pi2rho02Lambda5)+2rho0(1+fracm24pi2rho0Lambda3)k2-frac2Lambdapi-ikright]-1, with E=k2/m𝐸superscript𝑘2𝑚E=k2/m. Here we neglect the terms which vanish in the Λ→∞→ΛLambdatoinfty limit in the momentum integration. The scattering length a0subscript𝑎0a0 and the effective range resubscript𝑟𝑒re are read off by comparing it with the effective range expansion: f​(k)𝑓𝑘displaystyle f(k) =[−1a0+re2​k2+⋯−i​k]−1. absentsuperscriptdelimited-1subscript𝑎0subscript𝑟𝑒2superscript𝑘2⋯𝑖𝑘1displaystyle=left[-frac1a0+fracre2k2+cdots-ikright]-1. In this model, the effective range resubscript𝑟𝑒re can take any negative value. Positive resubscript𝑟𝑒re is, however, allowed only below an upper bound (Wigner bound) as discussed in Sec. . content_sections.III Range correction: We discuss the range correction in the weak-binding relation in this section. In Sec. , we first give the physical interpretation of each term in the weak-binding relation from the viewpoint of the low-energy universality. We then classify the property of the exact values of the compositeness of the models introduced in the previous section in Sec. . In Sec. , we show that the contradiction arises when we apply the weak-binding relation to the effective range model in the zero-range limit. To solve the above problem, we propose the range correction to the weak-binding relation in Sec. . A practical method to estimate the uncertainty of the compositeness is presented in Sec. .
content_sections.III Range correction III.1 Low-energy un: Let us consider the weak-binding relation from the perspective of the low-energy universality. It is known that the radius of the bound state R𝑅R is scaled by the scattering length a0subscript𝑎0a0 as a consequence of the low-energy universalitycitebib.bib13, bib.bib14: R𝑅displaystyle R ≡12​μ​B=a0. absent12𝜇𝐵subscript𝑎0displaystyleequivfrac1sqrt2mu B=a0. Both the scattering length a0subscript𝑎0a0 and the radius R𝑅R diverge when B→0→𝐵0Bto 0 as shown in Eq. . In this weak-binding limit, the weight of the two-body composite state dominates the whole wavefunction (X→1→𝑋1Xto 1)citebib.bib34. This is consistent with the consequence of the weak-binding relation with X=1𝑋1X=1 and a0=Rsubscript𝑎0𝑅a0=R for R→∞→𝑅Rtoinfty. When the binding energy is finite and the system is away from the weak-binding limit (RRsubscript𝑎0𝑅a0>R), suggesting the importance of the finite interaction rangecitebib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29.

In the zero-range model and the effective range model which have no couplings with other channels, the completeness relation is given by ∫d3​k(2​π)3​|𝒌⟩​⟨𝒌|=1,superscript𝑑3𝑘superscript2𝜋3ket𝒌bra𝒌1displaystyleintfracd3k(2pi)3ketkbrak=1, where |𝒌⟩ket𝒌ketk is the eigenstate of the free Hamiltonian with the relative momentum 𝒌𝒌k in the ψ​ψ𝜓𝜓psipsi scattering. content_sections.III Range correction III.3 Deviation fro: We now consider the role of the effective range in the weak-binding relation. The weak-binding relation implies a0=Rsubscript𝑎0𝑅a0=R, in accordance with Eq. , for any single-channel models (X=1𝑋1X=1) with the zero-range interaction (Rtyp=0subscript𝑅typ0Rrm typ=0). However, we show that the effective range model does not lead to a0=Rsubscript𝑎0𝑅a0=R in the zero-range limit. To see this, we derive the scattering amplitude in the zero-range limit by taking the Λ→∞→ΛLambdatoinfty limit in the renormalized scattering amplitude of the effective range model: f​(k)=[−1a0+re2​k2−i​k]−1. 𝑓𝑘superscriptdelimited-1subscript𝑎0subscript𝑟𝑒2superscript𝑘2𝑖𝑘1displaystyle f(k)=left[-frac1a0+fracre2k2-ikright]-1. To relate a0subscript𝑎0a0 and R𝑅R in this model, we search for the pole of f​(k)𝑓𝑘f(k). Because the bound-state pole appears at k=i/R𝑘𝑖𝑅k=i/R, we express a0subscript𝑎0a0 by R𝑅R and resubscript𝑟𝑒re using the bound-state condition a0subscript𝑎0displaystyle a0 =R​11−re/(2​R)absent𝑅11subscript𝑟𝑒2𝑅displaystyle=Rfrac11-re/(2R) =R​[1+𝒪​(|re|R)],absent𝑅delimited-1𝒪subscript𝑟𝑒𝑅displaystyle=Rleft[1+Oleft(frac|re|Rright)right], where we take the absolute value of resubscript𝑟𝑒re because it can be negative. We find the deviation from a0=Rsubscript𝑎0𝑅a0=R by the contribution from the finite effective range resubscript𝑟𝑒re. This contradicts with the weak-binding relation if Rtypsubscript𝑅typRrm typ is regarded as the interaction range. Therefore, Eq. shows the necessity of the range correction in the weak-binding relation. In addition, to discuss the effects of the higher order terms of the effective range expansion, we consider the zero-range model whose scattering amplitude includes the fourth order term of k𝑘k: f​(k)=[−1a0+re2​k2−Ps4​k4−i​k]−1,𝑓𝑘superscriptdelimited-1subscript𝑎0subscript𝑟𝑒2superscript𝑘2subscript𝑃𝑠4superscript𝑘4𝑖𝑘1displaystyle f(k)=left[-frac1a0+fracre2k2-fracPs4k4-ikright]-1, where Pssubscript𝑃𝑠Ps is the shape parameter with the dimension of length cubed. From the pole of Eq. , the scattering length a0subscript𝑎0a0 is obtained as follows: a0subscript𝑎0displaystyle a0 =R[1+12(reR)+14(reR)2+(re38+Ps4)(1R)3displaystyle=Rleft[1+frac12left(fracreRright)+frac14left(fracreRright)2+left(fracre38+fracPs4right)left(frac1Rright)3right. +𝒪(|re|4R4,|re​Ps|R4)]. displaystylequad+left. Oleft(frac|re|4R4,frac|rePs|R4right)right]. In ordinary systems, the effective range resubscript𝑟𝑒re and the shape parameter Pssubscript𝑃𝑠Ps are estimated by the typical length scale of the interaction. For instance, we expect that |re|≈1subscript𝑟𝑒1|re|approx 1fm and |Ps|≈1subscript𝑃𝑠1|Ps|approx 1fm in the case of the strong interaction. In the weak-binding case where R𝑅R is much larger than other scales, it is expected that |Ps|/R3≪|re|/Rmuch-less-thansubscript𝑃𝑠superscript𝑅3subscript𝑟𝑒𝑅|Ps|/R3ll|re|/R. In this case, Eq. reduces to Eq. for the effective range model. However, if the shape parameter happens to have a large magnitude, |Ps|/R3subscript𝑃𝑠superscript𝑅3|Ps|/R3 can be much larger than |re|/Rsubscript𝑟𝑒𝑅|re|/R. In this case, a0subscript𝑎0a0 becomes a0=R​[1+Ps4​(1R)3+𝒪​(|re|R)]=R​[1+𝒪​(|Ps|R3)]. subscript𝑎0𝑅delimited-1subscript𝑃𝑠4superscript1𝑅3𝒪subscript𝑟𝑒𝑅𝑅delimited-1𝒪subscript𝑃𝑠superscript𝑅3displaystyle a0=Rleft[1+fracPs4left(frac1Rright)3+Oleft(frac|re|Rright)right]=Rleft[1+Oleft(frac|Ps|R3right)right]. This equation shows that the leading order deviation from a0=Rsubscript𝑎0𝑅a0=R can be the term of Pssubscript𝑃𝑠Ps. The same discussion holds when the other length scales in the effective range expansion are accidentally large.
content_sections.III Range correction III.4 Range correct: Based on the above discussion, we propose the range correction in the weak-binding relation. We show that the weak-binding relation does not reproduce Eq. in the effective range model. To solve this problem, we consider the modification of the correction terms 𝒪​(Rtyp/R)𝒪subscript𝑅typ𝑅O(Rrm typ/R) in Eq. . First we define the length scale in the effective range expansion Reffsubscript𝑅effRrm eff as Reffsubscript𝑅effdisplaystyle Rrm eff =max⁡|re|,|Ps|R2,…. absentsubscript𝑟𝑒subscript𝑃𝑠superscript𝑅2…displaystyle=maxleft|re|,frac|Ps|R2,. . . right. Then we define Rintsubscript𝑅intRrm int as the interaction range which was denoted by Rtypsubscript𝑅typRrm typ in the previous workscitebib.bib11. As the range correction in the weak-binding relation, we propose to redefine Rtypsubscript𝑅typRrm typ in the correction terms as the largest length scale among Rintsubscript𝑅intRrm int and Reffsubscript𝑅effRrm eff: Rtypsubscript𝑅typdisplaystyle Rrm typ =max⁡Rint,Reff. absentsubscript𝑅intsubscript𝑅effdisplaystyle=maxRrm int,Rrm eff. By this redefinition, the improved weak-binding relation reproduces Eq. without any contradictions because of the nonzero correction terms with Reff=|re|subscript𝑅effsubscript𝑟𝑒Rrm eff=|re|. Furthermore, thanks to Eq. , Eq. is also reproduced when the shape parameter Pssubscript𝑃𝑠Ps is unusually large. The improved relation reduces to the previous one when Reff<Rintsubscript𝑅effsubscript𝑅intRrm eff<Rrm int. Hence, when there is a large length scale in the effective range expansion, it is necessary to use the weak-binding relation with the range correction. Although we consider the range correction to the correction terms 𝒪​(Rtyp/R)𝒪subscript𝑅typ𝑅O(Rrm typ/R), one may wonder about the possibility to introduce the contributions from the effective range directly to the first term in Eq. . This is achieved only when the origin of resubscript𝑟𝑒re can be clearly traced in the relation with the compositeness X𝑋X. Unfortunately, this is not the case, as we show below using the model with Eq. . Table shows that there are two models with X=1𝑋1X=1 and X<1𝑋1X<1 with a finite resubscript𝑟𝑒re. In the effective range model, resubscript𝑟𝑒re stems from the derivative couplings. Compared with the zero-range model with X=1𝑋1X=1, the finite resubscript𝑟𝑒re arises in the effective range model without changing the value of X𝑋X. Therefore, if resubscript𝑟𝑒re comes from the derivative couplings, the value of resubscript𝑟𝑒re can be taken independently of X𝑋X. On the other hand, in the resonance model, the coupling with the discrete state induces the finite resubscript𝑟𝑒re. In this case, resubscript𝑟𝑒re is related with X𝑋X because X<1𝑋1X<1 in the resonance model. As shown in Eq. with both the derivative couplings and the coupling with the discrete state, the terms of ρ0subscript𝜌0rho0 and g0subscript𝑔0g0 cannot be separated. Therefore, the origins of resubscript𝑟𝑒re in Eq. cannot be uniquely specified. In general, we cannot determine whether X𝑋X deviates from unity by the effective range. Thus, we need to introduce the contributions from resubscript𝑟𝑒re to the correction terms.
content_sections.III Range correction III.5 Uncertainty e: The range correction is of relevance for the estimation of the uncertainty of the compositeness by the correction terms as described in Ref. citebib.bib11. The central value of the estimated compositeness Xcsubscript𝑋𝑐Xc is obtained by solving Eq. for X𝑋X by neglecting the correction terms: Xc=a0/R2−a0/R. subscript𝑋𝑐subscript𝑎0𝑅2subscript𝑎0𝑅displaystyle Xc=fraca0/R2-a0/R. To estimate the correction terms in the weak-binding relation, we define the dimensionless quantity ξ𝜉xi as followscitebib.bib11: ξ=RtypR. 𝜉subscript𝑅typ𝑅displaystylexi=fracRrm typR. To expand a0subscript𝑎0a0 in powers of ξ𝜉xi in the weak-binding relation, it should be smaller than unity. Because Rtypsubscript𝑅typRrm typ is positive from Eq. , we also have ξ≥0𝜉0xigeq 0. Regarding the magnitude of the correction terms as ξ𝜉xi, we obtain the upper (lower) bound of the compositeness Xusubscript𝑋𝑢Xu (Xlsubscript𝑋𝑙Xl): Xu​(ξ)subscript𝑋𝑢𝜉displaystyle Xu(xi) =a0/R+ξ2−a0/R−ξ,absentsubscript𝑎0𝑅𝜉2subscript𝑎0𝑅𝜉displaystyle=fraca0/R+xi2-a0/R-xi, Xl​(ξ)subscript𝑋𝑙𝜉displaystyle Xl(xi) =a0/R−ξ2−a0/R+ξ,absentsubscript𝑎0𝑅𝜉2subscript𝑎0𝑅𝜉displaystyle=fraca0/R-xi2-a0/R+xi, for 0≤ξ<10𝜉10leqxi<1. For the range correction, we need to consider both the interaction range Rintsubscript𝑅intRrm int and the length scale in the effective range expansion Reffsubscript𝑅effRrm eff. We thus define ξeffsubscript𝜉effxirm eff and ξintsubscript𝜉intxirm int: ξeff=ReffR,ξint=RintR. formulae-sequencesubscript𝜉effsubscript𝑅eff𝑅subscript𝜉intsubscript𝑅int𝑅displaystylexirm eff=fracRrm effR,quadxirm int=fracRrm intR. The uncertainty band of the compositeness X𝑋X is given by Xl​(ξ)≤X≤Xu​(ξ). subscript𝑋𝑙𝜉𝑋subscript𝑋𝑢𝜉displaystyle Xl(xi)leq Xleq Xu(xi). Now we recall that the value of the compositeness X𝑋X of a bound state is restricted within 0≤X≤10𝑋10leq Xleq 1 from the definition in Eq. . In other words, the exact value Xexactsubscript𝑋exactXrm exact cannot go beyond this region and therefore the uncertainty band outside the definition can be safely excluded. We thus define X¯u​(ξ)=min⁡Xu​(ξ),1,X¯l​(ξ)=max⁡Xl​(ξ),0,formulae-sequencesubscript¯𝑋𝑢𝜉subscript𝑋𝑢𝜉1subscript¯𝑋𝑙𝜉subscript𝑋𝑙𝜉0displaystylebarXu(xi)=minXu(xi),1,quadbarXl(xi)=maxXl(xi),0, and consider the uncertainty band consistent with the definition of the compositeness as X¯l​(ξ)≤X≤X¯u​(ξ). subscript¯𝑋𝑙𝜉𝑋subscript¯𝑋𝑢𝜉displaystylebarXl(xi)leq XleqbarXu(xi). We will use this uncertainty estimation in the following sections.
content_sections.IV Numerical calculations: To check the validity of the range correction in the weak-binding relation, we perform numerical calculations with models in Sec. , which have the finite interaction range Rintsubscript𝑅intRrm int and the effective range resubscript𝑟𝑒re. For example, in the effective range model (ρ0≠0subscript𝜌00rho0neq 0 and g0=0subscript𝑔00g0=0), we can calculate the scattering length a0subscript𝑎0a0 and the effective range resubscript𝑟𝑒re as shown in Eqs. and , and the exact value of the compositeness Xexact=1subscript𝑋exact1Xrm exact=1 is known as discussed in Sec. . Therefore, we can study the applicability of the weak-binding relations by comparing Xexactsubscript𝑋exactXrm exact and the compositeness X𝑋X estimated by the relations. In Sec. , through this comparison, we establish a condition for the valid application of the weak-binding relation. We then perform the numerical calculations to discuss the applicable parameter region by using the effective range model with Xexact=1subscript𝑋exact1Xrm exact=1 in Sec. . The resonance model with Xexact<1subscript𝑋exact1Xrm exact<1 is also examined in Sec. . We also numerically investigate the effects of the higher order terms in the effective range expansion discussed in the previous workcitebib.bib11 in Sec. .
content_sections.IV Numerical calculations IV.1 Validity: We now propose the validity condition for the applicability of the weak-binding relation. For the validity condition to be satisfied, the exact value of the compositeness Xexactsubscript𝑋exactXrm exact should be contained in the estimated uncertainty region of X𝑋X. This condition can be expressed as X¯l​(ξ)≤Xexact≤X¯u​(ξ). subscript¯𝑋𝑙𝜉subscript𝑋exactsubscript¯𝑋𝑢𝜉displaystylebarXl(xi)leq Xrm exactleqbarXu(xi). For the previous weak-binding relation to be applicable, it is necessary that the validity condition with ξ=ξint𝜉subscript𝜉intxi=xirm int is satisfied. However, the improved relation is applicable when the validity condition with ξ=ξint𝜉subscript𝜉intxi=xirm int (ξ=ξeff𝜉subscript𝜉effxi=xirm eff) is satisfied for ξint>ξeffsubscript𝜉intsubscript𝜉effxirm int>xirm eff (ξintξintsubscript𝜉effsubscript𝜉intxirm eff>xirm int. We also consider the precision of the estimated compositeness. When the uncertainty of the compositeness is too large, we cannot determine the internal structure of the states from the weak-binding relation, even if the validity condition is satisfied. To estimate the precision, we introduce E¯¯𝐸barE as the magnitude of the uncertainty of Eq. : E¯=X¯u−X¯l. ¯𝐸subscript¯𝑋𝑢subscript¯𝑋𝑙displaystylebarE=barXu-barXl. Given that 0≤X≤10𝑋10leq Xleq 1, we require E¯≤0.5¯𝐸0.5barEleq 0.5 for the meaningful estimation in addition to the validity condition. While E¯¯𝐸barE can be negative with Xl>1subscript𝑋𝑙1Xl>1 or Xu<0subscript𝑋𝑢0Xu<0, the validity condition is never satisfied in this case because of the definition of X𝑋X. We do not need to consider the precision in such a case because the weak-binding relation is not applicable.
content_sections.IV Numerical calculations IV.2 Effective: In this section, we study the applicability of the weak-binding relations in the effective range model [ρ0≠0subscript𝜌00rho0neq 0 and g0=0subscript𝑔00g0=0 in Eq. ] with a finite cutoff ΛΛLambda. This model has the bare parameters λ0subscript𝜆0lambda0 and ρ0subscript𝜌0rho0. The scattering length a0subscript𝑎0a0, the effective range resubscript𝑟𝑒re, and the radius of the bound state R𝑅R are calculated from λ0subscript𝜆0lambda0, ρ0subscript𝜌0rho0, and ΛΛLambda as shown in Sec. . The exact value of the compositeness is known as Xexact=1subscript𝑋exact1Xrm exact=1 (see Sec. ). In this model, ξeffsubscript𝜉effxirm eff and ξintsubscript𝜉intxirm int are ξeffsubscript𝜉effdisplaystylexirm eff =ReffR=|re|R,absentsubscript𝑅eff𝑅subscript𝑟𝑒𝑅displaystyle=fracRrm effR=frac|re|R, ξintsubscript𝜉intdisplaystylexirm int =RintR=1Λ​R,absentsubscript𝑅int𝑅1Λ𝑅displaystyle=fracRrm intR=frac1Lambda R, where we note that Rintsubscript𝑅intRrm int is estimated by the inverse of the cutoff ΛΛLambda. By varying the bare parameters λ0subscript𝜆0lambda0, ρ0subscript𝜌0rho0, and the cutoff ΛΛLambda, we can arbitrarily choose the effective range resubscript𝑟𝑒re and the radius of the bound state R𝑅R. With these resubscript𝑟𝑒re and R𝑅R, the quantities ξeffsubscript𝜉effxirm eff and ξintsubscript𝜉intxirm int are obtained from Eqs. and . Therefore, we can examine the validity conditions by ξeffsubscript𝜉effxirm eff and ξintsubscript𝜉intxirm int separately. For the convenience of numerical calculations, we introduce the dimensionless quantities. The dimensionless resubscript𝑟𝑒re, ρ0subscript𝜌0rho0, and ΛΛLambda in units of a0subscript𝑎0a0 and m𝑚m are given by resubscript𝑟𝑒displaystyletildere =rea0,absentsubscript𝑟𝑒subscript𝑎0displaystyle=fracrea0, ρ0subscript𝜌0displaystyletilderho0 =m​ρ0a03,absent𝑚subscript𝜌0superscriptsubscript𝑎03displaystyle=fracmrho0a03, ΛΛdisplaystyletildeLambda =a0​Λ. absentsubscript𝑎0Λdisplaystyle=a0Lambda.
