Eq. ]. This model-independent analysis provides a consistent picture, where values of Z𝑍Z greater than γbexp/mπ∼0.3similar-tosuperscriptsubscript𝛾𝑏expsubscript𝑚𝜋0.3gammabrm exp/mpisim 0.3 are very implausible, which confirms the dominant molecular structure of the deuteron citebib.bib1.
content_sections.IV The 𝑫𝒔​𝟎∗​(𝟐𝟑𝟏𝟕)±subscriptsuperscript: This scalar narrow resonance (Γ<3.8Γ3.8Gamma<3.8 MeV), which lies 45​MeV45MeV45,textMeV below the D​K𝐷𝐾DK threshold and has valence-quark content c​s¯𝑐¯𝑠cbars, was discovered in 2003 by the BaBar Collaboration citebib.bib19. Its abnormally light mass cannot be easily accommodated within constituent quark modelscitebib.bib166, bib.bib167, bib.bib110, bib.bib111, bib.bib113, and as a consequence, it is common to describe this exotic resonance as the result of the S-wave D​K𝐷𝐾DK interactioncitebib.bib35, bib.bib37, bib.bib168, bib.bib50, bib.bib169, bib.bib170, bib.bib171, bib.bib172, bib.bib73, bib.bib173. Unitarized heavy-meson chiral approaches predict that the Ds​0∗​±subscriptsuperscript𝐷∗𝑠0superscript2317plus-or-minusDasts0pm would belong to a light-flavor SU anti-triplet, completed by an isospin doublet associated to the scalar D0∗​subscriptsuperscript𝐷02300D*0 resonancecitebib.bib57, bib.bib59. Here, we will discuss, within the scheme outlined above, the D​K𝐷𝐾DK molecular probability of the Ds​0∗​±subscriptsuperscript𝐷∗𝑠0superscript2317plus-or-minusDasts0pm, neglecting the isospin-violating Ds​πsubscript𝐷𝑠𝜋Dspi decay channel. Given the lack of D​K𝐷𝐾DK scattering data, we take the values of the isoscalar S-wave D​K𝐷𝐾DK scattering length and effective range obtained in Ref. citebib.bib171 from the finite volume QCD levels reported in Refs. citebib.bib169, bib.bib170. Namely, we use aexp=−1.3​​fmsubscript𝑎exp1.35fmarm exp.3,textfm and rexp=−0.1​​fmsubscript𝑟exp0.13fmrrm exp=-0.1,textfm. In addition, we take Ebexp=−45​​MeVsuperscriptsubscript𝐸𝑏exp454MeVEbrm exp=-45,textMeV, estimated from the experimental masses compiled in the PDG citebib.bib7, which leads to γbexp=0.95​​fm−1superscriptsubscript𝛾𝑏exp0.954superscriptfm1gammabrm exp=0.95,textfm-1 when isospin averaged masses are used for the kaon and D𝐷D mesons. Due to the large uncertainties affecting both aexpsubscript𝑎exparm exp and rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp, it is not necessary to take into account the subtleties associated with the D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+–D+​K0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0D+K0 isospin breaking effects. The scale β𝛽beta is in this case of the order of 300​MeV300MeV300,textMeV, estimated from the expected effects induced by the nearest Ds​ηsubscript𝐷𝑠𝜂Dseta threshold citebib.bib137 and/or by the two-pion exchange interaction, none of which are explicitly treated in the ERE. Hence, we expect the accuracy of the NLO γb−limit-fromsubscript𝛾𝑏gammab-expansion to be of the order of (γb2/β2)∼40%similar-tosuperscriptsubscript𝛾𝑏2superscript𝛽2percent40(gammab2/beta2)sim 40%, which we adopt for Δ​γbexpΔsubscriptsuperscript𝛾exp𝑏Deltagammarm expb, while for Δ​aexpΔsubscript𝑎expDelta arm exp and Δ​rexpΔsubscript𝑟expDelta rrm exp, we use the errors quoted above. The compositeness 2D distribution of Eq. for the Ds​0∗​±subscriptsuperscript𝐷∗𝑠0superscript2317plus-or-minusDasts0pm is shown in Fig. . The results favor D​K𝐷𝐾DK molecular probabilities of at least 50%, which is in agreement with previous calculationscitebib.bib50, bib.bib171, bib.bib174, bib.bib73, bib.bib173, bib.bib137, bib.bib140. We cannot be as predictive in this case as for the deuteron, not only because of the bigger uncertainties of the input, but also because of the larger size of the power-counting parameter γb/β∼0.6similar-tosubscript𝛾𝑏𝛽0.6gammab/betasim 0.6. Nevertheless, the approach is still consistent since values of Z𝑍Z of order 𝒪​(γb/β)𝒪subscript𝛾𝑏𝛽cal Oleft(gammab/betaright), or smaller, are favored by the compositeness distribution for this state. We however should note that the binding energy of the Ds​0∗​±subscriptsuperscript𝐷∗𝑠0superscript2317plus-or-minusDasts0pm is significantly higher than that of deuteron, and the NLO approach employed here is at the limit of its applicability. To be quantitatively more precise, it would be necessary to know more details about the dynamics at short distances of this exotic state than those encoded in the first two parameters of the ERE.
content_sections.V The 𝐒𝟎𝟏superscriptsubscript𝐒01{}^{1}{\: The p​n𝑝𝑛pn scattering length and effective range determined in Ref. citebib.bib165 for this isovector partial wave are aexp=23.735​​fmsubscript𝑎exp23.73516fmarm exp=23.735 textfm and rexp=2.68​​fmsubscript𝑟exp2.683fmrrm exp=2.68 textfm, respectively, with the total uncertainties obtained by adding statistical and systematic errors in quadrature. This partial wave has a shallow virtual state (pole on the real energy-axis below the threshold on the unphysical sheet), the position of which is determined by the condition cot⁡δ​(−i​γv)=+i𝛿𝑖subscript𝛾𝑣𝑖cotdelta(-igammav)=+i, with γv=2​μ​|Ev|subscript𝛾𝑣2𝜇subscript𝐸𝑣gammav=sqrt2mu|Ev| and Ev0.2𝑍0.2Z>0.2, the 2D function ℒ​(Z,δ​r)ℒ𝑍𝛿𝑟cal L(Z,delta r) takes values larger than 2, while its minimum values are found for Z<0.1𝑍0.1Z<0.1. This is consistent with the general understanding that virtual states are the result of two-particle interactions, and in this sense can be considered of molecular typecitebib.bib137.
content_sections.VI An estimation of 𝑻𝒄​𝒄+superscriptsubs: The Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+ state has been recently discovered by the LHCb collaboration citebib.bib17, bib.bib18 as a prominent peak in the D0​D0​π+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐷0superscript𝜋D0D0pi+ spectrum. It is very close to the D∗+​D0superscript𝐷∗absentsuperscript𝐷0Dast+D0 threshold, since the experimental analysis throw Δ​MTc​c+=MTc​c+−mD∗+−mD0=−273​​keVΔsubscript𝑀superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐subscript𝑀superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐subscript𝑚superscript𝐷∗absentsubscript𝑚superscript𝐷027361keVDelta MTcc+=MTcc+-mDast+-mD0=-273,textkeVcitebib.bib17 or Δ​MTc​c+=−360​​keVΔsubscript𝑀superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐36040keVDelta MTcc+=-360,textkeVcitebib.bib18.4Here we do not discuss the width, the importance of which has been highlighted in the literature, e. g. in Refs. citebib.bib175, bib.bib176, bib.bib177, bib.bib178, bib.bib179. However, the D∗0​D+superscript𝐷∗absent0superscript𝐷Dast 0D+ threshold is only 1.4​MeV1.4MeV1.4,textMeV above, and therefore one must consider coupled channels in order to have a fully accurate description of the state. In this situation one cannot straightforwardly apply Weinberg’s compositeness criteria, which requires the coupling of the bound state to a single channel. The approach should be modified in the presence of close coupled channels that play an important role on the long-distance dynamics of the state, and as a consequence, they significantly modify the effective range parametercitebib.bib137. In Ref. citebib.bib180, it is shown for the χc​1​subscript𝜒𝑐13872chic1 and Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+ exotic states that the appearance of a large and negative effective range, which in the one-channel case would indicate the dominance of a compact componentcitebib.bib1, can be naturally generated by the coupled-channel dynamics. Hence, in presence of coupled channels, and before analyzing the estimator of the compositeness proposed in this work, it would be necessary to correct the effective range by a termcitebib.bib137, bib.bib180 that stems from coupled-channel effects, and which clearly needs to be attributed to the molecular component of the state. Other efforts beyond the one just mentioned have been devoted to extend the compositeness condition to coupled channels (see references in Sec. ). However, in this work we adopt a different perspective and for illustrative purposes, we have qualitatively applied our generalization of Weinberg compositeness condition to the Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+ state within a simplified scenario. We have reduced the coupled-channel problem to a single-channel one by considering the model of Ref. citebib.bib76 in the exact isospin limit, which we briefly outline below. The latter work performs an S-wave D∗+​D0superscript𝐷∗absentsuperscript𝐷0Dast+D0, D∗0​D+superscript𝐷∗absent0superscript𝐷Dast 0D+ coupled-channel analysis in terms of two coupling constants C0,1subscript𝐶01C0,1 and an ultraviolet cutoff ΛΛLambda. If one takes common masses for the D(∗)superscript𝐷∗D(ast) mesons, mD(∗)=(mD(∗)++mD(∗)​0)/2subscript𝑚superscript𝐷∗subscript𝑚superscript𝐷limit-from∗subscript𝑚superscript𝐷∗02mD(ast)=(mD(ast)++mD(ast)0)/2, then the two-channel problem diagonalizes and one ends up with two independent amplitudes for each of the definite-isospin (I=0𝐼0I=0 and I=1𝐼1I=1) sectors. Taking this limit, and using the values for the constant C0subscript𝐶0C0 fitted in Ref. citebib.bib76, we obtain aph=−5.38​​fmsubscript𝑎ph5.3830fmatextph=-5.38,textfm and rph=0.95​​fmsubscript𝑟ph0.9532fmrtextph=0.95,textfm for the isoscalar5We assume that the Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+ state is mostly an isoscalar state, as suggested by additional experimental information besides the D0​D0​π+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐷0superscript𝜋D0D0pi+ spectrum, see Refs. citebib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib76. scattering length and effective range, respectively. The central values account for the averages of Eqs. (3.9a) and(3.9b) in Ref. citebib.bib76, computed for two different values of the cutoff Λ=0.5​GeVΛ0.5GeVLambda=0.5,textGeV and 1.0​GeV1.0GeV1.0,textGeV. The quoted errors represent the addition in quadrature of the statistical error and half of the dispersion between both determinations. In this isospin limit, the Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+ binding energy (respect to the average threshold) increases, and we find Δ​MTc​c+ph=−833​​keVΔsuperscriptsubscript𝑀superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐ph83367keVDelta MTcc+textph=-833,textkeV and −856​​keV85653keV-856,textkeV for Λ=0.5​GeVΛ0.5GeVLambda=0.5,textGeV and Λ=1.0​GeVΛ1.0GeVLambda=1.0,textGeV, respectively, which lead to a binding momentum γbph=40.2​(1.7)​MeVsuperscriptsubscript𝛾𝑏ph40.21.7MeVgammabtextph=40.2(1.7),textMeV. Taking the scale β≃mπsimilar-to-or-equals𝛽subscript𝑚𝜋betasimeq mpi, one obtains (γbph/β)2≃8%similar-to-or-equalssuperscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝛾𝑏ph𝛽2percent8(gammabtextph/beta)2simeq 8%, which is of the order of the uncertainty in the scattering length and smaller than that of the effective range. For the Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+ and contrary to the case of the deuteron, we are not determining the binding momentum, the scattering length and the effective range from an experimental analysis, but instead from a phenomenological model citebib.bib76. For this reason, we have denoted these quantities as γbph,aphsuperscriptsubscript𝛾𝑏phsubscript𝑎phgammabtextph,atextph and rphsubscript𝑟phrtextph, respectively. It is worth pointing out that two recent LQCD simulations citebib.bib181, bib.bib182 have also computed the scattering length and effective range for S𝑆S-wave I=0𝐼0I=0 D​D¯∗𝐷superscript¯𝐷∗DbarDast scattering, and have found a value r0≃1​fmsimilar-to-or-equalssubscript𝑟01fmr0simeq 1,textfm, similar to the one obtained in Ref. citebib.bib76, to be used in our work. We also note that, while here rphsubscript𝑟phrtextph is small and positive, the experimental value for the effective range obtained in Ref. citebib.bib18 is large and negative. However, in the latter work, the negative value is a built-in property of the model used in the analysis. More importantly, it is obtained in a coupled channel scheme, whereas rphsubscript𝑟phrtextph obtained here refers to the single channel case in the exact isospin limit. Therefore, the comparison between the two values is not meaningful. Further discussions on this subject can be found in Refs. citebib.bib137, bib.bib71, bib.bib180. Nevertheless, we should mention that the results of Ref. citebib.bib76 are consistent with those found in the state of the art work of Ref. citebib.bib71, where i) the D∗+​D0superscript𝐷∗absentsuperscript𝐷0Dast+D0, D∗0​D+superscript𝐷∗absent0superscript𝐷Dast 0D+ coupled-channel dynamics, ii) long-range interactions provided by the one pion exchange mechanism and iii) effects from the three-body D​D​π𝐷𝐷𝜋DDpi thresholds, which lie very close to and below the two-body D∗​Dsuperscript𝐷∗𝐷DastD ones, are accurately taken into account. Indeed, the parameters of the effective range expansion from the low-energy scattering amplitude are also reliably extracted in Ref. citebib.bib71, and it turns out that both scattering length and effective range are compatible, within uncertainties, with aphsubscript𝑎phatextph and rphsubscript𝑟phrtextph used here, and obtained from the analysis of Ref. citebib.bib76. This is so once the the largest contribution to the effective range, originated from isospin breaking related to the D(∗)superscript𝐷D(*)-meson mass differences, that is, from the coupling of D∗+​D0superscript𝐷∗absentsuperscript𝐷0Dast+D0 to the slightly higher D∗0​D+superscript𝐷∗absent0superscript𝐷Dast 0D+ channel is discounted6Such correction is so large that it leads to a positive residual finite range (≈rph∼1absentsubscript𝑟phsimilar-to1approx rtextphsim 1 fm), which is the quantity entering in the Weinberg’s relations. (see Table IV and Eq. of Ref. citebib.bib71), as mentioned in the beginning of the section. Therefore, though the results shown below for the compositeness of the Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+, obtained within the model of Ref. in the isospin limit, should be considered as only qualitative ones, they might be sufficiently realistic to illustrate the performance of the estimator of the compositeness proposed in this work. Bearing all these caveats in mind, we have computed the distribution of Eq. , using the numerical values discussed above for aphsubscript𝑎phatextph, rphsubscript𝑟phrtextph and γbphsuperscriptsubscript𝛾𝑏phgammabtextph. The results are shown in Fig. , similarly as done for the deuteron in Fig. . As can be seen in the leftmost panel, ℒ​(Z,δ​r)<0.5ℒ𝑍𝛿𝑟0.5L(Z,delta r)<0.5 for Z<0.2𝑍0.2Z<0.2 with γbph/β∼0.3similar-tosuperscriptsubscript𝛾𝑏ph𝛽0.3gammabtextph/betasim 0.3, and the minimum of ℒ​(Z,δ​r)ℒ𝑍𝛿𝑟L(Z,delta r) is quite compatible with Z=0𝑍0Z=0. Indeed in the middle panel, for δ​r=0.95​fm(=rph)𝛿𝑟annotated0.95fmabsentsubscript𝑟phdelta r=0.95,textfm(=rtextph) such that rNLO​(Z=0,δ​r=0.95​fm)=rphsubscript𝑟NLOformulae-sequence𝑍0𝛿𝑟0.95fmsubscript𝑟phrtextNLO(Z=0,delta r=0.95,textfm)=rtextph, it can be seen that the scattering length aNLOsubscript𝑎NLOatextNLO coincides with aphsubscript𝑎phatextph at Z=0𝑍0Z=0. This analysis supports a molecular picture for the Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+ state, as previous works have also concluded citebib.bib75, bib.bib71, and in particular the model of Ref. citebib.bib76, whose predictions for the binding momentum, scattering length and effective range have been used here.
content_sections.VII Summary and discussion: We have discussed a model-independent estimator of the likelihood of the compositeness of a shallow S-wave bound or virtual state. It relies only on the proximity of the energy of the state to the two-hadron threshold to which it significantly couples and on the experimental scattering length and effective range low energy parameters. The approach is based on NLO Weinberg’s relations and it is self-consistent as long as the obtained Z𝑍Z is small of the order of 𝒪​(γb/β)𝒪subscript𝛾𝑏𝛽cal O(gammab/beta). To systematically study systems where Z𝑍Z could take larger values, the order 𝒪​(k4)𝒪superscript𝑘4cal O(k4) or higher terms in the ERE would be required. This is because in those cases, it would be necessary to include additional details to further constrain the short-range structure of the wave-function. We have analysed the case of the deuteron (Sec. ), the exotic Ds​0∗​±subscriptsuperscript𝐷∗𝑠0superscript2317plus-or-minusDasts0pm resonance (Sec. ), and the S01superscriptsubscriptS011rm S0 nucleon-nucleon virtual state (Sec. ), and found strong support to the molecular interpretation in all cases. Nevertheless, results are less conclusive for the Ds​0∗​±subscriptsuperscript𝐷∗𝑠0superscript2317plus-or-minusDasts0pm due to the large size of the power-counting parameter γb/β∼0.6similar-tosubscript𝛾𝑏𝛽0.6gammab/betasim 0.6 and therefore the NLO approach employed here is at the limit of its applicability. As discussed in Sec. , the Weinberg compositeness criteria as well as the extension presented here are only valid for the case of a single channel, and the approach needs to be modified when coupled channels are present citebib.bib137, bib.bib180. To avoid this problem, in Sec. we have discussed the case of the Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+, by reducing the coupled-channel (D∗+​D0superscript𝐷∗absentsuperscript𝐷0Dast+D0-D∗0​D+superscript𝐷∗absent0superscript𝐷Dast 0D+) problem to a single-channel one in the isospin limit using the phenomenological model of Ref. citebib.bib76. Applying the generalization of Weinberg’s compositeness condition proposed in the present work, we have shown that the parameters are compatible with a molecular interpretation of the Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+ state. These results are, however, only qualitative and for illustrating purposes, since we have not considered the complexity of the very close thresholds D0​D∗+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐷∗absentD0Dast+ and D+​D∗0superscript𝐷superscript𝐷∗absent0D+Dast 0, which could make more difficult to appreciate the main ingredients of the approach (expansion) derived in this paper.

all_papers.title:
A Possible Solution to the Difficulty in the Interpretation of Deuteron
  Compositeness
all_papers.abstract:
We study the theoretical structure of compositeness with explicit energy
dependence, and find a possible explanation for the difficulty in the
interpretation of compositeness of deuteron. Compositeness of deuteron is
calculated as larger than one in many methods like weak-binding limit. Even
though it is widely assumed that the energy dependence in interaction always
comes from other states, which we call surjective interpretation, we find that
the outcome of deuteron may suggest a violation of surjective interpretation.
We directly perform numerical and perturbative calculations of deuteron
compositeness. It is concluded that if the energy dependent part of interaction
contributes to attraction, compositeness is likely to be enhanced from unity.
We discuss the indications of this outcome and the model dependence of
compositeness. We propose a straightforward extension and a thorough revise on
the formalism of compositeness with field theory considerations.

sections.2: Hyodo, What we know about the Λ(1405), AIP Conference Proceedings 1735, 020012 (2016).
sections.5: Weinberg, Elementary particle theory of composite particles, Physical Review 130, 776 (1963).
sections.7: Oller, New results from a number operator interpretation of the compositeness of bound and resonant states, Annals of Physics 396, 429 (2018).
sections.18: Weinberg, Elementary particle theory of composite particles, Physical Review 130, 776 (1963).
sections.19: Weinberg, Evidence that the deuteron is not an elementary particle, Physical Review 137, B672 (1965).

qualitative: Compositeness is a measure of the proportion of a hadronic state that is composed of more elementary constituents, such as nucleons in the case of the deuteron.
The deuteron is expected to be predominantly composite, with compositeness close to unity but not exceeding it.

Current formalisms for compositeness, particularly in the weak-binding limit, yield compositeness values greater than one, implying negative elementariness, which is unphysical since proportions should be non-negative.
The standard interpretation (surjective interpretation) assumes that energy dependence in the interaction arises from contributions of other states or scattering channels.

Violation of the surjective interpretation may be indicated by the unphysical results in deuteron compositeness calculations, suggesting that energy-dependent parts of the interaction might directly contribute to attraction rather than being solely due to other states.

The formalism of compositeness based on the Lippmann-Schwinger equation and Schrödinger equation can be extended to include energy-dependent potentials, but this extension challenges the conventional surjective interpretation.
A negative elementariness arises when the binding energy exceeds the value predicted by the scattering length, indicating a breakdown of the standard formalism under certain conditions.
The model dependence of compositeness is significant, and the current formalism may not be robust without proper field-theoretic foundations.
A thorough revision of the compositeness formalism, incorporating field theory considerations, is proposed to resolve inconsistencies and ensure physical consistency.
quantitative: Compositeness > 1 (in many methods including weak-binding limit), leading to negative elementariness.
Elementariness = 1 - Compositeness; thus, compositeness > 1 implies elementariness < 0.
The weak-binding limit recovers the original S. Weinberg result, which yields compositeness > 1 for the deuteron.
Binding energy higher than that predicted by the scattering length leads to unphysical negative elementariness.
The formalism of T. Sekihara et al. extends compositeness to energy-dependent interactions via the Lippmann-Schwinger equation, assuming surjective interpretation.
No specific numerical value for deuteron compositeness is provided in the paper, but the result is consistently greater than one across methods.
The paper does not report new numerical values from lattice QCD or perturbative calculations, but emphasizes that such calculations support the need for formalism revision.

all_papers.reason:
论文紧扣“氘核复合度”与“束缚态组分分析”核心，明确探讨德布罗意复合度的理论结构及能量依赖性，直接回应Weinberg复合度准则的解释难题。其数值与微扰计算、场论修正等方法均与查询要求高度契合。

约束项检查：  
- C1：研究对象为氘核复合度 — 满足。证据：“We study the theoretical structure of compositeness with explicit energy dependence” 对比 “Weinberg composite degree criterion”  
- C2：涉及束缚态组分分析 — 满足。证据：“compositeness of deuteron is calculated” 对比 “bound state component analysis”  
- C3：关注复合度大于1的解释困境 — 满足。证据：“compositeness is likely to be enhanced from unity” 对比 “difficulty in interpretation”  

不相关性 / 风险点 / 缺口：  
- 无明显偏离。论文虽未显式引用Weinberg，但其理论框架与核心问题完全一致。
authors: Zanpeng Yin, Daisuke Jido

content_sections.I Introduction: Hadrons are composed of quarks and gluons which are governed by Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD). Normal hadrons are made up by a pair of a quark and an antiquark like mesons or three quarks like baryons, and these hadrons form the basis of low energy effective theory for hadrons. However, some outliers exist outside of such categories, and they are named exotic hadrons. Additionally, while more hadronic composite states are allowed in QCD, we can only observe several of such states, which makes the research of exotic hadrons more important. Some currently observed exotic hadrons include ΛΛLambdacitebib.bib1, Xcitebib.bib2, Ycitebib.bib3, etc. The structure of exotic hadrons are proposed to be elementary or composite. From the aspect of hadron physics, elementary states are particle states that cannot be expressed by other hadrons, and composite states are composed by other hadrons with a larger size than normal hadrons. However, quark radial excitation, quark pair creation, and meson creation are all on similar energy scales, so these excitation modes may compete with each other in hadron structure. A hadron state may, as a result, be a mixture among those states. To better understand the hadron structure, separating these contributions from different origins is important. Compositeness is a quantity proposed by S. Weinberg in Refs. citebib.bib4, bib.bib5 to distinguish whether deuteron is constructed by nucleons or a distinct elemental particle composed of, for example, six quarks. Compositeness is defined by the proportion of a state composed of other more elementary particles, in this case the proportion of deuteron being composed of nucleons. This procedure can be seen as a mixture of elementary states and composite states to a physical state. S. Weinberg formalized his calculation based on nucleon scattering in the low energy regime. With current experimental values, this method resulted in a compositeness larger than unity, which means a negative elementariness. However, it is not a reasonable outcome because elementariness, same as compositeness, is defined to be a proportion, and a proportion should not be negative. In last century, works are done on the properties and calculations of compositeness mostly in a Quantum Field Theory (QFT) scheme citebib.bib6, bib.bib7, and a modern QFT version of compositeness is given in Ref. citebib.bib8. Recently, compositeness has been developed by T. Sekihara et al. and utilized in the description of various particles citebib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12. They have derived the formulae of compositeness and elementariness based on Schrödinger equation and extended them for quantities obtained in Lippmann-Schwinger scattering equation by interpreting that any energy dependence in the interaction between hadrons as outcomes of elementary states and other scattering channels. This extension is practically important because one is able to use scattering experimental data to fix the hadronic interaction and evaluate compositeness and elementariness based on experimental observables. By taking weak-binding limit, the calculation of S. Weinberg can be recovered. The idea of weak-binding limit has been developed in Refs. citebib.bib10, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16. The importance of weak-binding limit lies in the fact that this method is almost model independent, i. e. compositeness can be calculated from physical observables only. Various theoretical works on the interpretation and calculation methods of compositeness are performed in citebib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib10, bib.bib21. On the other hand, model calculation without using weak-binding limit has been performed in Refs. citebib.bib9, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24. Numerical calculation of compositeness utilizing Lattice QCD calculation has been carried out in citebib.bib25, bib.bib26 Despite the wide discussion and usage of compositeness, few has been done to confirm its soundness. The compositeness of deuteron is still larger than one, or with negative elementariness, in the current formalism. On the other hand, we expect that deuteron is at least almost composite, which means compositeness is almost unity but smaller. In this paper, we would aim at filling the gap between calculation and expectation by testing the soundness of compositeness as a physical quantity from a hadron physics point of view . In Section , we will first review the modern formalism of T. Sekihara et. al. with generalization into energy dependent potential, and the positivity of elementariness is derived from a theoretical viewpoint. Under surjective interpretation, where the energy dependent part of the interaction is always interpreted to come from other states, T. Sekihara’s formalism can be recovered. On the other hand in Section , we find that binding energy higher than that indicated by the scattering length is likely to result in an unphysical negative elementariness. In Section , we discuss the reason and indication of this difficulty and proposed some alternative approaches for the phenomenological calculation of compositeness. Section is devoted to conclusion of this work.
content_sections.II Model and Formalism: In this section, we review the formalism of compositeness with development in energy dependent potential. To do this, we first define the model space and compositeness in a theoretical point of view. Then we demonstrate the calculation of compositeness from Schrodinger equation which can be connected to the scattering theory with surjective interpretation which will be defined later citebib.bib9.
content_sections.II Model and Formalism II.1 Model and De: Let us consider a scenario which is composed by scattering states in several channels |qj⟩ketsubscript𝑞𝑗ketqj and a number of elementary states |ψa⟩ketsubscript𝜓𝑎ketpsia. Each scattering state |qj⟩ketsubscript𝑞𝑗ketqj is composed of two free particles with mass mjsubscript𝑚𝑗mj and Mjsubscript𝑀𝑗Mj propagating with momentum qjsubscript𝑞𝑗qj in the center-of-mass(CM) frame. We use the subscript j𝑗j to indicate different channels. The elementary states are one-body states and cannot be described by the interaction of the scattering states. The full Hamiltonian 𝑯𝑯H for this system will be composed by two parts, 𝑯=𝑯0+𝑽𝑯subscript𝑯0𝑽H=H0+V where 𝑯0subscript𝑯0H0 is the Hamiltonian when no interaction exists and 𝑽𝑽V is the interaction between eigenstates of this free system, which may depend on the energy of the system. We will first define the eigenstates in the free system as 𝑯0​|qj⟩subscript𝑯0ketsubscript𝑞𝑗displaystyleH0ketqj =Eqj​|qj⟩,absentsubscript𝐸subscript𝑞𝑗ketsubscript𝑞𝑗displaystyle=Eqjketqj, 𝑯0​|ψa⟩subscript𝑯0ketsubscript𝜓𝑎displaystyleH0ketpsia =MBa​|ψa⟩,absentsubscript𝑀subscript𝐵𝑎ketsubscript𝜓𝑎displaystyle=MBaketpsia, where Eqjsubscript𝐸subscript𝑞𝑗displaystyle Eqj =Mt​h,j+qj22​μj,absentsubscript𝑀𝑡ℎ𝑗superscriptsubscript𝑞𝑗22subscript𝜇𝑗displaystyle=Mth,j+fracqj22muj, with the threshold energy Mt​h,j=mj+Mjsubscript𝑀𝑡ℎ𝑗subscript𝑚𝑗subscript𝑀𝑗Mth,j=mj+Mj and the reduced mass μ=mj​Mjmj+Mj𝜇subscript𝑚𝑗subscript𝑀𝑗subscript𝑚𝑗subscript𝑀𝑗mu=fracmjMjmj+Mj. We also explicitly define one body elementary states |ψa⟩ketsubscript𝜓𝑎ketpsia with no requirement on the origin of these states. The normalization writes as follows, ⟨q′j|qk⟩inner-productsubscriptsuperscript𝑞′𝑗subscript𝑞𝑘displaystylebraketqprimejqk =(2​π)3​δ​(q′−q)​δj​k,absentsuperscript2𝜋3𝛿superscript𝑞′𝑞subscript𝛿𝑗𝑘displaystyle=(2pi)3delta(qprime-q)deltajk, ⟨ψa|ψb⟩inner-productsubscript𝜓𝑎subscript𝜓𝑏displaystylebraketpsiapsib =δa​b,absentsubscript𝛿𝑎𝑏displaystyle=deltaab, ⟨ψa|qk⟩inner-productsubscript𝜓𝑎subscript𝑞𝑘displaystylebraketpsiaqk =0. absent0displaystyle=0. We assume that these states form a complete orthonormal basis, which we call model space, as 1=∑a|ψa⟩​⟨ψa|+∑j∫d3​q(2​π)3​|qj⟩​⟨qj|.1subscript𝑎ketsubscript𝜓𝑎brasubscript𝜓𝑎subscript𝑗superscript𝑑3𝑞superscript2𝜋3ketsubscript𝑞𝑗brasubscript𝑞𝑗1=sumaketpsiabrapsia+sumjintdfracd3q(2pi)3ketqjbraqj. By including the interaction term 𝑽𝑽V, it generates a physical bound state |ψ⟩ket𝜓ketpsi with the eigenenergy of the full Hamiltonian 𝑯𝑯H or mass MBsubscript𝑀𝐵MB as 𝑯​|ψ⟩=MB​|ψ⟩,𝑯ket𝜓subscript𝑀𝐵ket𝜓Hketpsi=MBketpsi, where we normalize the bound state |ψ⟩ket𝜓ketpsi as unity: ⟨ψ|ψ⟩=1. inner-product𝜓𝜓1braketpsipsi=1. From here, we define compositeness X𝑋X and elementariness Z𝑍Z as Xjsubscript𝑋𝑗displaystyle Xj =∫d3​qj(2​π)3​⟨ψ|qj⟩​⟨qj|ψ⟩,absentsuperscript𝑑3subscript𝑞𝑗superscript2𝜋3inner-product𝜓subscript𝑞𝑗inner-productsubscript𝑞𝑗𝜓displaystyle=intdfracd3qj(2pi)3braketpsiqjbraketqjpsi, Zasubscript𝑍𝑎displaystyle Za =⟨ψ|ψa⟩​⟨ψa|ψ⟩. absentinner-product𝜓subscript𝜓𝑎inner-productsubscript𝜓𝑎𝜓displaystyle=braketpsipsiabraketpsiapsi. By defining the wave function of the bound state as ψj​(q)=⟨qj|ψ⟩subscript𝜓𝑗𝑞inner-productsubscript𝑞𝑗𝜓tildepsij(q)=braketqjpsi in momentum space, the compositeness can also be written as Xj=∫d3​qj(2​π)3​ψj∗​(q)​ψj​(q). subscript𝑋𝑗superscript𝑑3subscript𝑞𝑗superscript2𝜋3superscriptsubscript𝜓𝑗𝑞subscript𝜓𝑗𝑞Xj=intdfracd3qj(2pi)3tildepsij*(q)tildepsij(q). The compositeness represents the proportion of the physical state composed by a scattering channel, and elementariness is the proportion of that composed by the elementary state. From the assumption of the complete Hilbert space in Eq. , and the definition of the normalization factor in Eq. , we can achieve a normalization of elementariness and compositeness as 1=∑aZa+∑jXj,1subscript𝑎subscript𝑍𝑎subscript𝑗subscript𝑋𝑗1=sumaZa+sumjXj, which is in accordance with our intuition that the sum of all components ought to go to one. For later convenience, we assume a separable form in the momentum space: ⟨qj|𝑽|q′k⟩quantum-operator-productsubscript𝑞𝑗𝑽subscriptsuperscript𝑞′𝑘displaystylebraketqjVqprimek =vj​k​(E)​fj​(q2)​fk​(q′⁣2)absentsubscript𝑣𝑗𝑘𝐸subscript𝑓𝑗superscript𝑞2subscript𝑓𝑘superscript𝑞′2displaystyle=vjk(E)fj(q2)fk(qprime 2) ⟨ψa|𝑽|q′j⟩quantum-operator-productsubscript𝜓𝑎𝑽subscriptsuperscript𝑞′𝑗displaystylebraketpsiaVqprimej =g​a,j​fj​(q′⁣2)absentsubscript𝑔0𝑎𝑗subscript𝑓𝑗superscript𝑞′2displaystyle=ga,jfj(qprime 2) ⟨ψa|𝑽|ψb⟩quantum-operator-productsubscript𝜓𝑎𝑽subscript𝜓𝑏displaystylebraketpsiaVpsib =0,absent0displaystyle=0, in which f​(q2)𝑓superscript𝑞2f(q2) is a form factor, and coupling g​a,jsubscript𝑔0𝑎𝑗ga,j is assumed to be constant. We can choose an appropriate phase so that vj​ksubscript𝑣𝑗𝑘vjk and g​a,jsubscript𝑔0𝑎𝑗ga,j are real. We assume that the interaction vj​ksubscript𝑣𝑗𝑘vjk may depend on an external energy E𝐸E, which we do not specify here. We can find that the energy of intermediate states are not in the potential vj​ksubscript𝑣𝑗𝑘vjk. This is because the interaction vj​ksubscript𝑣𝑗𝑘vjk is already complete even without a dependency on Eq′subscript𝐸superscript𝑞′Eqprime as long as we absorb any of the energy dependence in form factor fk​(q′⁣2)subscript𝑓𝑘superscript𝑞′2fk(qprime 2), and the energy of the state |q⟩ket𝑞ketq is fixed in Eq. . We would like to note that the word energy-dependent may be unnatural in some research areas, as the full Hamiltonian should not have any energy dependence. The usage of the word here is indicating velocity dependence in nuclear physics. In Eq. , there is E𝐸E that is written explicitly, but due to the fact that E𝐸E is the energy of the system, it is not an independent variable but dependent on q𝑞q. The full form of potential V𝑉V can be written as a functional of q𝑞q and q′superscript𝑞′qprime as 𝑽​(q2,q′⁣2,E​(q2))𝑽superscript𝑞2superscript𝑞′2𝐸superscript𝑞2V(q2,qprime 2,E(q2)), that E𝐸E is separated to indicate the energy dependence unable to be absorbed in the form factor.
content_sections.II Model and Formalism II.2 Compositenes: In this section, we will mostly follow the formalism developed in Ref. citebib.bib9 and pay special attention to energy dependent potentials. We will first discuss the compositeness of a bound state in single channel case. We assume one scattering channel |q⟩ket𝑞ketq and one elementary state |ψ0⟩ketsubscript𝜓0ketpsi0 in the free theory. The wave function ψ​(q)=⟨q|ψ⟩𝜓𝑞inner-product𝑞𝜓tildepsi(q)=braketqpsi for the bound state |ψ⟩ket𝜓ketpsi is represented by the coupled-channel Schrodinger equations ⟨q|𝑯|ψ⟩quantum-operator-product𝑞𝑯𝜓displaystylebraketqHpsi =f​(q2)​∫d3​q′(2​π)3​v​(Eq)​f​(q′⁣2)​ψ​(q′)absent𝑓superscript𝑞2superscript𝑑3superscript𝑞′superscript2𝜋3𝑣subscript𝐸𝑞𝑓superscript𝑞′2𝜓superscript𝑞′displaystyle=f(q2)intdfracd3qprime(2pi)3v(Eq)f(qprime 2)tildepsi(qprime) +Eq​ψ​(q)+g​f​(q2)​⟨ψ0|ψ⟩=MB​ψ​(q),subscript𝐸𝑞𝜓𝑞subscript𝑔0𝑓superscript𝑞2inner-productsubscript𝜓0𝜓subscript𝑀𝐵𝜓𝑞displaystyle+Eqtildepsi(q)+gf(q2)braketpsi0psi=MBtildepsi(q), ⟨ψ0|𝑯|ψ⟩=MB0​⟨ψ0|ψ⟩+g​∫quantum-operator-productsubscript𝜓0𝑯𝜓subscript𝑀subscript𝐵0inner-productsubscript𝜓0𝜓subscript𝑔0displaystylebraketpsi0Hpsi=MB0braketpsi0psi+gint d3​q′(2​π)3​f​(q′⁣2)​ψ​(q′)superscript𝑑3superscript𝑞′superscript2𝜋3𝑓superscript𝑞′2𝜓superscript𝑞′displaystyledfracd3qprime(2pi)3f(qprime 2)tildepsi(qprime) =MB​⟨ψ0|ψ⟩. absentsubscript𝑀𝐵inner-productsubscript𝜓0𝜓displaystyle=MBbraketpsi0psi. These equations are obtained by taking the matrix elements of Eq. , inserting the complete set and using the matrix elements of 𝑽𝑽V from Eq. . Combining Eq. and Eq. , we can transform them into Eq​ψ​(q)+f​(q2)​vint​(Eq)​∫d3​q′(2​π)3subscript𝐸𝑞𝜓𝑞𝑓superscript𝑞2superscript𝑣intsubscript𝐸𝑞superscript𝑑3superscript𝑞′superscript2𝜋3displaystyle Eqtildepsi(q)+f(q2)vtextint(Eq)intdfracd3qprime(2pi)3 f​(q′⁣2)​ψ​(q′)𝑓superscript𝑞′2𝜓superscript𝑞′displaystyle f(qprime 2)tildepsi(qprime) =MB​ψj​(q),absentsubscript𝑀𝐵subscript𝜓𝑗𝑞displaystyle=MBtildepsij(q), with an integrated potential defined as vint​(E)=v​(E)+(g)2E−MB0. superscript𝑣int𝐸𝑣𝐸superscriptsubscript𝑔02𝐸subscript𝑀subscript𝐵0vtextint(E)=v(E)+dfrac(g)2E-MB0. The integrated potential vint​(E)superscript𝑣int𝐸vtextint(E) contains the effect of the elementary state acting on scattering states is expressed in addition to the interaction v​(E)𝑣𝐸v(E) between the scattering states.1In Ref. citebib.bib9, vint​(E)superscript𝑣int𝐸vtextint(E) is called effective potential, while we call it integrated potential to distinguish it from the effective potential originated from an effective theory. Formally, the solution of Eq. can be written as ψ​(q)=−c​f​(q2)Eq−MB,𝜓𝑞𝑐𝑓superscript𝑞2subscript𝐸𝑞subscript𝑀𝐵displaystyletildepsi(q)=-dfraccf(q2)Eq-MB, with c=vint​(MB)​∫d3​q′(2​π)3​f​(q′⁣2)​ψ​(q′). 𝑐superscript𝑣intsubscript𝑀𝐵superscript𝑑3superscript𝑞′superscript2𝜋3𝑓superscript𝑞′2𝜓superscript𝑞′displaystyle c=vtextint(MB)intdfracd3qprime(2pi)3f(qprime 2)tildepsi(qprime). We need to note this set of equations is equivalent to Schordinger equations and and still yet to be solved. In order to have a non-trivial solution, we require a non-zero c𝑐c. By inserting Eq. into Eq. , we obtain 1−vint​(MB)​∫d3​q′(2​π)3​[f​(q′⁣2)]2MB−Eq′=0.1superscript𝑣intsubscript𝑀𝐵superscript𝑑3superscript𝑞′superscript2𝜋3superscriptdelimited-𝑓superscript𝑞′22subscript𝑀𝐵subscript𝐸superscript𝑞′01-vtextint(MB)intdfracd3qprime(2pi)3dfrac[f(qprime 2)]2MB-Eqprime=0. By introducing a function G𝐺G as G​(E)=∫d3​q(2​π)3​[f​(q2)]2E−Eq,𝐺𝐸superscript𝑑3𝑞superscript2𝜋3superscriptdelimited-𝑓superscript𝑞22𝐸subscript𝐸𝑞G(E)=intdfracd3q(2pi)3dfrac[f(q2)]2E-Eq, which turns out to be the Green’s function that represents the propagator in the free system. We will be able to arrive at the bound state condition 1=vint​(MB)​G​(MB),1superscript𝑣intsubscript𝑀𝐵𝐺subscript𝑀𝐵1=vtextint(MB)G(MB), which will also be seen in Lippmann-Schwinger equation later. By utilizing Eq. and Eq. , the compositeness is expressed as X𝑋displaystyle X =∫d3​q(2​π)3​|ψ​(q)|2=−|c|2​[d​Gd​E]E=MB. absentsuperscript𝑑3𝑞superscript2𝜋3superscript𝜓𝑞2superscript𝑐2subscriptdelimited-𝑑𝐺𝑑𝐸𝐸subscript𝑀𝐵displaystyle=intdfracd3q(2pi)3|tildepsi(q)|2=-|c|2[dfracdGdE]E=MB. Similarly, we also express the elementariness of a bound state, which is Z𝑍displaystyle Z =⟨ψ|ψ0⟩​⟨ψ0|ψ⟩absentinner-product𝜓subscript𝜓0inner-productsubscript𝜓0𝜓displaystyle=braketpsipsi0braketpsi0psi =|c|2​G​(MB)​g​g(MB−MB0)2​G​(MB)absentsuperscript𝑐2𝐺subscript𝑀𝐵subscript𝑔0subscript𝑔0superscriptsubscript𝑀𝐵subscript𝑀subscript𝐵02𝐺subscript𝑀𝐵displaystyle=|c|2G(MB)dfracgg(MB-MB0)2G(MB) =−|c|2​[G​d​(vint−v)d​E​G]E=MBabsentsuperscript𝑐2subscriptdelimited-𝐺𝑑superscript𝑣int𝑣𝑑𝐸𝐺𝐸subscript𝑀𝐵displaystyle=-|c|2[Gdfracd(vtextint-v)dEG]E=MB in which we have inserted Eq. into Eq. . We note that, if v𝑣v depends on the energy E𝐸E, we should subtract the contribution of v​(E)𝑣𝐸v(E) from the elementariness, because the elementariness is defined by the attribution of the elementary state. Thanks to the completeness of the Hilbert space and normalization of ⟨ψ|ψ⟩=1inner-product𝜓𝜓1braketpsipsi=1, we will have −|c|2​[d​Gd​E+G​d​(vint−v)d​E​G]E=MB=X+Z=1. superscript𝑐2subscriptdelimited-𝑑𝐺𝑑𝐸𝐺𝑑superscript𝑣int𝑣𝑑𝐸𝐺𝐸subscript𝑀𝐵𝑋𝑍1-|c|2[dfracdGdE+Gdfracd(vtextint-v)dEG]E=MB=X+Z=1.
content_sections.II Model and Formalism II.3 Compositenes: We have discussed the formalism of compositeness for a single channel of interest. This formalism can be extended to multi-channels, and also for several elementary states straightforwardly as is described in Ref. citebib.bib9 for constant interactions. We will refrain from giving the full formalism, and only the outcome will be stated. For a bound state with E=MB𝐸subscript𝑀𝐵E=MB, we have Xjsubscript𝑋𝑗displaystyle Xj =∫d3​q(2​π)3​|ψj​(q)|2=−|cj|2​[d​Gjd​E]E=MB,absentsuperscript𝑑3𝑞superscript2𝜋3superscriptsubscript𝜓𝑗𝑞2superscriptsubscript𝑐𝑗2subscriptdelimited-𝑑subscript𝐺𝑗𝑑𝐸𝐸subscript𝑀𝐵displaystyle=intdfracd3q(2pi)3|tildepsij(q)|2=-|cj|2[dfracdGjdE]E=MB, Zasubscript𝑍𝑎displaystyle Za =⟨ψ|ψa⟩​⟨ψa|ψ⟩absentinner-product𝜓subscript𝜓𝑎inner-productsubscript𝜓𝑎𝜓displaystyle=braketpsipsiabraketpsiapsi =∑j,kcj​ck​Gj​(MB)​g​a,j​g​a,k(MB−MBa)2​Gk​(MB),absentsubscript𝑗𝑘subscript𝑐𝑗subscript𝑐𝑘subscript𝐺𝑗subscript𝑀𝐵subscript𝑔0𝑎𝑗subscript𝑔0𝑎𝑘superscriptsubscript𝑀𝐵subscript𝑀subscript𝐵𝑎2subscript𝐺𝑘subscript𝑀𝐵displaystyle=sumj,kcjckGj(MB)dfracga,jga,k(MB-MBa)2Gk(MB), Z𝑍displaystyle Z =∑Za=−∑j,kcj​ck​[Gj​d​(vj​kint−vj​k)d​E​Gk]E=MB,absentsubscript𝑍𝑎subscript𝑗𝑘subscript𝑐𝑗subscript𝑐𝑘subscriptdelimited-subscript𝐺𝑗𝑑superscriptsubscript𝑣𝑗𝑘intsubscript𝑣𝑗𝑘𝑑𝐸subscript𝐺𝑘𝐸subscript𝑀𝐵displaystyle=sum Za=-sumj,kcjck[Gjdfracd(vjktextint-vjk)dEGk]E=MB, in which the subscripts notate the entries of the matrix that is used to notate multichannels. The wave function ψj​(q)subscript𝜓𝑗𝑞tildepsij(q) is defined by ψj​(q)=⟨qj|ψ⟩subscript𝜓𝑗𝑞inner-productsubscript𝑞𝑗𝜓tildepsij(q)=braketqjpsi, and cjsubscript𝑐𝑗cj is defined in a similar way as Eq. , Gjsubscript𝐺𝑗Gj is the loop function in channel j𝑗j, Xjsubscript𝑋𝑗Xj is the compositeness in j𝑗j channel, Zasubscript𝑍𝑎Za is the elementariness as a result of the existence of state a𝑎a, and vj​kintsuperscriptsubscript𝑣𝑗𝑘intvjktextint is the integrated potential between channels j𝑗j and k𝑘k as vj​kint=vj​k​(E)+∑ag​a,j​g​a,kE−MBa,superscriptsubscript𝑣𝑗𝑘intsubscript𝑣𝑗𝑘𝐸subscript𝑎subscript𝑔0𝑎𝑗subscript𝑔0𝑎𝑘𝐸subscript𝑀subscript𝐵𝑎vjktextint=vjk(E)+sumafracga,jga,kE-MBa, The compositeness and the total elementariness can both be found in a similar form as in the single channel case. Since the compositeness is related to the completeness of the model space, we will have a normalization, which writes −∑j,kcj​ck​[δj,k​d​Gjd​E+Gj​d​(vj​kint−vj​k)d​E​Gk]E=MB=1. subscript𝑗𝑘subscript𝑐𝑗subscript𝑐𝑘subscriptdelimited-subscript𝛿𝑗𝑘𝑑subscript𝐺𝑗𝑑𝐸subscript𝐺𝑗𝑑superscriptsubscript𝑣𝑗𝑘intsubscript𝑣𝑗𝑘𝑑𝐸subscript𝐺𝑘𝐸subscript𝑀𝐵1-sumj,kcjck[deltaj,kdfracdGjdE+Gjdfracd(vjktextint-vjk)dEGk]E=MB=1. We need to note that compositeness, as well as elementariness, are highly influenced by the way we define the dynamics. We can include an explicit channel N𝑁N into integrated potential through Feshbach method and make them implicitcitebib.bib9, bib.bib27, by defining wj​k=vj​kint+∑j,k≠Nvj​Nint​GN​(E)1−vN​Nint​GN​(E)​vN​kint,subscript𝑤𝑗𝑘subscriptsuperscript𝑣int𝑗𝑘subscript𝑗𝑘𝑁subscriptsuperscript𝑣int𝑗𝑁subscript𝐺𝑁𝐸1subscriptsuperscript𝑣int𝑁𝑁subscript𝐺𝑁𝐸subscriptsuperscript𝑣int𝑁𝑘wjk=vtextintjk+sumj,kneq NvtextintjNdfracGN(E)1-vtextintNNGN(E)vtextintNk, where N𝑁N indicates the channel to be reduced, and the contribution of this channel is absorbed into the integated interaction wj​ksubscript𝑤𝑗𝑘wjk of this channel. We call such channels reduced channels. Because the contribution of the reduced channels gives additional energy dependence to the integrated interaction, the reduced channels can be another source of the elementariness, even though the contribution of the reduced channels is originally counted as the compositeness before the channel reduction: Zw=−∑j,k≠Ncj​ck​[Gj​d​(wj​k−vj​k)d​E​Gk]E=MB=Z+XN,superscript𝑍𝑤subscript𝑗𝑘𝑁subscript𝑐𝑗subscript𝑐𝑘subscriptdelimited-subscript𝐺𝑗𝑑subscript𝑤𝑗𝑘subscript𝑣𝑗𝑘𝑑𝐸subscript𝐺𝑘𝐸subscript𝑀𝐵𝑍subscript𝑋𝑁Zw=-sumj,kneq Ncjck[Gjdfracd(wjk-vjk)dEGk]E=MB=Z+XN, in which XNsubscript𝑋𝑁displaystyle XN =−cN2​[d​GNd​E]E=MB,absentsuperscriptsubscript𝑐𝑁2subscriptdelimited-𝑑subscript𝐺𝑁𝑑𝐸𝐸subscript𝑀𝐵displaystyle=-cN2[dfracdGNdE]E=MB, cNsubscript𝑐𝑁displaystyle cN =11−vN​Nint​GN​∑kck​Gk​vN​kint. absent11subscriptsuperscript𝑣int𝑁𝑁subscript𝐺𝑁subscript𝑘subscript𝑐𝑘subscript𝐺𝑘subscriptsuperscript𝑣int𝑁𝑘displaystyle=dfrac11-vtextintNNGNsumkckGkvtextintNk. This reduction can be performed on multiple channels. It means that we are able to send the influence of explicit channels into the integrated interaction, and the original compositeness of the reduced channels will appear as elementariness. We can find that this reduction is similar to what we do to the bare states and absorb its contribution into vintsuperscript𝑣intvtextint, in which the explicit elementary state becomes implicit. Effectively, we reduce the model space into its subspace which will be actually solved, then the integrated interaction has energy dependence and the elementariness is evaluated by its energy dependence.

When the interaction v𝑣v is a constant, Eqs. and are equivalent to and , as T. content_sections.II Model and Formalism II.5 Positivity o: In this subsection, we will prove the positivity of elementariness if the energy dependence of the integrated interaction stems from physical states. This proof is partly based on the nature of the Hilbert space as an inner product space. We will be assuming E=MB𝐸subscript𝑀𝐵E=MB, as only the bound state is considered.As can be seen in Eq. and Eq. , the contribution of any elementary states on elementariness ought to be positive. It is because we have included real elementary states, and thus such states are a part of our Hilbert space, where the norm ought to be positive.We have introduced the integrated potential coming from a scattering channel in Eq. . Considering channel N𝑁N to be reduced into channel 0, where interactions v0​Nsubscript𝑣0𝑁v0N and vN​Nsubscript𝑣𝑁𝑁vNN are constant, we have ∂w00∂Esubscript𝑤00𝐸displaystylefracpartial w00partial E =v0​N2​∂∂E​GN​(E)1−vN​N​GN​(E)absentsubscriptsuperscript𝑣20𝑁𝐸subscript𝐺𝑁𝐸1subscript𝑣𝑁𝑁subscript𝐺𝑁𝐸displaystyle=v20Nfracpartialpartial EdfracGN(E)1-vNNGN(E) =v0​N2​∂GN∂E​1(1−vN​N​GN)2<0,absentsubscriptsuperscript𝑣20𝑁subscript𝐺𝑁𝐸1superscript1subscript𝑣𝑁𝑁subscript𝐺𝑁20displaystyle=v20Nfracpartial GNpartial Efrac1(1-vNNGN)2<0, where we have used the conclusion that ∂GN∂E<0subscript𝐺𝑁𝐸0fracpartial GNpartial E<0 when the energy is below the threshold. It is valid because the loop function is limited by optical theorem. We are actually able to derive this sign from the mathematical form of loop function, G​(E)𝐺𝐸displaystyle G(E) =−∫s+∞𝑑s′​ρ​(s′)(s′−E2),absentsuperscriptsubscriptsubscript𝑠differential-dsuperscript𝑠′𝜌superscript𝑠′superscript𝑠′superscript𝐸2displaystyle=-ints+inftydsprimefracrho(sprime)(sprime-E2), G′​(E)superscript𝐺′𝐸displaystyle Gprime(E) =−∫s+∞𝑑s′​ρ​(s′)(s′−E2)2​(2​E)<0,absentsuperscriptsubscriptsubscript𝑠differential-dsuperscript𝑠′𝜌superscript𝑠′superscriptsuperscript𝑠′superscript𝐸222𝐸0displaystyle=-ints+inftydsprimefracrho(sprime)(sprime-E2)2(2E)<0, in which ρ𝜌rho represents the phase space, and G𝐺G is a real function below the threshold. 2 One may consider subtracted dispersion relations instead of Eq. . The subtraction terms will introduce energy dependence which is not constrained by unitarity. Nevertheless, for practical use for hadronic phenomenology, Eq. is still satisfied, because the loop function for two body scattering is converge with once subtracted dispersion relation, which introduces only a subtraction constant and it does not contribute to the derivative. From Eq. , we always have positive elementariness when including a two-body channel with constant interactions, which is similar to including an explicit state.The same statement can be extended to 3-body states. The only difference from a 2-body scattering state and a 3-body scattering state can be found only in the loop function. Let us take the case of deuteron, and define the 3-body state as a N​N​π𝑁𝑁𝜋NNpi state, which represents the pion exchange procedure. Note that the mass is excluded from the energy in this subsection for simplicity. If we define the reduced mass according to Ref. citebib.bib28 as μN​Nsubscript𝜇𝑁𝑁displaystylemuNN =mN2,absentsubscript𝑚𝑁2displaystyle=fracmN2, μπsubscript𝜇𝜋displaystylemupi =mπ​(mN+mN)mN+mN+mπ=2​mπ​mN2​mN+mπ,absentsubscript𝑚𝜋subscript𝑚𝑁subscript𝑚𝑁subscript𝑚𝑁subscript𝑚𝑁subscript𝑚𝜋2subscript𝑚𝜋subscript𝑚𝑁2subscript𝑚𝑁subscript𝑚𝜋displaystyle=fracmpi(mN+mN)mN+mN+mpi=frac2mpimN2mN+mpi, we will be able to express this 3-body loop function as G𝐺displaystyle G (E)=∫d3​pπ(2​π)3​d3​qN​N(2​π)3​1E+i​ϵ−pπ22​μπ−qN​N22​μN​N𝐸superscript𝑑3subscript𝑝𝜋superscript2𝜋3superscript𝑑3subscript𝑞𝑁𝑁superscript2𝜋31𝐸𝑖italic-ϵsuperscriptsubscript𝑝𝜋22subscript𝜇𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑞𝑁𝑁22subscript𝜇𝑁𝑁displaystyle(E)=intfracd3ppi(2pi)3fracd3qNN(2pi)3frac1E+iepsilon-fracppi22mupi-fracqNN22muNN =∫d3​pπ(2​π)3​[−2​μN​N​ΛN​N2​π2−2​i​μN​N4​π​2​μN​N​(E−pπ22​μπ)],absentsuperscript𝑑3subscript𝑝𝜋superscript2𝜋3delimited-2subscript𝜇𝑁𝑁subscriptΛ𝑁𝑁2superscript𝜋22𝑖subscript𝜇𝑁𝑁4𝜋2subscript𝜇𝑁𝑁𝐸superscriptsubscript𝑝𝜋22subscript𝜇𝜋displaystyle=intfracd3ppi(2pi)3[-frac2muNNLambdaNN2pi2-frac2imuNN4pisqrt2muNN(E-fracppi22mupi)], where ΛN​NsubscriptΛ𝑁𝑁LambdaNN is the cutoff of two body loop function GN​Nsubscript𝐺𝑁𝑁GNN, and we utilize a loop function that is stated in Ref. citebib.bib29 with a modification by adding a constant to adapt to some more proper calculations on renormalizatons. Since the loop function is constrained by optical theorem, taking this form for calculation will not lose generality. We will not evaluate the loop function directly, and only look at the sign of d​Gd​E𝑑𝐺𝑑𝐸fracdGdE, d​Gd​E=−μN​N328​2​π2​∫0∞𝑑pπ​pπ2​1−E+pπ22​μπ. 𝑑𝐺𝑑𝐸superscriptsubscript𝜇𝑁𝑁3282superscript𝜋2superscriptsubscript0differential-dsubscript𝑝𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑝𝜋21𝐸superscriptsubscript𝑝𝜋22subscript𝜇𝜋displaystylefracdGdE=-fracmuNNfrac328sqrt2pi2int0inftydppippi2frac1sqrt-E+fracppi22mupi. In the area of interest, E<0𝐸0E<0, and thus d​Gd​E<0𝑑𝐺𝑑𝐸0fracdGdE<0. We need to note that even there is a square root in the expression, the Riemann surface structure is not relavent. It is because the branch cut in scattering theory is usually taken from the threshold pointing to the positive direnction. In this way, the elementariness generated by 1,2,3-body scattering states with constant interaction is proved to be positive. This proof is made based on the definition of elementariness and the sign of loop functions. This outcome can also be interpreted in a way that the energy dependence stems form the reduction of the Hilbert space and the completeness should be kept in the channel reduction. Further, when taking surjective interpretation, it is natural that we conclude that elementariness Z˘˘𝑍breveZ evaluated by Eq. is also positive.
content_sections.II Model and Formalism II.6 Discussion:: We have reviewed the calculation method of compositeness and elementariness based on scattering theory. We also concluded that, if the energy dependence of the integrated interaction comes from the channel reduction, the elementariness Z˘˘𝑍breveZ evaluated by Eq. is expected to be positive. We have also clarified that the calculation through Eq. and as described in Ref. citebib.bib9 relies on surjective interpretation, that is, the method works with a positive value of elementariness when the energy dependence comes soley from physical states. In later sections, however, we will see that Eqs. and may provide a negative value of Z˘˘𝑍breveZ in certain situations, such as deuteron. To connect the compositeness and elementariness in the Schrodinger formalism and the Lippmann Schwinger formalism, one needs to pin down the origin of the energy dependence in the integrated interaction vintsuperscript𝑣intvtextrmint. It is possible when one knows a priori that the intrinsic energy dependence is negligibly smaller than that of the other energy dependence. Alternatively, we can also pin down the intrinsic energy dependence by using effective theories of the scattering system, such as chiral effective theories. For this purpose, we need to introduce models to describe the integrated interaction and the evaluation of compositeness becomes more model dependent. We would like to emphasize that using the scattering equation in the calculation of compositeness and elementariness has a practical advantage, because scattering data are available for the determinations of the residue of the scattering amplitude at the bound state pole and the energy dependence of the integrated interaction, which allows us to evaluate compositeness and elementariness phenomenologically based on physical observables. This is one of the most important findings in Ref. citebib.bib9. As phenomenological investigations, one may parameterize the integrated interaction of the scattering system in interest by assuming minimal requirements, such as unitary and symmetries. With enough experimental data, these parameters can be determined by scattering observations, and evaluation of compositeness and elementariness in a less model dependent way becomes possible. To be clear, our definition of compositeness and elementariness are based on quantum mechanics, in which one requires clearly defined intermediate states when one inserts the complete set as intermediate states. This is formulated as old-fashioned perturbation theory (OFPT) citebib.bib30, in which every intermediate state is on-shell while energy conservation is violated at vertices. Thus, the energy of the intermediate state is well fixed and there is no room for the intrinsic energy dependence for the integrated interaction. QFT formulates perturbation theory differently, in which four-momentum is conserved at vertices and intermediates can be off-shell. Because the intermediates do not have definite energy, the integrated interaction can be energy dependent. Of course, it is known that both formulations provide equivalent results on the observations, but some off-shell behaviors can be different.
content_sections.III Application to Deuteron: Complicatio: Even though Eqs. and show promising indication that compositeness and elementariness can be obtained by using scattering observables, it has been shown that a numerical calculation of compositeness of deuteron is larger than one. When Weinberg proposed compositeness, he intended to answer the question whether deuteron is a composite particle or not. He presented a calculation framework under weak-binding limit and obtained 1.68 compositeness with physical observables such as scattering length and effective range citebib.bib4citebib.bib5. Since compositeness should be interpreted as a proportion, a compositeness larger than one is not natural, even though S. Weinberg concluded that deuteron is at least almost composite. This section will be dedicated to dicussing the difference between X𝑋X and X˘˘𝑋breveX, Z𝑍Z and Z˘˘𝑍breveZ. We will demonstrate the calculation of the compositeness of deuteron based on Eq. , a. k. a. X˘˘𝑋breveX, using simple models and see that the value of compositeness turns to be greater than one when we use observed N​N𝑁𝑁NN scattering properties and deuteron binding energy. We will also find out that the origin of the problems is in the calculation method and and show that it is not likely to be avoidable for the deuteron case. Finally we discuss phenomenological implication of the findings of this section.

Based on Eqs. and , we perform the calculation of the compositeness of the deuteron by introducing an effective interaction, and it turns out that the value of deuteron compositeness becomes greater than unity. We use simple models for the integrated interaction by considering a low momentum expansion. The parameters of the integrated interaction are fixed by the observables such as the scattering length, the effective range and the deuteron binding energy. Besides, the calculation is based on a local and separable form of interaction. For more sophisticated calculations, we can use whole amplitudes of the N​N𝑁𝑁NN scattering at low energy, which are available in market. The simplest model may be a constant interaction. We can fix the value of the constant interaction with the scattering length. This model can reproduce neither the deuteron binding energy nor effective range. Actually, this model underestimates the deuteron binding energy. The value of compositeness of this simple model is one by definition because the interaction has no energy dependence and the elementariness is zero. In order to provide a more realistic deuteron binding energy, we need an attractive correction in the effective interaction of the S-wave. In the momentum expansion, we use c0+c1​q2subscript𝑐0subscript𝑐1superscript𝑞2c0+c1q2 and the parameters are fixed by the scattering length and the effective range. The deuteron binding energy is calculated as 2.232 MeV, which is consistent with the observation. Because the q2superscript𝑞2q2 dependence in the interaction is counted as the energy dependence for the matrix element given by Eq. , the value of compositeness should be deviated from unity. We find the value of deuteron compositeness of this model to be 1.479, which is grater than one. This implies that the energy dependence of the effective interaction which corrects the deuteron binding energy by attractive shift from the constant interaction provides a negative contribution for the value of deuteron elementariness. We find that this problem cannot be resolved by adding higher q2superscript𝑞2q2 corrections, d𝑑d-wave contribution nor a more realistic one-pion exchange potential as shown in Table . The details of the calculation is shown in Appendix .

In this subsection, we generalize the discussion given in the previous subsection by proving that energy-dependent attractive correction in the interaction is likely to always provide a value of compositeness lager than unity if one uses Eq. . Let us consider a two-body scattering system. We assume that with a constant interaction v0subscript𝑣0v0, the system has a bound state with mass M0subscript𝑀0M0, and consequently the T𝑇T-matrix of this system has a pole at E=M0𝐸subscript𝑀0E=M0. The value of the compositeness for this bound state is unity owing to the constant interaction; X˘0=1subscript˘𝑋01breveX0=1. Then we consider an attractive energy dependent correction v​(E)𝑣𝐸tildev(E) in this system. With this correction the interaction is given by v​(E)=v0+v​(E)𝑣𝐸subscript𝑣0𝑣𝐸v(E)=v0+tildev(E) and the bound state energy is shifted to M𝑀M. The T𝑇T-matrix of the new system has a pole at E=M𝐸𝑀E=M. These two systems have the same free Hamiltonian H0subscript𝐻0H0 and consequently the same loop function G𝐺G. The compositeness of the bound state E=M𝐸𝑀E=M, X˘˘𝑋breveX, can be calculated by using Eq. . The details of the calculation are shown in Appendix . Finally we find the compositeness of the bound state M𝑀M in the first order perturbation of v​(E)𝑣𝐸tildev(E) as X˘≃X˘0​(1−λ​v′G′​v02),similar-to-or-equals˘𝑋subscript˘𝑋01𝜆superscript𝑣′superscript𝐺′superscriptsubscript𝑣02breveXsimeqbreveX0(1-lambdafractildevprimeGprimev02), which relates compositeness X˘˘𝑋breveX to the compositeness of the unperturbed bound state X˘0=1subscript˘𝑋01breveX0=1. Here λ𝜆lambda is the perturbation parameter indicating order, and v′superscript𝑣′tildevprime and G′superscript𝐺′Gprime are defined as their derivatives evaluated at E=M0𝐸subscript𝑀0E=M0 v′≡∂v∂E|E=M0,G′≡∂G∂E|E=M0,formulae-sequencesuperscript𝑣′evaluated-at𝑣𝐸𝐸subscript𝑀0superscript𝐺′evaluated-at𝐺𝐸𝐸subscript𝑀0tildevprimeequivleft. fracpartialtildevpartial Eright|E=M0,qquad Gprimeequivleft. fracpartial Gpartial Eright|E=M0, respectively. As discussed in the previous section, the derivative of the loop function G′superscript𝐺′Gprime is negative below the threshold. (We consider bound states, and M0subscript𝑀0M0 is smaller than the threshold energy. ) Therefore, any positive v′superscript𝑣′tildevprime will have a positive impact on compositeness. Here we consider an attractive energy dependent correction v​(E)𝑣𝐸tildev(E) which shifts the binding energy M0subscript𝑀0M0 to MM0𝐸subscript𝑀0E>M0 and this is out of our consideration because the presence of a bound state higher than M0subscript𝑀0M0 is regarded as repulsive correction. In this way, in order to deepen the bound state, it is highly likely to increase compositeness from unity for energy dependent potentials. This implies that the value of elementariness of this state can be negative. This contradicts with the positivity of elementariness when all of the energy dependence in the integrated interaction stems from the reduction of states and scattering channels. This outcome shows that if the calculation based on near-threshold limit gives a value of compositeness larger than unity, it is an implication that the energy dependence cannot be totally attributed to other states and channels, but being inherently in the interaction instead. We need to note that this calculation is based solely on the Lippmann-Schwinger equation in a contact interaction, which means that this calculation did not explicitly take the model space into account, which can be the limitation of the calculation with Eqs. and .

Recall that in subsection , we have discussed the property that other states will bring about positive elementariness based on Schrodinger equation and Feshbach procedure, and we have an expectation to have 0≤Z˘≤10˘𝑍10leqbreveZleq 1 as it is to Z𝑍Z. However, as shown in this section, if we accept surjective interpretation and the calculation based on it in Eqs. and , the value of deuteron elementariness Z˘˘𝑍breveZ is highly likely to be negative due to the insufficient attraction caused by the scattering length. As a result, we give an evidence that under some circumstances, considering X˘˘𝑋breveX and Z˘˘𝑍breveZ as the originally defined X𝑋X and Z𝑍Z may not be physically appropriate.
content_sections.IV Alternative approaches to compositnes: In this section, the origin of the problem of using X˘˘𝑋breveX in place of X𝑋X will be discussed. Based on this discussion, some alternative phenomenological approaches will be considered.

To calculate compositeness, a phenomenological potential is required. The path of getting this potential is soley to reproduce experimental observables, and the theoretical considerations of the Hilbert space is skipped, which ought to give a positive elementariness. Let us look at the one-body state as an example. We notice that the restriction on the sign of the elementary state to be reduced in Eq. is not manifested in the final outcome of compositeness and elementariness . From a phenomenological point of view, there might be no limitation to stop the potential from taking a form like vint​(E)=v−(g)2E−MB0. superscript𝑣int𝐸𝑣superscriptsubscript𝑔02𝐸subscript𝑀subscript𝐵0vtextint(E)=v-dfrac(g)2E-MB0. This potential can be interpreted as a negative norm state, and would lead to a negative elementariness. Further, the generalization of this statement from one-body state to two-body and three-body state could be straight-forward. This would show a possibility of finding a negative elementariness in some particles from phenomenology based on surjective interpretation, and deuteron is one example. In this way, we give a possible reason of why compositeness of deuteron is usually calculated as largen than one, which is taking surjective interpretation and using Z˘˘𝑍breveZ interchangably with Z𝑍Z.

In a local Hamiltonian QFT under the Effective Range Model, it is found that as long as the effective range is positive, the price must be paid that the space of quantum states will include negative-norm states citebib.bib31. We can find the same scenario in deuteron, meaning if a calculation on deuteron is to be carried out in this model, negative-norm states will appear for the same reason. A phenomenologcial calculation on NN scattering successfully reproduced the phase shift by including a negative norm state citebib.bib32. This outcome aligns with our calculation in Eq. where the effective range will be the dominant contributor of v′superscript𝑣′tildevprime, making it also the main reason for negative elementariness. Even though negative norm state may be a reasonable price to pay when only the experimental values are considered, it may not be a good option in the calculation compositeness. Compositeness is not an observable, but rather a value that encapsulates how the particle of interest is to be interpreted. Accepting negative norm states will make it neccessary to find a way to interprete negative elementariness outside the current probability framework of interpretation, weakening the motivation of compositeness as the probability. On the other hand, while effective range model model is closely related to weak-binding limit and our calculation, the outcomes above may be an indication that effective range model is not appropriate in certain cases.

We may disregard surjective interpretation and accept that energy dependence is intrinsic inside the interaction as vint​(E)=v​(E)+(g)2E−MB0. superscript𝑣int𝐸𝑣𝐸superscriptsubscript𝑔02𝐸subscript𝑀subscript𝐵0vtextint(E)=v(E)+dfrac(g)2E-MB0. As we have seen before, substituting the wave function into Schrodinger Equation , we have, ⟨ψ0|ψ⟩=c​g0MB−MB0,inner-productsubscript𝜓0𝜓𝑐subscript𝑔0subscript𝑀𝐵subscript𝑀subscript𝐵0braketpsi0psi=fraccg0MB-MB0, where c𝑐c originates from the normalization constant when solving the Schrodinger equation. Thus, the elementariness is obtained as Z𝑍displaystyle Z =⟨ψ|ψ0⟩​⟨ψ0|ψ⟩absentinner-product𝜓subscript𝜓0inner-productsubscript𝜓0𝜓displaystyle=braketpsipsi0braketpsi0psi =−|c|2​[G​d​(vint−v)d​E​G]E=MBabsentsuperscript𝑐2subscriptdelimited-𝐺𝑑superscript𝑣int𝑣𝑑𝐸𝐺𝐸subscript𝑀𝐵displaystyle=-|c|2[Gdfracd(vtextint-v)dEG]E=MB ≠−|g|2​[G​d​vintd​E​G]E=MB=Z˘. absentsuperscript𝑔2subscriptdelimited-𝐺𝑑superscript𝑣int𝑑𝐸𝐺𝐸subscript𝑀𝐵˘𝑍displaystyleneq-|g|2[GdfracdvtextintdEG]E=MB=breveZ. From the second line to the third line, we are supposed to use the derivative of Eq. . However, contrary to Eq. , in which we have constant v𝑣v, we are unable to ignore the derivative of v​(E)𝑣𝐸v(E), and thus the relation c=g𝑐𝑔c=g breaks. As a result, we are unable to define compositeness and elementariness as they are in Eqs. and , if there is energy dependence inherent inside the potential instead of interpreted as integrated potential from other channels. We need to note that even if c≠g𝑐𝑔cneq g, the idendity in Eq. is not influenced, since it is totally an outcome of Lippmann-Schwinger equation and pole structure.We have found that it is theoretically difficult to acquire deuteron property and reasonable compositeness at the same time under surjective interpretation. This is because there should be intrinsic energy dependence in the integrated interaction which cannot be interpreted as the reduction of other explicit states and channels. Thus, we ought to extend the theory to treat energy dependence more thoroughly. To do this, we will go back to the formalism of compositeness. Specifically, we may compare completeness relations and , which are the identities originated from Schrodinger equation and Lippmann-Schwinger equation. Under surjective interpretation, if we do model calculation, we actually perform the calculation of X𝑋X and Z𝑍Z twice, once from Schrodinger equation and once from Lippmann-Schwinger equation. We confirm, instead of utilizing, the completeness of Hilbert space. This redundancy aimed at the connection between Schrodinger equation and Lippmann-Schwinger equation, and finally c=g𝑐𝑔c=g. As this method may not be appropriate, we propose to cut off this connection first. Considering the nature of the Hilbert space and the discussion about positivity of elementariness in Section. , we conclude that compositeness and elementariness both are positive. With the positive sign of the residue |g|2superscript𝑔2|g|2 and normalization factor |c|2superscript𝑐2|c|2, we have [d​Gd​E]E=MB<0,subscriptdelimited-𝑑𝐺𝑑𝐸𝐸subscript𝑀𝐵0displaystyle[dfracdGdE]E=MB<0, [G​d​vint−vd​E​G]E=MB<0. subscriptdelimited-𝐺𝑑superscript𝑣int𝑣𝑑𝐸𝐺𝐸subscript𝑀𝐵0displaystyle[Gdfracdvtextint-vdEG]E=MB<0. With this idea, we can try to recover a similar form from Eq. X´=−|g|2​[d​Gd​E]E=MB=X˘,´𝑋superscript𝑔2subscriptdelimited-𝑑𝐺𝑑𝐸𝐸subscript𝑀𝐵˘𝑋displaystyleacuteX=-|g|2[dfracdGdE]E=MB=breveX, Y´=−|g|2​[G​d​vd​E​G]E=MB,´𝑌superscript𝑔2subscriptdelimited-𝐺𝑑𝑣𝑑𝐸𝐺𝐸subscript𝑀𝐵displaystyleacuteY=-|g|2[GdfracdvdEG]E=MB, Z´=−|g|2​[G​d​(vint−v)d​E​G]E=MB,´𝑍superscript𝑔2subscriptdelimited-𝐺𝑑superscript𝑣int𝑣𝑑𝐸𝐺𝐸subscript𝑀𝐵displaystyleacuteZ=-|g|2[Gdfracd(vtextint-v)dEG]E=MB, where we defined a new quantity Y´´𝑌acuteY, which we call as interactioness. The new set of quantities are labeled with an acute mark to separate from both the original formalism and surjective interpretation. Because of Eq. , X´´𝑋acuteX and Z´´𝑍acuteZ are both positive, and X´+Y´+Z´=1´𝑋´𝑌´𝑍1acuteX+acuteY+acuteZ=1. Therefore, we can interprete the interactioness Y´´𝑌acuteY as a part of compositeness, so that the compositeness can be less than one. The reason for this set of quantities to be chosen is that it bears strong physical relation with the original formalism. Even though the equivalence between c𝑐c and g𝑔g is broken, we can view Eq. as a generalization from equivalence to relation, where the model independence is lost due to the explicit existence of v𝑣v. Finally, this set of quantities can smoothly connect to surjective interpretation with d​vd​E=0𝑑𝑣𝑑𝐸0dfracdvdE=0. From here, we can generalize the word ”interpretation” as a way to separate v𝑣v from vintsuperscript𝑣intvtextint, or Y´´𝑌acuteY and Z´´𝑍acuteZ, or equivalently how the model space is viewed. From this view point, surjective interpretation is one among the infinite number of interpretations, and is a special case where no intrinsic energy dependent potential exists. As a simple example, we can define another extreme case of interpretation that all the energy dependence of vintsuperscript𝑣intvtextint is intrinsic, i. e. originates from v𝑣v. Under this interpretation, any bound states origine from scattering states and always have elementariness zero, i. e. totally composite particle. To obtain a clearer understanding of interpretation, let us assume that we have exact knowledge of T𝑇T matrix. This is not practically possible, but can make the consequences straighforward. From the knowledge of a channel of interest, we are able to acquire its loop function G𝐺G, and from Lippmann-Schwinger equation , vintsuperscript𝑣intvtextint can be solved. By implementing some interpretation, we get to know v𝑣v since the interpretation limits the intrinsic energy dependence. No further information or assumptions are required in the calculation of compositeness and elementariness. With exact knowledge of the experiment, fixing interpretation may lead to reasonable compositeness and elementariness.

In the construction of calculation of compositeness, we work in the momentum space and required that the interaction is always local. It is in most of the cases a reasonable choice, but the interaction does not always have to take such form. For example, there are other formalisms that work in the coordinate space. citebib.bib33 This effect may be important in deuteron because pion exchange plays an important role. Such diagram shall form a ladder diagram where the off-shell part of pion can be important, and a low energy expansion in the momentum space may not be able to catch such characteristics. In Ref. citebib.bib34, a pionless effective theory that contains an energy dependent interaction is shown, and this interaction provides a positive effective range. This interaction plays a similar role as some negative norm state in the integrated interaction even though such state does not exist in the original theory. The energy dependent interactions in the pionless effective theory may be obtained from pionful theories by integrating out the pion degrees of freedom. We propose to modify the formalism of compositeness completely, preferably with field theory approach. In QFT, we have well defined models such as Chiral Perturbation Theory that can eliminate the arbitrariness in interactions. On the other hand, it is relatively difficult to interprete compositeness, because compositeness relies on states, and states are not the main entities discussed in field theory. Some work has been done in this direction. citebib.bib19, bib.bib8
content_sections.V Conclusion: In this paper, we have formalized compositeness and elementariness in the case that the interaction contains an intrinsic energy dependence. We then discussed the separation of Schrodinger equation and Lippmann-Schwinger equation driven by the energy dependency. Since none of the numerical calculations can reproduce a reasonable compositeness for deuteron under current assumption, we have performed a perturbative calculation on compositeness. We have found that if we follow surjective interpretation strictly, we can have a direct relationship between binding energy and compositeness: if the binding energy indicated by scattering length is lower than that from experiment, the compositeness will likely always be larger than one, and vice versa. Further, under the surjective interpretation that any energy dependency comes from other states, we concluded that elementary should be positive. Through proof by contradiction, we have concluded that surjective interpretation is not appropriate for deuteron through T. Sekihara’s formalism. The origin of such an outcome can be traced back to energy dependence in the interaction that cannot be expressed by other states or channels. In order to solve this problem, we proposed a straightforward extension by defining a new quantity interactioness, and thus the generalized interpretation can be discussed case by case. However, this extension will require an exact knowledge on the interaction. Alternatively, we propose to modify the theory from quantum mechanics into field theory formalism. Even though there may be some difficulty in interpretation since states are not prominent in QFT, the interactions are more well-defined. At the current state, compositeness can be utilized as a way to benchmark effective theories. Since the positivity of elementariness is proved in this paper, this positivity should hold when we take an interpretation under such effective theory.
