all_papers.title:
Model independent analysis of femtoscopic correlation functions: An
  application to the $D_{s0}^*(2317)$
all_papers.abstract:
We face the inverse problem of obtaining the interaction between coupled
channels from the correlation functions of these channels. We apply the method
to the interaction of the $D^0 K^+$, $D^+ K^0$, and $D^+_s \eta$ channels, from
where the $D^*_{s0}(2317)$ state emerges. We use synthetic data extracted from
an interaction model based on the local hidden gauge approach and find that the
inverse problem can determine the existence of a bound state of the system with
a precision of about 20 MeV. At the same time, we can determine the isospin
nature of the bound state and its compositeness in terms of the channels.
Furthermore, we evaluate the scattering length and effective range of all three
channels, as well as the couplings of the bound state found to all the
components. Lastly, the size parameter of the source function, $R$, which in
principle should be a magnitude provided by the experimental teams, can be
obtained from a fit to the data with relatively high accuracy. These findings
show the value of the correlation function to learn about the meson-meson
interaction for systems which are difficult to access in other present
facilities.

qualitative: The inverse problem approach allows extraction of interaction properties between coupled channels from femtoscopic correlation functions without relying on a specific model.
The method enables determination of the existence, binding energy, isospin nature, and compositeness of a bound state formed by coupled channels such as D⁰K⁺, D⁺K⁰, and Dₛ⁺η.
The compositeness of a bound state can be quantified in terms of its contribution from each channel, providing insight into its molecular nature.
The method accounts for uncertainties due to parameter correlations and can include contributions from missing channels or non-molecular components.
The size parameter of the source function, R, can be extracted from the fit to correlation functions with high accuracy, even if it is typically provided by experiment.
The Dₛ₀*(2317) state is likely a molecular state dominated by D⁰K⁺ and D⁺K⁰ channels in isospin I=0, with lattice QCD supporting ~72% DK content.
The method is complementary to traditional resonance studies and avoids ambiguities from resonance reflections in invariant mass analyses.
Synthetic data generated from a local hidden gauge model are used to validate the inverse method's ability to recover physical parameters.
quantitative: The Dₛ₀*(2317) state is bound by approximately 42 MeV below the D⁰K⁺ threshold.
The method determines the existence of a bound state with a precision of about 20 MeV in binding energy.
Lattice QCD simulations indicate that the D⁰K⁺ and D⁺K⁰ components contribute about 72% to the wave function of the Dₛ₀*(2317) state.
The Dₛ₀*(2317) has a very small width (< 3.8 MeV), attributed to isospin violation in its decay channel Dₛ⁺π⁰.
The Tcc state (D⁰D*⁺ bound state) has a much smaller binding energy of ~360 keV compared to the 42 MeV binding of Dₛ₀*(2317).
The size parameter R of the source function can be determined from the fit to correlation functions with relatively high accuracy, despite being a priori an experimental input.

all_papers.reason:
论文核心聚焦于通过关联函数反演强子态的组分结构，明确分析了$D_{s0}^*(2317)$的束缚态成分与通道混杂性，直接对应“Weinberg复合度准则”中对束缚态组分的定量分析要求。研究提取了各通道耦合、有效范围、散射长度及束缚态对各分量的贡献，完全契合该准则的评估维度。

约束项检查：  
- C1：任务为“束缚态组分分析” — 满足。证据：“determine the compositeness in terms of the channels” 对比 “Weinberg复合度准则”  
- C2：方法需基于Weinberg准则框架 — 满足。证据：通过关联函数反演相互作用，直接计算组分权重，符合该准则的物理内涵  
- C3：研究对象为强子束缚态的复合结构 — 满足。证据：“bound state of the system” 对比 “复合度分析”  

不相关性 / 风险点 / 缺口：  
- 无。论文完全覆盖查询核心，无偏离。
authors: Natsumi Ikeno, Genaro Toledo, Eulogio Oset

content_sections.I Introduction: The femtoscopic correlations function measurements citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13 have opened a new window to learn about hadron interactions and much theoretical work is following that pathcitebib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26. The correlation functions provide information on the wave function of the pairs of particles observed in the experiment, from where one can obtain information on the interaction of these particles. The method will prove complementary to the studies done by other facilities such as LHCb, Belle, and BESIII. On the first hand, there is in principle no limitation to the pairs that one selects, and hence one can have access to the interaction of many pairs which would not be accessible with other facilities. On the second hand, the experiments select pairs of particles and the correlation functions are sensitive to the interaction of the pairs. Conversely, in the most popular and successful methods, one studies three body decays of some hadrons, let’s say B𝐵B decays, then one can play around the invariant mass of different pairs, but the analysis of the data are complicated, and sometimes ambiguous, because one has to deal simultaneously with the interaction of these pairs, and resonances appearing in some channel produce reflections in other channels that one must be careful not to identify with new resonances citebib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29. So far, in order to learn about the interaction of particles from the correlation functions, one compares experimental correlation functions with models for the interaction, with the aim of discriminating among models, or allowing the determination of certain parameters from a given modelcitebib.bib23, bib.bib21. In the present work, we follow a different path and use a method that allows one to determine the physical magnitudes related to the studied channel, eventually determining if there are some bound states below the threshold, as well as to determine the nature of the states obtained. The method starts considering the most general interaction potential in coupled channels, which involves a few parameters. Then the correlation functions of coupled channels are evaluated and a simultaneous fit to these correlation functions is conducted to determine the parameters of the theory. We realize that there are large correlations between the parameters and we play with the freedom to select them in order to quantify uncertainties in the magnitudes evaluated. The method also allows for the contribution of missing channels or the existence of a state of nonmolecular nature that can couple to these channels. These possible contributions, with their uncertainty, also come from a fit to the correlation functions, such that in the case of the presence of bound states related to the studied channels, we can determine the probability that the state is of molecular nature formed by these channels and their relative contributions to the full wave function of the state. In the present work, we apply the method to the D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+, D+​K0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0D+K0, and Ds+​ηsubscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠𝜂D+seta channels as an example, and we show that by looking at the correlation function of these three channels, we can deduce that there is a state bound by about 42MeV, that can be associated to be Ds​0∗​subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠02317D*s0 state, and we can say something about the nature of this state. In the absence of real data, we produce synthetic data from a successful model. The inverse method used here allows us to obtain information from the original model, which is sufficient to determine physical quantities and tell us with which precision we can obtain them given the information provided by the correlation functions.
content_sections.II Formalism of the inverse problem for: The Ds​0∗​subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠02317D*s0 is supposed to be a state of molecular nature from the channels D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+, D+​K0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0D+K0, and Ds+​ηsubscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠𝜂D+seta citebib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, mostly a D​K𝐷𝐾DK state in isospin I=0𝐼0I=0 which is also supported by lattice QCD simulationscitebib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41. A detailed analysis of lattice QCD data done in Ref. citebib.bib42 could even qualify the content of D​K𝐷𝐾DK in the wave function of the Ds​0∗​subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠02317D*s0 around 72% probability. The state is bound by about 42MeV with respect to the D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+ threshold, a large binding compared to 360keV of the Tc​c​subscript𝑇𝑐𝑐3875Tcc with respect to the D0​D∗+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐷absentD0D*+ threshold, which is assumed to be a bound state of the D0​D∗+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐷absentD0D*+ and D∗​D∗0superscript𝐷superscript𝐷absent0D*D*0 channelscitebib.bib43 (see also this conclusion in the analysis of Ref. citebib.bib44). The state is observed in the Ds+​π0subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠superscript𝜋0D+spi0 channelcitebib.bib45, which violates isospin, and this is the reason for its extremely small width (<3.8absent3.8<3.8MeV). In the present work, we shall assume that three correlation functions of the D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+, D+​K0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0D+K0 and Ds+​ηsubscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠𝜂D+seta are given to us, and from there we shall try to induce the existence of the Ds​0∗​subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠02317D*s0 state and its properties. These correlation functions are not yet available, but plans are already made to extend the study of the correlation functions to the D𝐷D sector in a near futurecitebib.bib46. Then, to explain how the method works and what it accomplishes, we shall generate the correlation function using an updated version of the work of Ref. citebib.bib24.

We use an extension of the local hidden gauge approach citebib.bib47, bib.bib48, bib.bib49, bib.bib50 in which the interaction is obtained from the exchange of vector mesons as depicted in Fig. . The interaction between the channels D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+, D+​K0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0D+K0, Ds+​ηsubscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠𝜂D+seta is given by Vi​j=Ci​j​g2​(p1+p3)⋅(p2+p4);subscript𝑉𝑖𝑗⋅subscript𝐶𝑖𝑗superscript𝑔2subscript𝑝1subscript𝑝3subscript𝑝2subscript𝑝4displaystyle Vij=Cij g2(p1+p3)cdot(p2+p4); g=MV2​f,MV=800​MeV,f=93​MeV,formulae-sequence𝑔subscript𝑀𝑉2𝑓formulae-sequencesubscript𝑀𝑉800MeV𝑓93MeVdisplaystyle g=fracMV2f, MV=800textMeV, f=93textMeV, where Ci​j=(−12​(1Mρ2+1Mω2)−1Mρ223​1MK∗2−12​(1Mρ2+1Mω2)23​1MK∗20),subscript𝐶𝑖𝑗121superscriptsubscript𝑀𝜌21superscriptsubscript𝑀𝜔21superscriptsubscript𝑀𝜌2231subscriptsuperscript𝑀2superscript𝐾missing-subexpression121superscriptsubscript𝑀𝜌21superscriptsubscript𝑀𝜔2231subscriptsuperscript𝑀2superscript𝐾missing-subexpressionmissing-subexpression0displaystyle Cij=left(beginarrayccc-frac12left(frac1Mrho2+frac1Momega2right)-frac1Mrho2frac2sqrt3frac1M2K*[2.84544pt] -frac12left(frac1Mrho2+frac1Momega2right)frac2sqrt3frac1M2K* 0endarrayright),, and projected over s𝑠s-wave (p1+p3)⋅(p2+p4)⋅subscript𝑝1subscript𝑝3subscript𝑝2subscript𝑝4displaystyle(p1+p3)cdot(p2+p4) →12[3s−(M2+m2+M′2+m′2)displaystyletofrac12big[3s-(M2+m2+Mprime2+mprime2) −1s(M2−m2)(M′2−m′2)],displaystyle-frac1s(M2-m2)(Mprime2-mprime2)big],, where M𝑀M, m𝑚m are the masses of the D𝐷D, K𝐾K initial mesons in Fig. and M′superscript𝑀′Mprime, m′superscript𝑚′mprime the corresponding ones for the final state. As can be seen in Refs. citebib.bib51, bib.bib29, qmaxsubscript𝑞maxqtextmax provides the range of the interaction in momentum space. The same model is used in Ref. citebib.bib24 to obtain the correlation functions. The interaction is the same except for the V13subscript𝑉13V13, V23subscript𝑉23V23 matrix elements. Now we shall assume that we have the correlation functions provided by some experiment. In the absence of such data at present, we shall show how the method works by using the correlation functions generated using the model of section. We shall make no assumption on the potential Vi​jsubscript𝑉𝑖𝑗Vij except that for good reasons we take V33​(3≡Ds​η)=0subscript𝑉333subscript𝐷𝑠𝜂0V33(3equiv Dseta)=0. This is the case for the local hidden gauge potential, but it is quite general since the vertex η​η​V𝜂𝜂𝑉etaeta V which will be induced in most models is zero. Thus, we have the potential (symmetric) V=(V11V12V13V22V230). Next, we will assume that the potential has isospin symmetry. Using the isospin wave function of Eq. , we impose that ⟨I=0|V|I=1⟩=0quantum-operator-product𝐼0𝑉𝐼10 I=0|V|I=1=0 and conclude that V11=V22,V13=V23,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑉11subscript𝑉22subscript𝑉13subscript𝑉23displaystyle V11=V22,V13=V23, ⟨D​K,I=0|V|D​K,I=0⟩=V11+V12,quantum-operator-product𝐷𝐾𝐼0𝑉𝐷𝐾𝐼0subscript𝑉11subscript𝑉12displaystyle DK,I=0|V|DK,I=0=V11+V12,, ⟨D​K,I=1|V|D​K,I=1⟩=V11−V12. quantum-operator-product𝐷𝐾𝐼1𝑉𝐷𝐾𝐼1subscript𝑉11subscript𝑉12displaystyle DK,I=1|V|DK,I=1=V11-V12,. Hence, our matrix V𝑉V gets a bit simplified to V=(V11V12V13V11V130). 𝑉subscript𝑉11subscript𝑉12subscript𝑉13missing-subexpressionsubscript𝑉11subscript𝑉13missing-subexpressionmissing-subexpression0V=left(beginarraycccV11V12V13 V11V13 0endarrayright). On the other hand, and in order to account for possible missing coupled channels, as well as for the possible contribution of a non-molecular state, we introduce energy dependent parts in the potential, as done in Ref. citebib.bib44, such that V11=V11′+αMV2​(s−s¯),subscript𝑉11superscriptsubscript𝑉11′𝛼subscriptsuperscript𝑀2𝑉𝑠¯𝑠displaystyle V11=V11prime+fracalphaM2V(s-bars),, V12=V12′+βMV2​(s−s¯),subscript𝑉12superscriptsubscript𝑉12′𝛽subscriptsuperscript𝑀2𝑉𝑠¯𝑠displaystyle V12=V12prime+fracbetaM2V(s-bars),, V13=V13′+γMV2​(s−s¯),subscript𝑉13superscriptsubscript𝑉13′𝛾subscriptsuperscript𝑀2𝑉𝑠¯𝑠displaystyle V13=V13prime+fracgammaM2V(s-bars),, where s¯¯𝑠bars is the energy squared of the D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+ threshold. The T𝑇T matrix with the three coupled channels would be evaluated in Eq. and we have now eight free parameters V11′subscriptsuperscript𝑉′11Vprime11, V12′subscriptsuperscript𝑉′12Vprime12, V13′subscriptsuperscript𝑉′13Vprime13, α𝛼alpha, β𝛽beta, γ𝛾gamma, qmaxsubscript𝑞maxqtextmax, and R𝑅R. The question now is to see if from the knowledge of the three correlation functions we can determine these parameters and with which uncertainties. Once we have determined these parameters, we evaluate the magnitudes, aisubscript𝑎𝑖ai, r0​isubscript𝑟0𝑖r0i corresponding to the scattering length and the effective range for each channel, determine if there is a bound state and, if so, we determine the couplings and the compositeness of the state, meaning the probabilities Pisubscript𝑃𝑖Pi (i=1,2,3𝑖123i=1,2,3) for the three channels and their sum. The errors are evaluated directly on the magnitudes mentioned above, since the errors in the parameter are not too meaningful due to strong correlations between them. Indeed, from Eq. we see that if we have a state with isospin I=0𝐼0I=0, what matters is V11+V12subscript𝑉11subscript𝑉12V11+V12 and not the value of the individual parameters. The same can then be said about α𝛼alpha and β𝛽beta since what matters for I=0𝐼0I=0 is α+β𝛼𝛽alpha+beta. On the other hand, there is some correlation between qmaxsubscript𝑞maxqtextmax and V11+V12subscript𝑉11subscript𝑉12V11+V12 since, taking a single channel, the T𝑇T matrix is T=1V−1−G. 𝑇1superscript𝑉1𝐺T=frac1V-1-G. If we have a pole where V−1=Gsuperscript𝑉1𝐺V-1=G, we can make a trade off between qmaxsubscript𝑞maxqtextmax and V𝑉V such that δ​V−1=δ​G𝛿superscript𝑉1𝛿𝐺delta V-1=delta G and then the pole appears at the same place [see discussion to this respect in Ref. citebib.bib44]. To determine the value of the observables and their uncertainty, we use the method of resamplingcitebib.bib54, bib.bib55, bib.bib56. We take 31 points in each correlation function and an error of the value of the correlation function of 0.02, typical of present experimental correlation functions, then generate the centroid of the point with a Gaussian distribution probability and do so for all the 31 points of each correlation function. After that, we make a χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 best fit to the data and determine the value of the parameters V11′subscriptsuperscript𝑉′11Vprime11, V12′subscriptsuperscript𝑉′12Vprime12, V13′subscriptsuperscript𝑉′13Vprime13, α𝛼alpha, β𝛽beta, γ𝛾gamma, qmaxsubscript𝑞maxqtextmax, and R𝑅R. Using these parameters, we calculate the different observables. We perform this procedure around 50 times and then determine the average value and dispersion of each one of the observables, scattering lengths, effective ranges, couplings and probabilities, and we also consider R𝑅R as an observable as well as the binding energy.
content_sections.III Results: In Fig. , we show the result for the correlation function of D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+, D+​K0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0D+K0, Ds+​ηsubscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠𝜂D+seta calculated for R=1𝑅1R=1fm. The correlation functions are very similar to those obtained in Refs. citebib.bib24 and citebib.bib26 for the D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+, which are the only ones depicted in the papers. We have checked that the agreement also holds with the D+​K0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0D+K0 correlation function of Ref. citebib.bib241We thank Li-Sheng Geng for providing us with this information. . In addition, we present the results for the Ds+​ηsuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝜂Ds+eta correlation function. We find that the D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+ correlation function has bigger strength than the one for D+​K0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0D+K0 and the latter has bigger strength than that for the Ds+​ηsuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝜂Ds+eta channel. We also corroborate the finding of Ref. citebib.bib24 that the effect of the Ds+​ηsuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝜂Ds+eta channel in the other two correlation functions is negligible. Although the precise values of the parameters are not very meaningful because of the strong correlations, we show the average value from all the fits for these parameters in order to get a feeling of their value. The R𝑅R parameter is uncorrelated and its uncertainty determines indeed the error of this parameter. From the values of the parameters of each fit of the resampling procedure, we can obtain Ti​jsubscript𝑇𝑖𝑗Tij and from these different magnitudes: The binding energy of the resulting bound state, the scattering length aisubscript𝑎𝑖ai and the effective range r0,isubscript𝑟0𝑖r0,i for the three channels. The coupling gisubscript𝑔𝑖gi of the bound state obtained to each one of the channels and the probabilities Pisubscript𝑃𝑖Pi for each channel in the wave function of the bound state obtained. For the D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+ channels, a1subscript𝑎1a1 and r0,1subscript𝑟01r0,1 will be real, but for the D+​K0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0D+K0 and Ds+​ηsubscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠𝜂D+seta channels, these magnitudes are complex because the decays to the D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+ for D+​K0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0D+K0 or D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+, D+​K0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0D+K0 in the case of the Ds+​ηsuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝜂Ds+eta channel are open. Taking into account that close to the peak of the bound state at s0subscript𝑠0s0 we have Ti​j∼gi​gjs−s0,similar-tosubscript𝑇𝑖𝑗subscript𝑔𝑖subscript𝑔𝑗𝑠subscript𝑠0Tijsimfracgigjs-s0,, then we calculate the coupling as g12=lims→s0(s−s0)​T11;gj=g1​lims→s0T1​jT11,formulae-sequencesuperscriptsubscript𝑔12subscript→𝑠subscript𝑠0𝑠subscript𝑠0subscript𝑇11subscript𝑔𝑗subscript𝑔1subscript→𝑠subscript𝑠0subscript𝑇1𝑗subscript𝑇11g12=limsrightarrow s0left(s-s0right)T11;quad gj=g1limsrightarrow s0fracT1jT11, and the probabilities of each channel Pi=−gi2​∂Gi∂s|s=s0. subscript𝑃𝑖evaluated-atsuperscriptsubscript𝑔𝑖2subscript𝐺𝑖𝑠𝑠subscript𝑠0Pi=-gi2fracpartial Gipartial s|s=s0. The results of the different fits described in the former section give us (E𝐸E stands for the energy of the bound state) E𝐸displaystyle E =2314.2±21.0​MeV,absentplus-or-minus2314.221.0MeVdisplaystyle=2314.2pm 21.0 textMeV, R𝑅displaystyle R =0.984±0.040​fm. absentplus-or-minus0.9840.040fmdisplaystyle=0.984pm 0.040 textfm,. The values for the rest of the magnitudes are given in Table. It is interesting to see that P1+P2=0.66subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃20.66P1+P2=0.66 is not 1 indicating that there is a contribution from some other channels. In this case, we have also the Ds+​ηsuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝜂Ds+eta channel, and we see that P1+P2+P3subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃3P1+P2+P3 is equal 0.75 within uncertainties, indicating that we have a largely molecular state. The result should not come as a surprise and is actually a tautology, since we started from a model to the correlation functions where the Ds​0∗​subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠02317D*s0 is generated dynamically from the D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+, D+​K0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0D+K0 and Ds+​ηsuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝜂Ds+eta channels with probabilities similar to these showing Eq. . Note that P1+P2≃0.66±0.18similar-to-or-equalssubscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2plus-or-minus0.660.18P1+P2simeq 0.66pm 0.18 also agrees with the results extracted from lattice QCD data in Ref. citebib.bib42 of 0.72±0.13±0.05plus-or-minus0.720.130.050.72pm 0.13pm 0.05. What is a novelty of the present approach is that given some experimental correlation functions, the method exposed here provides all these magnitudes, and in particular the probabilities, with a fair accuracy. It is also interesting to see that the couplings g1subscript𝑔1g1, g2subscript𝑔2g2 are very similar, which indicates (see Ref. citebib.bib44) that according to Eq. , we have an I=0𝐼0I=0 state. One good news from the present results is that from the correlations data one can obtain the value of R𝑅R with relative high precision. We started from the correlation functions using R=1𝑅1R=1fm and the return of the fits is R=0.984±0.040𝑅plus-or-minus0.9840.040R=0.984pm 0.040fm, which means that we do not have to rely upon the experimental people to tell us the expected value of R𝑅R for the reaction that they use. The value of R𝑅R can be obtained from a fit to the data. It also should not be underestimated the fact that from the correlation functions which are above the threshold of the channels, one can induce the existence of a bound state related to the interaction of the particles. Once more we could obtain the binding energy with an uncertainty of about 20MeV. It is worth noting here that in the study of lattice data in Ref. citebib.bib42 the binding energy was determined with a similar uncertainty B=38±18±9𝐵plus-or-minus38189B=38pm 18pm 9MeV. We should also note that the value of the scattering lengths are obtained with relatively high accuracy but the effective ranges appear with large uncertainties. We find it also interesting to see what happens if we perform a fit to the two correlation functions of D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+, D+​K0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0D+K0. Certainly we use less information and the uncertainties should be bigger. In order to see the value of using the three correlation functions, we compute only the binding and the probabilities, and find: Fit to the D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+ and D+​K0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0D+K0 correlation functions, E𝐸displaystyle E =2322.2±19.5​MeV,absentplus-or-minus2322.219.5MeVdisplaystyle=2322.2pm 19.5 textMeV, R𝑅displaystyle R =0.979±0.035​fm. absentplus-or-minus0.9790.035fmdisplaystyle=0.979pm 0.035 textfm,. Comparison of the results of Eq. and Table with those in Table obtained with three channels, tell us that we obtain a similar information with two channels as in three channels, only with a little bigger uncertainties. This information is useful from the experimental point of view since the Ds+​ηsuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝜂Ds+eta channel might be more difficult to access. To complete the information provided in this case we show the values of the different magnitudes in Table. We observe that the results for the magnitudes related to the D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+ and D+​K0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0D+K0 channels are similar to those in Table, done using three correlation functions, and the uncertainties are also similar. Only the magnitudes related to the Ds​ηsubscript𝐷𝑠𝜂Dseta channel have now somewhat larger uncertainties. We should make a small remark concerning the fact that P1+P2+P3subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃3P1+P2+P3 is not exactly one, although it can be close by counting uncertainties, in spite of using a model to generate the correlations that uses only the interaction between the molecular components. The reason has to be seen in the fact that the interaction of Eq. , stemming from the local hidden gauge approach, has some energy dependence and this has a consequence that P1+P2+P3≠1subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃31P1+P2+P3neq 1. For an energy independent potential the sum rule P1+P2+P3=1subscript𝑃1subscript𝑃2subscript𝑃31P1+P2+P3=1 is exactly fulfilledcitebib.bib34, bib.bib57. While this energy dependence has no practical effect in the determination of the probabilities for small binding energies, for large ones it produces effects attributing some probability to states outside those explicitly used in the approach.
content_sections.IV Summary: We have investigated here the inverse problem of extracting information from the correlation functions in a model independent way. This approach is different to other methods used in the literature where a particular model is assumed and the parameters are tuned to get a good agreement with the data. We tested the approach with a problem related to the Ds​0∗​superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠02317Ds0* resonance, which is tied to the interaction of the D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+, D+​K0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0D+K0, Ds+​ηsuperscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠𝜂Ds+eta channels. In the absence of real data, we produced synthetic data from the correlation functions using a model based on the local hidden gauge approach for the interaction of mesons. The model, tuning a regulator parameter in the meson–meson loop functions tied to the range of the interaction, produces a bound state at 2317MeV from the interaction of these three channels. The inverse problem is based on one interaction, very flexible, which relies upon 8 parameters. We performed many fits to the data using the resampling method, and for each fit we evaluated the observables, obtaining from them the average value and the dispersion. The method is suited for a problem like the present one where we have a large correlation between the parameters. This is one conclusion of our work, which is that the method used allows to show that different magnitudes tied to the interaction of the channels can be evaluated from the correlation functions with a relative accuracy. The uncertainties obtained are similar to those obtained from present lattice QCD simulationscitebib.bib42. The precision obtained in scattering lengths and effective ranges is also similar to that obtained in the lattice calculationcitebib.bib422In Ref. citebib.bib42, the scattering lengths where extracted for the I=0𝐼0I=0 state, while here we evaluated them for each individual channel. Multiplying the result of Ref. citebib.bib42 for D​K𝐷𝐾DK by 1212frac1sqrt2, and changing the sign of a𝑎a (1a1𝑎frac1a instead of −1a1𝑎-frac1a in Ref. citebib.bib42), the results are compatible within errors. The results of r0subscript𝑟0r0 have large errors in both cases. . We also addressed the issue of the nature of the bound state obtained. Starting from a model that generates the bound state as a molecular state of the three considered channels, one expects to obtain the same results. This is the case here, but the interesting thing is that probabilities of the different channels can be obtained with some uncertainty that could be improved if the experimental errors are smaller than assumed here. This is an important message that we can convey, since the analysis of correlation data along the lines discussed here can serve in the future to determine the nature of some bound states. We also addressed the issue of how relevant is to use three correlation functions simultaneously, instead of two, and found that the use of the three correlation functions helped improve the accuracy in the determination of different magnitudes, but using just the D0​K+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐾D0K+ and D+​K0superscript𝐷superscript𝐾0D+K0 correlation functions already gave us enough information concerning these two channels.

all_papers.title: A toy model for "elementariness"
all_papers.abstract:
Motivated by recent efforts to analyze corrections to Weinberg's relations
for the scattering length and effective range in the presence of a
near-threshold bound state, we play around with an instructive toy model for
non-relativistic scattering in a central potential. The model allows to
interpolate between bound-state configurations of high "compositeness", where
the wave function is spread over a wide region beyond the range of the
interaction, and compact configurations of high "elementariness", where the
wave function is confined to a small region around the center of the potential.

qualitative: The compositeness 𝒞B0 quantifies the degree to which a near-threshold bound state is composed of two-particle components, with higher values indicating a more composite state.
The elementariness ℰB0 measures the degree to which a bound state behaves as an elementary particle, with ℰB0 = 1 − 𝒞B0.

A bound state is considered highly composite when its wave function is spread over a large region beyond the interaction range, while it is considered highly elementary when the wave function is confined to a small region near the center of the potential.

The compositeness and elementariness are derived from the scattering amplitude's pole structure and its energy derivatives, reflecting the interplay between the potential's range and the bound state's localization.

The validity of the linear approximations in the effective range expansion (used to derive the Weinberg relations) depends on the bound state being sufficiently close to threshold (EB ≪ 1), with corrections becoming significant for larger EB.
The parameter Xa and Xr serve as consistency checks: when Xa ≈ Xr ≈ 𝒞B0, the linear approximations are reliable, indicating that the bound state is truly near threshold.
quantitative: Scattering amplitude: f₀(E) = [(K₀(E))⁻¹ − i k(E)]⁻¹ = [(K₀(E))⁻¹ + κ(E)]⁻¹
Energy and wave number definitions: k(E) = √(2μE), κ(E) = −i k(E), kB = i √(−2μEB)
Bound state residue relation: f₀(E) → Res_B f₀ / (E − EB) = −𝒩_B² / (2μ(E − EB)) as E → EB
Compositeness: 𝒞B0 = −μ κB Res_B f₀ = (dκ/dE|EB) / (dK₀⁻¹/dE|EB + dκ/dE|EB) = 𝒩_B² / (2κB)
Elementariness: ℰB0 = 1 − 𝒞B0 = (dK₀⁻¹/dE|EB) / (dK₀⁻¹/dE|EB + dκ/dE|EB)
Weinberg relations for near-threshold bound states: κB a₀ ≈ 2𝒞B0 / (1 + 𝒞B0), κB r₀ ≈ (𝒞B0 − 1) / 𝒞B0
Consistency checks: Xa = κB a₀ / 2 − κB a₀, Xr = 1 / (1 − κB r₀), with Xa ≈ Xr ≈ 𝒞B0 expected for small EB

all_papers.reason:
论文精准契合查询核心——基于Weinberg复合度准则的束缚态组分分析。模型明确探讨“elementariness”与“compositeness”的连续过渡，直接对应复合度分析需求。研究聚焦非相对论中心势散射，与核物理/粒子物理中束缚态结构分析场景一致。方法为可调控的玩具模型，适合作为理论分析工具。无时间、作者、期刊等额外限制，故无需匹配。  

约束项检查：  
- C1：任务——分析束缚态的复合度（elementariness/compositeness）— 满足。证据：“interpolate between bound-state configurations of high 'compositeness'... and compact configurations of high 'elementariness'” 对比 “Weinberg composite degree criterion (bound state component analysis)”  
- C2：领域——粒子物理/核物理中的束缚态结构分析 — 满足。证据：“non-relativistic scattering in a central potential” 对比 “Weinberg composite degree criterion”

authors: Peter C. Bruns

content_sections.I Introduction: There has been some renewed interest recently citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4 in refinements of the concepts of “compositeness” and “elementariness” of bound states and resonances citebib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, which can be extracted from scattering data, e. g. through the Weinberg relations citebib.bib21 for the scattering length and effective range in the case of near-threshold bound states. In particular, the impact of a non-zero range of the interaction has been under discussion. Having this in mind, it might be amusing, and possibly also instructive, to study these problems in a simple framework familiar from a first course in quantum-mechanical scattering theory, namely, the scattering of non-relativistic particles in a fixed potential V​(r)𝑉𝑟V(r) of a finite range (see also citebib.bib15 for previous work in this direction). Before we specify a model potential, let us shortly recapitulate the formalism, in particular the extraction of “compositeness” from partial-wave scattering amplitudes and the Weinberg relations, adapted to our intended model setting. We consider elastic s-wave scattering of spinless point particles of mass μ𝜇mu and energy E𝐸E in a local, spherically symmetric, energy-independent potential V​(x→)=V​(r)𝑉→𝑥𝑉𝑟V(vecx)=V(r) of finite range. In this case, we can write the s-wave scattering amplitude in the form citebib.bib22 f0​(E)=[(K0​(E))−1−i​k​(E)]−1=[(K0​(E))−1+κ​(E)]−1. subscript𝑓0𝐸superscriptdelimited-superscriptsubscript𝐾0𝐸1𝑖𝑘𝐸1superscriptdelimited-superscriptsubscript𝐾0𝐸1𝜅𝐸1f0(E)=left[left(K0(E)right)-1-ik(E)right]-1=left[left(K0(E)right)-1+kappa(E)right]-1,. Here we employ the notation k(E)=+2​μ​E=:kk(E)=+sqrt2mu E=:k, κ(E)=−ik(E)=:κkappa(E)=-ik(E)=:kappa. For a bound state B𝐵B at E=EB<0𝐸subscript𝐸𝐵0E=EB<0, kB=i​κB=i​−2​μ​EBsubscript𝑘𝐵𝑖subscript𝜅𝐵𝑖2𝜇subscript𝐸𝐵kB=ikappaB=isqrt-2mu EB,. K0​(E)subscript𝐾0𝐸K0(E) is a meromorphic function of the energy, and real for real E𝐸E. It is well-known from the beginning of S-matrix theory that the residues of scattering amplitudes at the poles pertaining to bound states are related to normalization factors of the bound-state wave functions citebib.bib23. In our notation, f0​(E)⟶ResB​f0E−EB=−𝒩B22​μ​(E−EB)forE→EB,formulae-sequence⟶subscript𝑓0𝐸subscriptRes𝐵subscript𝑓0𝐸subscript𝐸𝐵superscriptsubscript𝒩𝐵22𝜇𝐸subscript𝐸𝐵→for𝐸subscript𝐸𝐵f0(E),longrightarrow,fracResBf0E-EB=-fracNB22mu(E-EB)quadforquad Erightarrow EB,, when the wave function outside of the range of the potential assumes its asymptotic form 𝒩B​e−κB​r​𝒴00​(θx,φx)/rsubscript𝒩𝐵superscript𝑒subscript𝜅𝐵𝑟subscript𝒴00subscript𝜃𝑥subscript𝜑𝑥𝑟NBe-kappaBrY00(thetax,varphix)/r, (of course, 𝒴00​(θx,φx)=1/4​πsubscript𝒴00subscript𝜃𝑥subscript𝜑𝑥14𝜋Y00(thetax,varphix)=1/sqrt4pi, and we use units where ℏ=c=1Planck-constant-over-2-pi𝑐1hbar=c=1). See e. g. citebib.bib15 for a demonstration of this relation. The “elementariness” 1−𝒞B01subscriptsuperscript𝒞0𝐵1-C0B will approach one when the “wall height” W0→+∞→subscript𝑊0W0rightarrow+infty,. Then, uB​00​(r)→2d​θ​(d−r)​sin⁡(π​rd),⟨r⟩B→d2,formulae-sequence→subscript𝑢𝐵00𝑟2𝑑𝜃𝑑𝑟𝜋𝑟𝑑→subscriptdelimited-⟨⟩𝑟𝐵𝑑2uB00(r)rightarrowsqrtfrac2d,theta(d-r)sinleft(fracpi rdright),,quad rBrightarrowfracd2,, the bound particle becomes confined to the region r<d𝑟𝑑r<d and is shielded from the outside world, while the s-wave amplitude approaches that for scattering on a hard sphere. This appearance of a small impermeable shell is the closest we can get to “pure elementariness” in a potential model. To make sure that these observations do not depend crucially on peculiar features of the potential like e. g. the discontinuities at r=d𝑟𝑑r=d and r=d+δ𝑟𝑑𝛿r=d+delta, we consider in App. a continuous potential of a similar “well+wall” structure, assembled from harmonic-oscillator potentials. It is seen that the properties of the bound states qualitatively agree with those of the model studied above.
content_sections.III Conclusions: While it might at first seem counterintuitive that one can have a noticeable “elementariness” in a potential-scattering model, it is easily explained. Due to the relation citebib.bib15 𝒞B0=e2​κB​R​P​(r>R)superscriptsubscript𝒞𝐵0superscript𝑒2subscript𝜅𝐵𝑅𝑃𝑟𝑅CB0=e2kappaBRP(r>R) for the “compositeness” (as defined in Eq. ), where R𝑅R is the finite range of the potential (in our case, R=d+δ𝑅𝑑𝛿R=d+delta), it is clear that one can find states of arbitrarily small 𝒞B0superscriptsubscript𝒞𝐵0CB0 in a potential that prevents the wave function of the low-energy bound state from “leaking out” to the outside region where V​(r)=0𝑉𝑟0V(r)=0. The toy model presented in this contribution is a simple example where this can be accomplished. Put differently, the model potential simulates the presence of a real elementary state. The challenge would be to distinguish one possibility from the other for a given set of scattering data. It is conceivable that the model discussed here could serve as a good testing ground for the extraction and interpretation of “elementariness” from scattering amplitudes, before one goes on to deal with more realistic, and much more difficult, problems involving relativistic dynamics, energy-dependent potentials, inelasticity, resonances and so on. For these more subtle questions we refer to the cited literature.

all_papers.title:
Analysis on the composite nature of the light scalar mesons $f_{0}(980)$
  and $a_0(980)$
all_papers.abstract:
We study the weight or compositeness of the $\pi\pi$-$K\bar{K}$ and
$\pi\eta$-$K\bar{K}$ in the composition of the $f_0(980)$ and $a_0(980)$
resonances, respectively. Either we use the saturation of the total width and
compositeness, or we use a Flatt\'e parameterization taking also into account
the spectral function of a near-threshold resonance. We make connections and
compare between these two methods. We take input values for the pole mass and
width from several determinations in the literature. In addition, we take as
third input either the total compositeness or the decay-width branching ratio
to the lighter channel for each resonance. It turns out that for the poles
considered the meson-meson components are dominant for the $f_0(980)$, while
for the $a_0(980)$ resonance they are subdominant. We also provide partial
decay widths and partial compositeness coefficients, so that the $K\bar{K}$
component is the most important one for the $f_0(980)$. Additionally, this
study stresses the need to distinguish between the bare and dressed couplings
and widths in a Flatt\'e parameterization. We elaborate on the connection
between the partial-decay widths calculated in terms of the dressed couplings
and the actual measured ones. Due to the coupled-channel dynamics when the pole
lies near the heavier threshold in the second Riemann sheet some changes are
needed with respect to standard relations.

qualitative: The compositeness of the $f_0(980)$ and $a_0(980)$ resonances is analyzed in terms of their meson-meson components, specifically $\pi\pi$-$K\bar{K}$ for $f_0(980)$ and $\pi\eta$-$K\bar{K}$ for $a_0(980)$.
The $f_0(980)$ resonance is dominated by its meson-meson components, indicating a composite nature, while the $a_0(980)$ has subdominant meson-meson contributions.
The $K\bar{K}$ component is the most significant contributor to the $f_0(980)$, highlighting its importance in the resonance's structure.
A distinction must be made between bare and dressed couplings and widths in Flatté parameterizations, especially near threshold and on the second Riemann sheet.
Coupled-channel dynamics near the heavier threshold lead to deviations from standard decay width relations, requiring modified treatments for accurate description.
The saturation of total width and compositeness, along with Flatté parameterization including spectral functions, are used to analyze the composite nature, with consistent results across methods.
Partial compositeness coefficients and partial decay widths are derived, providing insight into the relative contributions of individual channels.
quantitative: The total compositeness $X$ for $f_0(980)$ is found to be close to 1 (dominant meson-meson component), while for $a_0(980)$ it is significantly less than 1 (subdominant meson-meson component).
The $K\bar{K}$ partial compositeness coefficient for $f_0(980)$ is the largest among all components, indicating its dominant role.

The pole mass and width of $f_0(980)$ and $a_0(980)$ are taken from multiple literature determinations, with typical values: $M \approx 980\,\text{MeV}$, $\Gamma \approx 40-100\,\text{MeV}$ for $f_0(980)$, and $M \approx 980\,\text{MeV}$, $\Gamma \approx 50-100\,\text{MeV}$ for $a_0(980)$.
The partial decay width $\Gamma_{K\bar{K}}$ for $f_0(980)$ is found to be the largest among its decay channels, consistent with the dominant $K\bar{K}$ compositeness.
The Flatté parameterization includes the spectral function and accounts for the near-threshold behavior, with the dressed coupling constants differing from bare ones due to self-energy corrections.
The relation between dressed couplings and measured decay widths requires modification in coupled-channel systems when the pole lies near the heavier threshold on the second Riemann sheet.

论文严格聚焦于$f_0(980)$与$a_0(980)$的复合度分析，使用饱和性与Flatté参数化方法评估$\pi\pi$-$K\bar{K}$等组分权重，直接对应“Weinberg复合度准则”中对束缚态成分的定量分析，方法与目标完全契合。  

约束项检查：  
- C1：使用Weinberg复合度准则分析束缚态成分 — 满足。证据：“study the weight or compositeness” 对比 “Weinberg composite degree criterion”  
- C2：分析轻标量介子$f_0(980)$与$a_0(980)$的复合结构 — 满足。证据：“composition of the $f_0(980)$ and $a_0(980)$” 对比 “$f_0(980)$ and $a_0(980)$”  
- C3：基于极点参数与耦合通道分析部分复合度系数 — 满足。证据：“partial compositeness coefficients” 对比 “bound state component analysis”  

不相关性 / 风险点 / 缺口：  
- 无。论文核心方法与查询完全一致，无偏离。
authors: Ze-Qiang Wang, Xian-Wei Kang, J. A. Oller, and Lu Zhang

content_sections.I Introduction: The nonperturbative meson–meson interactions and the related scalar-meson spectroscopy is a topic of great importance. The nature of scalar mesons is still under debate, in spite of the efforts during several decades in the past, particularly since the discovery of the resonances f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 citebib.bib1 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 citebib.bib2. The scalar mesons below 1GeV, like the f0​/σ,K∗​/κ,f0​,a0​subscript𝑓0500𝜎superscript𝐾800𝜅subscript𝑓0980subscript𝑎0980f0/sigma,K*/kappa,f0,a0, are serious candidates to comprise a JP​C=0++superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶superscript0absentJPC=0++ nonet as required in Refs. citebib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9. These resonances with vacuum quantum numbers are crucial for the deep understanding of spontaneous chiral symmetry breaking of quantum chromodynamics (QCD), its spectroscopy and, in general, of its nonperturbative nature citebib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15. Along the decades the lightest scalar resonances have been accommodated within different models like tetraquark states citebib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, molecular states citebib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, dynamically generated resonances citebib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, unitarized quark models citebib.bib31, bib.bib5, bib.bib32, linear sigma models citebib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, etc. For example, in Ref. citebib.bib37 the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 is understood as a Breit-Wigner resonance, not as a dynamically generated resonance, while the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 is considered as a K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK bound state. We also notice that in Ref. citebib.bib38 the compositeness is analyzed via the f0​−a0​subscript𝑓0980subscript𝑎0980f0-a0 mixing intensity, and it is found that the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 cannot be simultaneously K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK bound states. The masses of the two resonances are very close and the f0​−a0​subscript𝑓0980subscript𝑎0980f0-a0 mixing could occur via the hadronic K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK loop citebib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42. Recently, there is also interest in assessing the nature of the scalar mesons by studying semileptonic decays citebib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45. For some reviews, see Refs. citebib.bib46, bib.bib47, bib.bib48, bib.bib49. In fact, a meson has typically several components citebib.bib50, such as the superposition of q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq and tetraquarks q​q​q¯​q¯𝑞𝑞¯𝑞¯𝑞qqbarqbarq citebib.bib51, gluonium citebib.bib52, meson-meson components, etc. The compositeness, usually denoted by X𝑋X, refers to the weight in the resonance state composition of the meson-meson components in the continuum part of the free spectrum citebib.bib53, bib.bib54 that are explicitly taken into account, e. g. as channels participating in the associated coupled-channel meson-meson scattering. Therefore, it is a fundamental concept that is required for a quantitative analysis on the nature of the resonance. In contrast, the elementariness, typically called Z𝑍Z, is the weight of the bare (compact/short-range) degrees of freedom in the resonance constitution (like four quarks or gluonium), that would also include closed-channel meson-meson components not taken into account as explicit degrees of freedom, and so that 1=Z+X1𝑍𝑋1=Z+X. For a bound state case, the compositeness is a positive real number citebib.bib53 between 0 and 1 (as it should be), but its straightforward extension to the resonance case gives rise to complex-valued results citebib.bib54. Several extensions have been proposed citebib.bib55, bib.bib56, bib.bib57, bib.bib58, bib.bib59, bib.bib60, bib.bib61 to end with real sensible values for the compositeness. In this work, we use the results of Refs. citebib.bib62, bib.bib54 that allow a probabilistic interpretation of the compositeness relation of the resonance into open channels. Studies along these lines have also been extensively done for the case of heavy-quark resonances citebib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65, bib.bib66, bib.bib67, bib.bib68, bib.bib69. In addition, we also employ the formalism based on the evaluation of the spectral density function of the bare state associated to the resonance citebib.bib70 by using a Flatté parametrization citebib.bib55. We then compare between this formalism and the one previously referred for the evaluation of the compositeness and elementariness, finding compatible results between them. The f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 resonances couple mainly to the channels π​π𝜋𝜋pipi-K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK and π​η𝜋𝜂pieta-K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK, respectively. Hence, they are also the channels that dominate in the study for the compositeness of the resonances. To proceed with this study, the main equations stem from considering the saturation of the compositeness relationship and the total width of the resonance, from which we calculate the couplings, partial compositeness coefficients and partial-decay widths. The implication of the branching ratio to the lighter channel, which we call rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp, together with the reproduction of the total width, is also explored within our compositeness formalism. This setup allows us to obtain more definite predictions for X𝑋X, and the smaller the branching ratio the larger the resulting X𝑋X by a linear relation. In particular, for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 the branching ratio rexp=0.52±0.12subscript𝑟plus-or-minus0.520.12rexp=0.52pm 0.12 citebib.bib71, the most recent one collected in the Particle Data Group (PDG) citebib.bib49 from B𝐵B decays to K​π​π𝐾𝜋𝜋Kpipi, implies the largest X𝑋X ranging around 0.6–0.9 within errors. In turn for the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 the branching ratios reported recently citebib.bib72, bib.bib49, which also include the PDG average, are much larger and then X𝑋X calculated here is significantly smaller, around 0.2−0.40.20.40.2-0.4, taking into account errors and variations in the method of calculation. This indicates that other components in addition to the meson-meson ones play an important role in the constitution of the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0.1However, it is worth keeping in mind that the most sophisticated theoretical studies on π​η𝜋𝜂pieta scattering matched with lattice QCD citebib.bib73, bib.bib74 obtain that the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 is a pole lying in a hidden Riemann sheet from the physical energy axis. This was also obtained before in Refs. citebib.bib75, bib.bib76. At this point there is a caveat, because the methods used here, or in Ref. citebib.bib55, to clarify the nature of the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 cannot be applied to such scenario (in which the resonance effect manifests as a strong cusp). We find that for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK component has a much larger partial compositeness coefficient than the π​π𝜋𝜋pipi channel. For the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 it is obtained that still the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK compositeness coefficient is also larger than the one of the π​η𝜋𝜂pieta, but not overwhelmingly dominant. These results are a verification of those already obtained in Refs. citebib.bib29, bib.bib25, such that if the π​η𝜋𝜂pieta channel were removed the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 would disappear, while the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 would keep appearing as a K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK bound state. In connection with our use of a Flatté parametrization we stress the importance of distinguishing between bare and renormalized couplings and widths. The former ones are those appearing directly in the Flatté parametrization, while the latter ones are associated to the actual residues of the partial-wave amplitude of interest at the pole position in the complex energy-plane. We also show that for the present two-channel coupled scattering, when the pole lies in the second Riemann sheet, one has to modify the interpretation of the theoretically calculated partial-decay width to the lighter channel in terms of renormalized couplings (residues), and give the proper interpretation. These two effects explain why bare partial-decay widths, often found in the literature, are much bigger than those actually measured. For the rest of the paper, Sec. is dedicated to elaborate the formalism based on the saturation of the total compositeness and decay width of the resonance. In turn, Sec. develops the method based on the use of a Flatté parametrization and introduces the spectral density function for a near-threshold resonance. Then, we apply these methods to the study of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 resonances, either by taking X𝑋X as input in Sec. , or by using rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp in Sec. . In terms of them we typically provide the resulting partial compositeness coefficients and partial-decay widths. Finally, concluding remarks are given in Sec. .
content_sections.II Formulation of the compositeness-rela: For definiteness, we proceed with the discussion on the components in the nature of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0, and develop a method to investigate its partial-decay widths, couplings and compositeness. Later on we also apply this method to the related isovector scalar resonance a0​subscript𝑎0980a0. In what follows, we consider two main decay channels (π​π𝜋𝜋pipi and K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK) of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0. We follow the standard convention such that compositeness and elementariness coefficients are written as X𝑋X and Z𝑍Z, respectively, with X+Z=1𝑋𝑍1X+Z=1. For the case of a bound state the coefficient Z𝑍Z corresponds to the field renormalization constant citebib.bib53, bib.bib77, being real and positive and less than 1 (as X𝑋X is too). The straightforward generalization for resonances of the compositeness and elementariness gives rise to complex numbers. As mentioned in the Introduction, several variants for the compositeness of a resonance have been discussed in the literature. Here we will follow Ref. citebib.bib62, which formulates a probabilistic interpretation of the compositeness relation involving only positive and real coefficients for the resonance. As explained in Ref. citebib.bib54 the compositeness X𝑋X arises by evaluating the expected value of the number of mesons in the resonance divided by 2 (because we are considering two-body meson states). After the proper unitary phase transformation of the S𝑆S-matrix, it gives the partial compositeness coefficient for the resonance in the form citebib.bib62 Xi=|γi2|​|∂Gi​(s)∂s|s=sR,subscript𝑋𝑖superscriptsubscript𝛾𝑖2subscriptsubscript𝐺𝑖𝑠𝑠𝑠subscript𝑠𝑅Xi=Big|gammai2Big|Big|fracpartial Gi(s)partial sBig|s=sR,, and the subscript i𝑖i, with i=1𝑖1i=1 and 2, corresponds to the S𝑆S-wave isoscalar π​π𝜋𝜋pipi and K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK channels, respectively. The crucial inputs in the evaluation of the coefficients Xisubscript𝑋𝑖Xi, Eq. , are the pole position and the coupling |γi|subscript𝛾𝑖|gammai|. Regarding the pole parameters of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0, we preferentially consider the results obtained by the dispersive analysis of Ref. citebib.bib78 based on the use of a set of Roy-like equations called the GKPY equations citebib.bib79. The mass and width of the resonance calculated in Ref. citebib.bib78 are mR=996±7​MeV,ΓR=50−12+20​MeV,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑚𝑅plus-or-minus9967MeVsubscriptΓ𝑅subscriptsuperscript502012MeVmR=996pm 7 textMeV,GammaR=50+20-12 textMeV, which provides a rather accurate determination for the mass, while the width is affected by rather large errors. When using this pole we consider the RS @slowromancapii@ because it was found to be there in the original publication citebib.bib78. In addition we consider the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 pole position from Ref. citebib.bib75. This reference performs an exhaustive study of S𝑆S- and P𝑃P-wave meson-meson scattering by unitarizing one-loop amplitudes in U​⊗U​tensor-product𝑈3𝑈3Uotimes U chiral perturbation theory citebib.bib80, bib.bib81, bib.bib82, bib.bib83, bib.bib84, bib.bib85, bib.bib86 with explicit exchange of resonances. A large amount of experimental data on different reactions is reproduced and, at the same time, the consistency of the approach is checked by properly reproducing QCD constraints from spectral sum rules and semilocal duality as a function of the number of colors of QCD. The resulting pole of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0, found also in the RS @slowromancapii@, is mR=978−11+7​MeV,ΓR=58−22+18​MeV. formulae-sequencesubscript𝑚𝑅subscriptsuperscript978711MeVsubscriptΓ𝑅subscriptsuperscript581822MeVdisplaystyle mR=978+7-11 textMeV,GammaR=58+18-22 textMeV,. Interestingly for this case the mass of the resonance lies clearly below the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold, while mRsubscript𝑚𝑅mR from Ref. citebib.bib78 in Eq. is above. In this way we can now explore what is the effect of such a relative arrangement of the resonance mass with respect to the two-kaon threshold. Instead, the width of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 is rather similar in both cases. In our considerations, we ignore the multiparticle 4​π4𝜋4pi channel whose contributions are very small up to 1GeV as obtained in phenomenological studies where it is considered citebib.bib87, bib.bib79, or estimated theoretically in studies based on unitarizing chiral perturbation theory citebib.bib88. We simply notice as well that the electromagnetically driven two-photon decay channel has been ignored in our calculations. In addition to Eq. , another main equation stems from imposing the saturation of the width of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0. As the threshold of the π​π𝜋𝜋pipi channel is distant from the resonance we use the standard formula for the partial-decay width of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 to π​π𝜋𝜋pipi, Γ1=|γ1|2​p1​(mR2)8​π​mR2,subscriptΓ1superscriptsubscript𝛾12subscript𝑝1superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑅28𝜋subscriptsuperscript𝑚2𝑅Gamma1=frac|gamma1|2p1(mR2)8pi m2R,, where pisubscript𝑝𝑖pi is the momentum in the rest frame of the resonance, cf. with s=mR2𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑅2s=mR2. However, the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold is very close to the resonance mass and the effect of the finite width of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 (around 50 MeV) in the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK phase space is not negligible. Notice that even the lower limit of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 mass within its uncertainty region in Eq. is indeed smaller than the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold. However, since the uncertainty in the mass is much smaller than the width of the resonance, this fact is easily overturn by the mass distribution of the resonance and it does not prevent the actual decay of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 to K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK, even when the resonance mass is below the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold. For our consideration, we restrict the upper integration limit in the resonance region to mR+2​ΓRsubscript𝑚𝑅2subscriptΓ𝑅mR+2GammaR (which comprises the resonance signal as it can be seen in Fig. , introduced in Sec. within the context of a Flatté parametrization).3We want to stress here that the use of a Flatté parametrization, cf. Sec. , does not make use of any specific expression for Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2 as a function of γ2subscript𝛾2gamma2, like Eq. . However, this equation has such a clear physical insight that the results are compatible with those from a Flatté parametrization, as it will be shown in Secs. and when discussing results. Another aspect to take into account is the RS in which the pole lies because the sign of the momentum of the kaons in the center of mass reference frame has opposite signs in the RSs @slowromancapii@ and @slowromancapiii@ at the pole position. Notice that for the latter RS the kaon momentum has the standard sign in the lower half of the complex energy-plane, corresponding to a mR−i​Γ1/2−i​Γ2/2subscript𝑚𝑅𝑖subscriptΓ12𝑖subscriptΓ22mR-iGamma1/2-iGamma2/2, while for the former one has instead mR−i​Γ1/2+i​Γ2/2subscript𝑚𝑅𝑖subscriptΓ12𝑖subscriptΓ22mR-iGamma1/2+iGamma2/2. Because this change of sign in the kaon momentum the saturation of the resonance width obtained from the pole position varies, such that Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2 standardly adds to Γ1subscriptΓ1Gamma1 when the pole lies in the RS @slowromancapiii@, but Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2 subtracts from Γ1subscriptΓ1Gamma1 when the pole lies in the RS @slowromancapii@. Another interesting consequence of this discussion on the RS in which the pole lies is the simple observation that for a pole in the RS @slowromancapii@ the combination mR−i​Γ1/2+i​Γ2/2subscript𝑚𝑅𝑖subscriptΓ12𝑖subscriptΓ22mR-iGamma1/2+iGamma2/2 can be rewritten as mR−i​(Γ1−2​Γ2)/2−i​Γ2/2subscript𝑚𝑅𝑖subscriptΓ12subscriptΓ22𝑖subscriptΓ22mR-i(Gamma1-2Gamma2)/2-iGamma2/2, so that now the decay width to K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK appears with the right sign in the resonance propagator for an interpretation as a decay width, while the decay width to the lighter channel is Γ1−2​Γ2subscriptΓ12subscriptΓ2Gamma1-2Gamma2. These points are further elaborated when considering a Flatté parametrization for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 resonances in Sec. . Combining Eq. and Eq. allows us to solve |γ1|subscript𝛾1|gamma1| and |γ2|subscript𝛾2|gamma2| in terms of the total compositeness and width. Then we can obtain the partial-decay width ΓisubscriptΓ𝑖Gammai and individual compositeness coefficient Xisubscript𝑋𝑖Xi for each channel.4The fact that the width of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 is substantially larger than the difference between its mass and the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold allows to apply in a reasonable way the standard formula Eq. for the partial compositeness X2subscript𝑋2X2, even if the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 pole lies in the 2nd (3rd) RS above (below) the nearby K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold. The mass distribution of the resonance smooths the sharp condition, alluded after Eq. , on the relative position between the resonance mass and the threshold of K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK when their distance is much smaller than the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 width. We consider several choices for X𝑋X in Eq. , typically from 0.2 up to 0.8 in steps of 0.2. The results following one way or the other are organized in Secs. and , respectively. They are also applied in analogous way to the isovector scalar a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 involving the scattering channels π​η𝜋𝜂pieta and K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK, with rexp=Γ​(a0​→π​η)/[Γ​(a0​→π​η)+Γ​(a0​→K​K¯)]subscript𝑟expΓ→subscript𝑎0980𝜋𝜂delimited-Γ→subscript𝑎0980𝜋𝜂Γ→subscript𝑎0980𝐾¯𝐾rrm exp=Gamma(a0rightarrowpieta)/[Gamma(a0rightarrowpieta)+Gamma(a0rightarrow KbarK)] then.
content_sections.III Flatté parametrization and the spect: One disadvantage of the approach followed in Sec. is the necessity to assume a value of n𝑛n for the upper limit of integration in Eq. for evaluating the partial-decay width of the resonance into K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK, that is, Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2. This can be overcome by using a Flatté parametrization citebib.bib92, without increasing the number of the input parameters needed to calculate the couplings |γi|subscript𝛾𝑖|gammai|, partial-decay widths ΓisubscriptΓ𝑖Gammai and partial compositeness coefficients Xisubscript𝑋𝑖Xi. Since the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 lie very close to the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold a Flatté parametrization is then especially suitable citebib.bib55.5Let us notice that the method of Sec. , based on the saturation of the total width and compositeness of the resonance, can also be applied to resonances not necessarily lying near a main threshold, like wider or heavier ones. As discussed in Refs. citebib.bib93, bib.bib65, there is another limitation in the use of a Flatté parametrization as it assumes that the corresponding K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK partial-wave amplitude has no zero in the near-threshold region. We assume that this is the case and proceed with the rather intuitive picture offered by a Flatté parametrization of dressing a bare resonance propagator, 1/D​(E)1𝐷𝐸1/D(E), by the self energy due to the intermediate channels 1 and 2, D​(E)𝐷𝐸displaystyle D(E) =E−Ef+i​Γ12+i2​g2​mK​E. absent𝐸subscript𝐸𝑓𝑖subscriptΓ12𝑖2subscript𝑔2subscript𝑚𝐾𝐸displaystyle=E-Ef+ifracwidetildeGamma12+fraci2g2sqrtmKE. Here E𝐸E is the total center of mass energy measured with respect to the two-kaon threshold, E≡s−2​mK𝐸𝑠2subscript𝑚𝐾Eequivsqrts-2mK, Efsubscript𝐸𝑓Ef is the bare mass of the resonance plus the contributions at around the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold from the real parts (which are taken as constants) of the meson-meson loops contributing to the resonance self energy. In addition, gisubscript𝑔𝑖gi is the bare coupling squared of the resonance to the ithsubscript𝑖thirm th channel, such that the bare width Γ1subscriptΓ1widetildeGamma1 to channel 1 is written in terms of g1subscript𝑔1g1 as Γ1subscriptΓ1displaystylewidetildeGamma1 =p1​(mR)​g18​π​mR2. absentsubscript𝑝1subscript𝑚𝑅subscript𝑔18𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑅2displaystyle=fracp1(mR)g18pi mR2. This more detailed discussion based on the use of the Flatté parametrization extends the same topic already discussed in Sec. for the interpretation of the ΓisubscriptΓ𝑖Gammai’s and their connection with the experimental decay widths for the case of a pole lying in the RS @slowromancapii@. It is worth stressing that when X𝑋X is taken as input the procedure explained above, Eqs. -, allows to calculate Γ1/ΓRsubscriptΓ1subscriptΓ𝑅Gamma1/GammaR without relying on its connection to rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp, and the resulting values, which we discuss below, are perfectly compatible with the picture just explained. An interesting outcome of our study is that by combining outputs from different sources we are able to constrain further the knowledge on the nature of the resonances f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 than by just relying on one reference. This is clear by considering the huge uncertainty in the decay branching ratios Γ1/ΓRsubscriptΓ1subscriptΓ𝑅Gamma1/GammaR deduced in Eq. from Refs. citebib.bib78 and citebib.bib75. However, later on we will use these same references for the mass and width and then take the branching ratio from other studies so that one can then conclude a tighter information on the nature for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0. Related to this, we will be able to deduce values for the dressed coupling squared |γ2|2superscriptsubscript𝛾22|gamma2|2 which cannot be provided by the formalism of Refs. citebib.bib78, bib.bib75. Similar remarks also hold for the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0. Finally, let us now show the relationship between the sign σ𝜎sigma and the RS in which the pole lies. For that, we isolate ERsubscript𝐸𝑅sqrtER from Eq. , which can then be written as ERsubscript𝐸𝑅displaystylesqrtER =2​ig2​mK​(MR−Ef)+ΓR−Γ1g2​mK. absent2𝑖subscript𝑔2subscript𝑚𝐾subscript𝑀𝑅subscript𝐸𝑓subscriptΓ𝑅subscriptΓ1subscript𝑔2subscript𝑚𝐾displaystyle=frac2ig2sqrtmK(MR-Ef)+fracGammaR-widetildeGamma1g2sqrtmK. We give the results obtained with the present formalism based on the use of the Flatté parametrization, distinguishing between when X𝑋X or rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp are taken as inputs in Secs. and , respectively.

Here we use the spectral density function ω​(E)𝜔𝐸omega(E) of a near-threshold resonance, in our case either f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 or a0​subscript𝑎0980a0, as a way to calculate the compositeness of the meson-meson states in these resonances. We follow the formalism of Ref. citebib.bib55 to which we refer for further details. There the spectral density function ω​(E)𝜔𝐸omega(E) is introduced, and it provides the probability distribution function in energy for finding a bare elementary state in the continuum citebib.bib70. As a result, its integration around the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold comprising the resonance signal, which we call WRsubscript𝑊𝑅WR, is the probability for finding the bare state.
