Therefore, in the region ρ0>−12​π2/Λ3subscript𝜌012superscript𝜋2superscriptΛ3tilderho0>-12pi2/tildeLambda3, there is one-to-one correspondence between resubscript𝑟𝑒tildere and ρ0subscript𝜌0tilderho0. While the lower limit of resubscript𝑟𝑒tildere is −∞-infty, the upper bound of resubscript𝑟𝑒tildere [Eq. ] is given by the ρ0→+∞→subscript𝜌0tilderho0to+infty limit. Hence the values of resubscript𝑟𝑒tildere are restricted in −∞≤resubscript𝑟𝑒displaystyle-inftyleqtildere ≤12π​Rint−12​Rint2+3​π​Rint3. absent12𝜋subscript𝑅int12superscriptsubscript𝑅int23𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑅int3displaystyleleqfrac12pitildeRrm int-12tildeRrm int2+3pitildeRrm int3. This upper bound is known as the Wigner boundcitebib.bib19. We note that resubscript𝑟𝑒tildere is always negative in the zero-range limit Rint→0→subscript𝑅int0tildeRrm intto 0 because the Wigner bound becomes re=0subscript𝑟𝑒0tildere=0. To study the applicable region of the weak-binding relations, we first discuss the behavior of the compositeness by varying the effective range resubscript𝑟𝑒tildere with a fixed interaction range Rintsubscript𝑅inttildeRrm int. We plot the central values of the compositeness Xcsubscript𝑋𝑐Xc (solid lines), the uncertainty bands in Eq. (gray bands), and the exact values of the compositeness Xexactsubscript𝑋exactXrm exact (dashed lines) as the functions of resubscript𝑟𝑒tildere with Rint=0.1subscript𝑅int0.1tildeRrm int=0.1 [Figs. (a) and (b)] and with Rint=0.5subscript𝑅int0.5tildeRrm int=0.5 [Figs. (c) and (d)]. This figure is plotted slightly below the Wigner bound (re≈0.27subscript𝑟𝑒0.27tildereapprox 0.27 with Rint=0.1subscript𝑅int0.1tildeRrm int=0.1 and re≈0.09subscript𝑟𝑒0.09tildereapprox 0.09 with Rint=0.5subscript𝑅int0.5tildeRrm int=0.5). Figures (a) and (c) show the case with ξ=ξeff𝜉subscript𝜉effxi=xirm eff, and Figs. (b) and (d) with ξ=ξint𝜉subscript𝜉intxi=xirm int. In this figure, the central value Xcsubscript𝑋𝑐Xc [Eq. ] deviates from Xexact=1subscript𝑋exact1Xrm exact=1 with the increase of |re|subscript𝑟𝑒|tildere|. In Figs. (a) and (c), the uncertainty bands by ξeffsubscript𝜉effxirm eff also increase, and therefore the exact value Xexact=1subscript𝑋exact1Xrm exact=1 is always contained in the uncertainty bands by ξeffsubscript𝜉effxirm eff. In other words, the validity condition is always satisfied with ξ=ξeff𝜉subscript𝜉effxi=xirm eff. We check that the same tendencies hold with other Rint≤0.5subscript𝑅int0.5tildeRrm intleq 0.5, and the validity condition by ξeffsubscript𝜉effxirm eff is always satisfied in this region. However, in Figs. (b) and (d), Xusubscript𝑋𝑢Xu becomes smaller than Xexactsubscript𝑋exactXrm exact at a large |re|subscript𝑟𝑒|tildere|. For example, Xexactsubscript𝑋exactXrm exact is not contained in the region re≲−0.2less-than-or-similar-tosubscript𝑟𝑒0.2tilderelesssim-0.2 with Rint=0.1subscript𝑅int0.1tildeRrm int=0.1 [Fig. (b)]. Therefore, the validity condition by ξintsubscript𝜉intxirm int is not satisfied in the large-|re|subscript𝑟𝑒|tildere| region. This indicates that X𝑋X cannot be estimated by the previous weak-binding relation with Rtyp=Rintsubscript𝑅typsubscript𝑅intRrm typ=Rrm int when the system has a large |re|subscript𝑟𝑒|tildere|. We now discuss the applicable regions of the weak-binding relations in the Rintsubscript𝑅inttildeRrm int-resubscript𝑟𝑒tildere plane. In Fig. , we first plot the Wigner bound and denote the region above it as region III which is not realized in this model. The remaining region is divided into regions I and II by the boundary at which the validity condition by ξintsubscript𝜉intxirm int is violated. The validity condition by ξintsubscript𝜉intxirm int is satisfied in region I, but not in region II. The correction terms in the improved weak-binding relation are determined by the dotted lines re=±Rintsubscript𝑟𝑒plus-or-minussubscript𝑅intre=pm Rrm int. In region I, both the previous and improved relations are applicable, because the validity conditions by ξeffsubscript𝜉effxirm eff and ξintsubscript𝜉intxirm int are satisfied. However, in region II, because the validity condition by ξintsubscript𝜉intxirm int is violated, the previous weak-binding relation with Rtyp=Rintsubscript𝑅typsubscript𝑅intRrm typ=Rrm int cannot be used. In region II, however, the improved relation is applicable because the validity condition by ξeffsubscript𝜉effxirm eff is satisfied and Rtyp=Reffsubscript𝑅typsubscript𝑅effRrm typ=Rrm eff for Reff>Rintsubscript𝑅effsubscript𝑅intRrm eff>Rrm int. From these discussions, we find that the applicable region of the improved weak-binding relation (region I+IIIIIrm I+rm II) is larger than the previous relation (regionI). We also consider the precision of the estimated compositeness by calculating the magnitude of the uncertainty E¯¯𝐸barE [Eq. ] in this model. In Fig. , we show the magnitude of the uncertainty E¯¯𝐸barE by ξeffsubscript𝜉effxirm eff [Fig. (a)] and by ξintsubscript𝜉intxirm int [Fig. (b)] in the Rintsubscript𝑅inttildeRrm int-resubscript𝑟𝑒tildere plane. The irrelevant regions (above the Wigner bound or Xl>1subscript𝑋𝑙1Xl>1) are filled in black. The unfilled regions correspond to E¯≤0.5¯𝐸0.5barEleq 0.5 where the meaningful estimation is possible. In Fig. (b), the change of the behavior of E¯¯𝐸barE at the boundary of the applicable region of the previous weak-binding relation can be explained by the difference of the definition of E¯¯𝐸barE: E¯=1−Xl¯𝐸1subscript𝑋𝑙barE=1-Xl above the boundary and E¯=Xu−Xl¯𝐸subscript𝑋𝑢subscript𝑋𝑙barE=Xu-Xl below the boundary. In Fig. (a), E¯¯𝐸barE by ξeffsubscript𝜉effxirm eff becomes smaller by decreasing the magnitude of the effective range |re|subscript𝑟𝑒|re|. This is because the uncertainty band increases with |re|subscript𝑟𝑒|re| in Figs. (a) and (c). Figure(a) also shows that E¯¯𝐸barE is stable against the variation of Rintsubscript𝑅inttildeRrm int. In fact, comparing with the uncertainty bands at the same resubscript𝑟𝑒tildere in Figs. (a) and (c), we find that E¯=1−Xl¯𝐸1subscript𝑋𝑙barE=1-Xl is not so sensitive to Rintsubscript𝑅intRrm int. When we compare E¯¯𝐸barE at positive and negative resubscript𝑟𝑒tildere with a fixed Rintsubscript𝑅inttildeRrm int, E¯¯𝐸barE with positive resubscript𝑟𝑒tildere is smaller. For example, E¯≈0.4¯𝐸0.4barEapprox 0.4 for (Rint,re)≈(0.2,0.2)subscript𝑅intsubscript𝑟𝑒0.20.2(tildeRrm int,tildere)approx(0.2,0.2) and E¯≈0.2¯𝐸0.2barEapprox 0.2 for (Rint,re)≈(0.2,−0.2)subscript𝑅intsubscript𝑟𝑒0.20.2(tildeRrm int,tildere)approx(0.2,-0.2). This reason is shown in Figs. (a) and (c). In the re>0subscript𝑟𝑒0tildere>0 region, a large part of the uncertainty band exceeds unity because the central value Xc>1subscript𝑋𝑐1Xc>1, and therefore the remaining E¯¯𝐸barE is small. In contrast, in the reRintsubscript𝑟𝑒subscript𝑅inttildere>tildeRrm int and re<−Rintsubscript𝑟𝑒subscript𝑅inttildere<-tildeRrm int and E¯¯𝐸barE by ξ=ξint𝜉subscript𝜉intxi=xirm int in −Rint<re<Rintsubscript𝑅intsubscript𝑟𝑒subscript𝑅int-tildeRrm int<tildere<tildeRrm int in Fig. . We consider that the compositeness X𝑋X is estimated meaningfully for E¯≲0.5less-than-or-similar-to¯𝐸0.5barElesssim 0.5 (unfilled region) as discussed in Sec. . In the Rint≲0.25less-than-or-similar-tosubscript𝑅int0.25tildeRrm intlesssim 0.25 region in Fig. , the meaningful estimation is possible in the re≳−0.25greater-than-or-equivalent-tosubscript𝑟𝑒0.25tilderegtrsim-0.25 region. In this region, the magnitude of the effective range |re|subscript𝑟𝑒|tildere| is sufficiently smaller than the scattering length. The boundary for the meaningful estimation is determined only by resubscript𝑟𝑒tildere, because the uncertainty is estimated by ξeffsubscript𝜉effxirm eff there. In the 0.25≲Rint≲0.4less-than-or-similar-to0.25subscript𝑅intless-than-or-similar-to0.40.25lesssimtildeRrm intlesssim 0.4 region, the boundary for the meaningful estimation is contained in the −Rint<re<Rintsubscript𝑅intsubscript𝑟𝑒subscript𝑅int-tildeRrm int<tildere<tildeRrm int region and depends on both resubscript𝑟𝑒tildere and Rintsubscript𝑅inttildeRrm int. The reason for this behavior was discussed above for E¯¯𝐸barE estimated by ξintsubscript𝜉intxirm int. In the Rint≳0.4greater-than-or-equivalent-tosubscript𝑅int0.4tildeRrm intgtrsim 0.4 region, E¯¯𝐸barE is always larger than 0.5 and we cannot estimate X𝑋X meaningfully because the interaction range is not negligible in comparison with the scattering length. We conclude that the internal structure can safely be determined by the weak-binding relation in the −0.25≲re≲0.25less-than-or-similar-to0.25subscript𝑟𝑒less-than-or-similar-to0.25-0.25lesssimtilderelesssim 0.25 and Rint≲0.25less-than-or-similar-tosubscript𝑅int0.25tildeRrm intlesssim 0.25 region.
content_sections.IV Numerical calculations IV.3 Resonance: In the previous section, we consider the applicable region of the weak-binding relations in the effective range model (ρ0≠0subscript𝜌00rho0neq 0 and g0=0subscript𝑔00g0=0) as a representative example with the exact value of the compositeness Xexact=1subscript𝑋exact1Xrm exact=1. In this section, we also discuss the case with Xexact<1subscript𝑋exact1Xrm exact<1, the resonance model (ρ0=0subscript𝜌00rho0=0 and g0≠0subscript𝑔00g0neq 0) with the finite cutoff ΛΛLambda, introduced in Sec. . As in Sec. two kinds of uncertainties (ξeffsubscript𝜉effxirm eff and ξintsubscript𝜉intxirm int) are considered in this model because of the finite effective range. The physical quantities are determined from the cutoff ΛΛLambda and three bare parameters g0,λ0subscript𝑔0subscript𝜆0g0,lambda0, and ν0subscript𝜈0nu0 in this model. (a) and (b)] and with ν0=0.5subscript𝜈00.5tildenu0=0.5, Rint=0.5subscript𝑅int0.5tildeRrm int=0.5 [Figs. (c) and (d)]. (a) and (c), because the uncertainty bands of ξeffsubscript𝜉effxirm eff become larger than the deviation of Xexactsubscript𝑋exactXrm exact from unity, Xexactsubscript𝑋exactXrm exact is always contained in the uncertainty region of ξeffsubscript𝜉effxirm eff. While Xexactsubscript𝑋exactXrm exact is smaller than unity, the central value Xcsubscript𝑋𝑐Xc is larger than unity in Fig. . By comparing Fig. (b) with Fig. , we find that the behaviors of Xcsubscript𝑋𝑐Xc are different from each other in contrast to Xexactsubscript𝑋exactXrm exact. Hence, it is expected that Xexactsubscript𝑋exactXrm exact goes beyond the uncertainty band at a smaller |re|subscript𝑟𝑒|tildere| with ν0=−0.5subscript𝜈00.5tildenu0=-0.5 than that with ν0=0.5subscript𝜈00.5tildenu0=0.5. We now plot the applicable regions of the weak-binding relations in the Rintsubscript𝑅inttildeRrm int-resubscript𝑟𝑒tildere plane for the resonance model with ν0=0.5subscript𝜈00.5tildenu0=0.5 [Fig. (a)] and ν0=−0.5subscript𝜈00.5tildenu0=-0.5 [Fig. (b)]. Those figures are plotted in the same manner as Fig. for the effective range model. Both the previous and improved relations are applicable in region I where the validity conditions by ξintsubscript𝜉intxirm int and ξeffsubscript𝜉effxirm eff are satisfied. In contrast, only the improved relation is applicable in region II where the validity condition by ξintsubscript𝜉intxirm int is not satisfied. Hence, the applicable region becomes large (I→→toI++II) with the range correction in the resonance model with Xexact<1subscript𝑋exact1Xrm exact<1 as in the same way with the effective range model (Xexact=1subscript𝑋exact1Xrm exact=1). By comparing the Figs. (a) and (b), we find that region I for ν0=−0.5subscript𝜈00.5tildenu0=-0.5 is smaller than that for ν0=0.5subscript𝜈00.5tildenu0=0.5. This can be understood by the above discussion on the behavior of the uncertainty bands and Xexactsubscript𝑋exactXrm exact in Figs. and .
content_sections.IV Numerical calculations IV.4 Higher or: In this section, we numerically examine the improvement of the central value of the compositeness Xcsubscript𝑋𝑐Xc with the effective range expansion in the previous workcitebib.bib11. By eliminating the scattering length a0subscript𝑎0a0 from Eq. with the pole condition up to the second order of the momentum k𝑘k in the effective range expansion, −re2​R2+1Rsubscript𝑟𝑒2superscript𝑅21𝑅displaystyle-fracre2R2+frac1R =1a0,absent1subscript𝑎0displaystyle=frac1a0, we obtain the central value of the compositeness expressed by the effective range resubscript𝑟𝑒re and the radius R𝑅R: Xc​(re,R)subscript𝑋𝑐subscript𝑟𝑒𝑅displaystyle Xc(re,R) =RR−re. absent𝑅𝑅subscript𝑟𝑒displaystyle=fracRR-re. To introduce the contributions of the higher order terms in the effective range expansion, we use X𝑋X expressed by resubscript𝑟𝑒re and the shape parameter Pssubscript𝑃𝑠Ps calculated from the residue of the bound-state pole in the scattering amplitude up to the fourth order of k𝑘kcitebib.bib11: Xc​(re,Ps)subscript𝑋𝑐subscript𝑟𝑒subscript𝑃𝑠displaystyle Xc(re,Ps) =[1−reR−PsR]−1. absentsuperscriptdelimited-1subscript𝑟𝑒𝑅subscript𝑃𝑠𝑅1displaystyle=left[1-fracreR-fracPsRright]-1. In this expression, the effect of Pssubscript𝑃𝑠Ps is implicitly included. If |Ps|/R3≪1much-less-thansubscript𝑃𝑠superscript𝑅31|Ps|/R3ll 1, we can neglect the higher order terms of Pssubscript𝑃𝑠Ps, and Eq. reduces to Eq. . Because Eq. is equivalent to Eq. under the condition of Eq. , Eq. would be an improvement of the weak-binding relation with the effects of Pssubscript𝑃𝑠Ps. We note that the difference between Eqs. and also expresses the effects of Pssubscript𝑃𝑠Ps because its contribution is neglected in Eq. . We discuss the contributions from the higher order terms of the effective range expansion by numerically comparing the improved central value [Eq. ] with the original one [Eq. ]. We use the effective range model with the finite cutoff (Rint=0.5subscript𝑅int0.5tildeRrm int=0.5) for the calculation. Figure shows the central values of the compositeness Xcsubscript𝑋𝑐Xc: the original one [Eq. , solid line], Xc​(re,R)subscript𝑋𝑐subscript𝑟𝑒𝑅Xc(re,R) [Eq. , dotted line], and Xc​(a0,re,R)subscript𝑋𝑐subscript𝑎0subscript𝑟𝑒𝑅Xc(a0,re,R) [Eq. , dash-dotted line]. We observe Xc=1subscript𝑋𝑐1Xc=1 at re=0subscript𝑟𝑒0tildere=0 in all cases. Equation induces Xc=1subscript𝑋𝑐1Xc=1 because the condition re=0subscript𝑟𝑒0tildere=0 corresponds to the zero-range model with a0=Rsubscript𝑎0𝑅a0=R. The higher order terms in the effective range expansion also vanish for re=0subscript𝑟𝑒0tildere=0 because the bare parameter ρ0subscript𝜌0tilderho0 becomes zero as shown in Fig. . Therefore, Eqs. and reduce to Eq. because the differences arise from the higher order terms, and Xc=1subscript𝑋𝑐1Xc=1 holds in all cases. We see from Fig. that the deviations of Eqs. and from Eq. arising from the Pssubscript𝑃𝑠Ps term, are of the same order. In contrast, the deviation of Xc​(a0,re,R)subscript𝑋𝑐subscript𝑎0subscript𝑟𝑒𝑅Xc(a0,re,R) from Xexact=1subscript𝑋exact1Xrm exact=1 is large in the re0subscript𝑟𝑒0tildere>0, it decreases for re<0subscript𝑟𝑒0tildere<0. Therefore, the contribution of the higher order terms in Eq. does not always improve the weak-binding relation. In fact, the differences in Fig. are much smaller than the width of the uncertainty bands in Fig. (d). Therefore, the order of the deviation of Eq. from Eq. is negligibly small, and the contribution of the higher order terms of the effective range expansion in Eq. does not affect the compositeness estimation. content_sections.V Application to physical systems: We apply the improved weak-binding relation with the range correction to the actual physical systems to investigate their internal structure. Because the weak-binding relation is based on the consequence of the low-energy universality, we can use it for any shallow bound states at arbitrary length scales. Here we apply the weak-binding relation to the hadrons, nuclei, and atomic systems. In Sec. , we summarize the observables and the interaction range in the systems to which we apply the weak-binding relation. We then discuss the importance of the range correction in these systems in Sec. . Finally, we estimate the compositeness to discuss the internal structure of the states in Sec. .
content_sections.V Application to physical systems V.1 Pr: We summarize the properties of the bound states we consider in Table. The deuteron d𝑑d in the p​n𝑝𝑛pn scattering is chosen as an example of the experimentally observed hadron. We focus on X​𝑋3872X, Ds​0∗​subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠02317D*s0, and Ds​1​subscript𝐷𝑠12460Ds1 as the representative candidates for the exotic hadronscitebib.bib35. These hadrons are regarded as a bound state of D0​D¯∗0superscript𝐷0superscript¯𝐷absent0D0barD*0, D​K𝐷𝐾DK, and D∗​Ksuperscript𝐷𝐾D*K, respectively.1While the wavefunction of the D𝐷D-meson pair coupled with X​𝑋3872X is the linear combination (D0​D¯∗0+D¯0​D∗0)/2superscript𝐷0superscript¯𝐷absent0superscript¯𝐷0superscript𝐷absent02(D0barD*0+barD0D*0)/sqrt2, here we denote it as D0​D¯∗0superscript𝐷0superscript¯𝐷absent0D0barD*0 for simplicity. In the recent lattice QCDcitebib.bib36, bib.bib37, N​Ω𝑁ΩNOmega and Ω​ΩΩΩOmegaOmega systems are predicted to have a shallow bound state in the S25superscriptsubscript𝑆255S2 and S01superscriptsubscript𝑆011S0 channel, respectively. We apply the weak-binding relation to those theoretically predicted states. As an example of the hypernuclei, we consider the HΛ3superscriptsubscriptHΛ33Lambdarm H as a bound state of d𝑑d and ΛΛLambda with a small binding energy. In addition to the hadrons and nuclei, the atomic states can also be considered. The He4superscriptHe44rm He dimer is the shallow bound state of two He4superscriptHe44rm He atoms. In Table, we show the masses of the two-body channel, m1subscript𝑚1m1 and m2subscript𝑚2m2, and the binding energy B𝐵B. The masses and the binding energies of the observed hadrons including d𝑑d are taken from the Particle Data Groupcitebib.bib35. The masses of K𝐾K, D𝐷D, and D∗superscript𝐷D* are averaged over the isospin multiplet. We adopt the binding energies B𝐵B of the N​Ω𝑁ΩNOmega and Ω​ΩΩΩOmegaOmega dibaryons by lattice QCDcitebib.bib36, bib.bib37. To be consistent with the calculation of B𝐵B, we use the masses in the N​Ω𝑁ΩNOmega and Ω​ΩΩΩOmegaOmega systems given by the same lattice setupcitebib.bib36, bib.bib37. The binding energy B𝐵B of HΛ3superscriptsubscriptHΛ33Lambdarm H is obtained from the emulsion datacitebib.bib38. We refer to Refs. citebib.bib39 and citebib.bib13 for the mass of the He4superscriptHe44rm He atom and the binding energy of the He4superscriptHe44rm He dimer, respectively. In general, the weak-binding relation cannot be applied to the systems with long-range interaction such as the Coulomb force. Because p𝑝p, ΩΩOmega, and d𝑑d are the charged particles in Table, the Coulomb force can be active in the N​Ω𝑁ΩNOmega and Ω​ΩΩΩOmegaOmega channels.2Because Ds​0∗​subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠02317D*s0 and Ds​1​subscript𝐷𝑠12460Ds1 have charge ±1plus-or-minus1pm 1, one of the particles in the two-body system is neutral. Therefore, the Coulomb force does not operate in these systems. When we choose the neutron ΩΩOmega scattering in the isospin doublet of the N​Ω𝑁ΩNOmega system, we can avoid the existence of the Coulomb interaction. However, the Coulomb interaction is unavoidable in the physical Ω​ΩΩΩOmegaOmega system because both particles are charged. Here we take the lattice results without the Coulomb interaction in Ref. citebib.bib37 to apply the weak-binding relation. We note that the properties of the physical p​Ω𝑝ΩpOmega, and Ω​ΩΩΩOmegaOmega dibaryons should be calculated with the Coulomb interaction. Strictly speaking, the weak-binding relation is applicable only to the stable bound states. Although X​𝑋3872X, Ds​0∗​subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠02317D*s0, Ds​1​subscript𝐷𝑠12460Ds1, and the N​Ω𝑁ΩNOmega dibaryon are unstable, those are expected to have a narrow decay width compared with that of typical unstable hadrons (≈100absent100approx 100 MeV). In fact, the widths of X​𝑋3872X, Ds​0∗​subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠02317D*s0, and Ds​1​subscript𝐷𝑠12460Ds1 are experimentally determined as 1.19±0.21plus-or-minus1.190.211.19pm 0.21 MeV, <3.8absent3.8<3.8 MeV and <3.5absent3.5<3.5 MeV, respectivelycitebib.bib35, and that of the N​Ω𝑁ΩNOmega dibaryon is calculated as ≈1absent1approx 1 MeV by the meson exchange modelcitebib.bib40. The narrow decay widths of Ds​0∗​→Ds​π→subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠02317subscript𝐷𝑠𝜋D*s0to Dspi and Ds​1​→Ds∗​π→subscript𝐷𝑠12460subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠𝜋Ds1to D*spi can be understood from the isospin breaking nature of the decays. The decay of the N​Ω𝑁ΩNOmega dibaryon is suppressed because of the large angular momentum ℓ=2ℓ2ell=2. Therefore, we apply the weak-binding relation to those states by neglecting the effects of the tiny decay widths. We also show the scattering length a0subscript𝑎0a0, the effective range resubscript𝑟𝑒re, the radius of the bound state R𝑅R, and the interaction range Rintsubscript𝑅intRrm int in Table. The radius R=1/2​μ​B𝑅12𝜇𝐵R=1/sqrt2mu B is calculated with μ=m1​m2/(m1+m2)𝜇subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2mu=m1m2/(m1+m2) and B𝐵B. content_sections.V Application to physical systems V.2 Co: Before the estimation of the compositeness, we discuss the significance of the range correction to the weak-binding relation by using the actual systems in Table. For this purpose, we use the applicable regions of the weak-binding relations in the effective range model (Fig. ). In Fig. , we compare the parameters listed in Table with the applicable regions in the effective range model in the Rintsubscript𝑅inttildeRrm int-resubscript𝑟𝑒tildere plane. The dotted lines stand for re=±Rintsubscript𝑟𝑒plus-or-minussubscript𝑅inttildere=pmtildeRrm int. For the previous weak-binding relation, the correction terms 𝒪​(Rtyp/R)𝒪subscript𝑅typ𝑅O(Rrm typ/R) are always estimated by Rtyp=Rintsubscript𝑅typsubscript𝑅intRrm typ=Rrm int. However, for the improved weak-binding relation with the range correction, we adopt the largest length scale among Rintsubscript𝑅intRrm int and Reffsubscript𝑅effRrm eff as Rtypsubscript𝑅typRrm typ. Hence, we find from Fig. that Rtyp=Rintsubscript𝑅typsubscript𝑅intRrm typ=Rrm int for Ds​0∗​subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠02317D*s0, Ds​1​subscript𝐷𝑠12460Ds1, and HΛ3superscriptsubscriptHΛ33Lambdarm H but Rtyp=Reffsubscript𝑅typsubscript𝑅effRrm typ=Rrm eff for all other states. This indicates that the range correction plays an important role for some physical systems. In fact, X​𝑋3872X is in the region where only the improved weak-binding relation with the range correction is applicable. Therefore, the previous relation may fail to estimate the compositeness of X​𝑋3872X, and we should use the improved relation for the reasonable estimation. In addition, because the N​Ω𝑁ΩNOmega dibaryon and He dimer lie close to the applicable boundary of the previous weak-binding relation, it is also expected that the range correction is quantitatively important for these states. We should, however, keep in mind that the applicable region in Fig. is the result of the specific model (the effective range model) and the applicable boundaries are model dependent. For the discussion of the meaningful estimation, we plot the parameters in Table in comparison with the magnitude of the uncertainty E¯¯𝐸barE in Rintsubscript𝑅inttildeRrm int-resubscript𝑟𝑒tildere plane in Fig. . Because all the states are contained in the region E¯≲0.5less-than-or-similar-to¯𝐸0.5barElesssim 0.5, we expect that meaningful estimations of the compositeness are possible for these states.
content_sections.V Application to physical systems V.3 Es: We now estimate the compositeness X𝑋X of the bound states listed in Table. We summarize the estimated uncertainties ξeff=|re|/Rsubscript𝜉effsubscript𝑟𝑒𝑅xirm eff=|re|/R and ξint=Rint/Rsubscript𝜉intsubscript𝑅int𝑅xirm int=Rrm int/R in Table. Here, we set Reff=|re|subscript𝑅effsubscript𝑟𝑒Rrm eff=|re| assuming that the coefficients of the higher order terms in the effective range expansion are of natural size. We then show the estimated compositeness with the uncertainty band with ξeffsubscript𝜉effxirm eff [X​(ξeff)𝑋subscript𝜉effX(xirm eff)] and ξintsubscript𝜉intxirm int [X​(ξint)𝑋subscript𝜉intX(xirm int)] in Table. In the last column we also show Rtypsubscript𝑅typRrm typ in the improved weak-binding relation. We can see that the central values of the compositeness Xcsubscript𝑋𝑐Xc are larger than unity except for X​𝑋3872X in Table. This is because the radius R𝑅R is smaller than the scattering length a0subscript𝑎0a0 in these states. As we discussed in Sec. , Xcsubscript𝑋𝑐Xc is larger than unity for a0>Rsubscript𝑎0𝑅a0>R. The relation between a0subscript𝑎0a0 and R𝑅R is also approximately determined by the sign of resubscript𝑟𝑒re. Neglecting the 𝒪​(k4)𝒪superscript𝑘4O(k4) terms in the effective range expansion, we obtain Eq. : a0subscript𝑎0displaystyle a0 =R​1−re/(2​R)+1. absent𝑅1subscript𝑟𝑒2𝑅1displaystyle=Rfrac1-re/(2R)+1. Because R>0𝑅0R>0, we obtain a0>Rsubscript𝑎0𝑅a0>R for positive re>0subscript𝑟𝑒0re>0, and a0Rsubscript𝑎0𝑅a0>R, which is expected to be realized with positive re>0subscript𝑟𝑒0re>0 when relation approximately holds. One may wonder that the central value Xc>1subscript𝑋𝑐1Xc>1 contradicts with the definition of the compositeness 0≤X≤10𝑋10leq Xleq 1. In fact, this problem for the deuteron partly motivates the works in Refs. citebib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29. From our viewpoint, this problem can be avoided by considering the uncertainty ξ𝜉xi as in Eq. as discussed below. Focusing on the N​Ω𝑁ΩNOmega dibaryon, we find that the lower limit of the compositeness estimated by ξintsubscript𝜉intxirm int is larger than unity [Xl​(ξint)=1.04subscript𝑋𝑙subscript𝜉int1.04Xl(xirm int)=1.04] from Table. Hence, the exact value of the compositeness of the N​Ω𝑁ΩNOmega dibaryon is not contained in the uncertainty band of X​(ξint)𝑋subscript𝜉intX(xirm int), and we cannot perform the meaningful estimation of the compositeness of the N​Ω𝑁ΩNOmega dibaryon with the previous weak-binding relation (Rtyp=Rintsubscript𝑅typsubscript𝑅intRrm typ=Rrm int). In fact, we have seen that the N​Ω𝑁ΩNOmega dibaryon exists near the boundary of the applicable region of the previous weak-binding relation in the effective range model as shown in Fig. . In the improved weak-binding relation with Eq. , we calculate compositeness with the uncertainty band as X​(ξeff)𝑋subscript𝜉effX(xirm eff) [X​(ξint)𝑋subscript𝜉intX(xirm int)] for Rtyp=Reffsubscript𝑅typsubscript𝑅effRrm typ=Rrm eff (Rtyp=Rintsubscript𝑅typsubscript𝑅intRrm typ=Rrm int). From the last column, we see that X​(ξint)𝑋subscript𝜉intX(xirm int) is adopted for the states Ds​0∗​subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠02317D*s0, Ds​1​subscript𝐷𝑠12460Ds1 and Λ3subscriptsuperscriptabsent3Λ3LambdaH, and X​(ξeff)𝑋subscript𝜉effX(xirm eff) for other states. By taking the region consistent with the definition 0≤X≤10𝑋10leq Xleq 1 in Eq. , we finally determine the compositeness X𝑋X as shown in Table. These results (0.5≤X≤10.5𝑋10.5leq Xleq 1) indicate that the composite component gives the largest fraction in the wavefunction for all states. In particular, the He4superscriptHe44rm He dimer is an almost purely composite state with a small fraction of the other components (≲7%less-than-or-similar-toabsentpercent7lesssim 7 %). However, the compositeness of X​𝑋3872X and Ds​1​subscript𝐷𝑠12460Ds1 can be as low as ≈approx0.5, which is the boundary of the composite dominance. Therefore, it is expected that the other components would play a substantial role in these states. We find that Eq. gives a reasonable estimation of the compositeness of the deuteron 0.74≤X≤10.74𝑋10.74leq Xleq 1, indicating its composite nature. The compositeness of the N​Ω𝑁ΩNOmega dibaryon is also meaningfully estimated thanks to the range correction. We compare our results with that of the previous works focusing on the deuteron d𝑑d, X​𝑋3872X, Ds​0∗​subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠02317D*s0 and Ds​1​subscript𝐷𝑠12460Ds1. The pioneering work for d𝑑d by Weinbergcitebib.bib9 concluded that d𝑑d was the composite state while the quantitative determination of the compositeness was not given. In Ref. citebib.bib47, the compositeness of d𝑑d was quantitatively calculated as 1.68−0.83+2.15subscriptsuperscript1.682.150.831.68+2.15-0.83 with the uncertainty estimation from the correction terms in the previous weak-binding relation. This result corresponds to X​(ξint)𝑋subscript𝜉intX(xirm int) in Table. By taking the range correction into account appropriately, we find the uncertainty band of the compositeness of d𝑑d, shown as X​(ξeff)𝑋subscript𝜉effX(xirm eff) in Table, is larger than that of Ref. citebib.bib47. In recent workscitebib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, the deuteron was found to be composite dominant. In particular, Ref. citebib.bib29 concluded that values of X𝑋X smaller than ≈0.7absent0.7approx 0.7 were very implausible. Those results are qualitatively consistent with our estimation 0.74≤X≤10.74𝑋10.74leq Xleq 1. The compositeness of Ds​0∗​subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠02317D*s0 and Ds​1​subscript𝐷𝑠12460Ds1 is discussed in Refs. citebib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib42. For Ds​0∗​subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠02317D*s0, Refs. citebib.bib28, citebib.bib29, and citebib.bib42 found that X>0.6𝑋0.6X>0.6, X>0.5𝑋0.5X>0.5, and X≈0.72𝑋0.72Xapprox 0.72, respectively. For Ds​1​subscript𝐷𝑠12460Ds1, Ref. citebib.bib28 and Ref. citebib.bib42 found that 0.4<X<0.70.4𝑋0.70.4<X<0.7 and X≈0.57𝑋0.57Xapprox 0.57, respectively. Our results [0.81≤X≤10.81𝑋10.81leq Xleq 1 for Ds​0∗​subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠02317D*s0 and 0.55≤X≤10.55𝑋10.55leq Xleq 1 for Ds​1​subscript𝐷𝑠12460Ds1] are similar to the previous works; Ds​0∗​subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠02317D*s0 is relatively composite dominant, and Ds​1​subscript𝐷𝑠12460Ds1 can contain an appreciable amount of the noncomposite components. The quantitative difference of the results of Ds​1​subscript𝐷𝑠12460Ds1 may be attributed to the large uncertainty ξint=0.345subscript𝜉int0.345xirm int=0.345 which indicates that the binding energy of Ds​1​subscript𝐷𝑠12460Ds1 is not sufficiently small. We note that the inputs a0subscript𝑎0a0 and resubscript𝑟𝑒re for the charmed-strange mesons still have a large uncertainty, for instance a0​(K​D)=+1.3±0.5±0.1subscript𝑎0𝐾𝐷plus-or-minus1.30.50.1a0(KD)=+1.3pm 0.5pm 0.1 fm and re​(K​D)=−0.1±0.3±0.1subscript𝑟𝑒𝐾𝐷plus-or-minus0.10.30.1re(KD)=-0.1pm 0.3pm 0.1 fmcitebib.bib42. The structure of X​𝑋3872X was studied in the hybrid model of c​c¯𝑐¯𝑐cbarc and hadronic moleculescitebib.bib48. Assuming the wavefunction of X​𝑋3872X as |X​⟩ket𝑋3872displaystyleketX =c1​|c​c¯⟩+c2​|D0​D¯∗0⟩+c3​|D+​D∗−⟩,absentsubscript𝑐1ket𝑐¯𝑐subscript𝑐2ketsuperscript𝐷0superscript¯𝐷absent0subscript𝑐3ketsuperscript𝐷superscript𝐷absentdisplaystyle=c1ketcbarc+c2ketD0barD*0+c3ketD+D*-, they determined the coefficients cisubscript𝑐𝑖ci from the comparison with the experiments which lead to −0.947≤c2≤−0.8710.947subscript𝑐20.871-0.947leq c2leq-0.871. Because the D0​D¯∗0superscript𝐷0superscript¯𝐷absent0D0barD*0 compositeness of X​𝑋3872X corresponds to X=|c2|2𝑋superscriptsubscript𝑐22X=|c2|2, this result is interpreted as 0.759≤X≤0.8970.759𝑋0.8970.759leq Xleq 0.897. Our model-independent result 0.53≤X≤10.53𝑋10.53leq Xleq 1 contains that of the model calculationcitebib.bib48, as expected.
content_sections.VI Summary: In this work, we have discussed the range correction to the weak-binding relation for the systems with a large effective range. We introduce an effective field theory to deal with the bound states in various models. Based on the effective range model in the zero range limit, we show the necessity of the range correction in the weak-binding relation. A prescription of the range correction is presented as the redefinition of Rtypsubscript𝑅typRrm typ in the correction terms as the maximum length scale among the interaction range Rintsubscript𝑅intRrm int and the length scale in the effective range expansion Reffsubscript𝑅effRrm eff. This range correction results in the modification of the uncertainty estimation of the compositeness, which should be performed in conjunction with the definition of the compositeness. The applicability of the weak-binding relations has been studied numerically with the effective range model (X=1𝑋1X=1) and the resonance model (X<1𝑋1X<1). In both cases, we show that the range correction improves the weak-binding relation with the larger applicable region than the previous one. We have also studied the precision of the estimation of X𝑋X to calculate the magnitude of the uncertainty E¯¯𝐸barE. Finally, we study the compositeness of the actual hadrons, nuclei and atomic states by the weak-binding relation with the range correction. All of states we discuss [the deuteron, X​𝑋3872X, Ds​0∗​subscriptsuperscript𝐷𝑠02317D*s0, Ds​1​subscript𝐷𝑠12460Ds1, N​Ω𝑁ΩNOmega dibaryon, Ω​ΩΩΩOmegaOmega dibaryon, HΛ3superscriptsubscriptHΛ33Lambdarm H, and He4superscriptHe44rm He dimer] are contained in the region where the meaningful estimation can be performed (E¯≲0.5less-than-or-similar-to¯𝐸0.5barElesssim 0.5). Our final results in Table show that these are basically composite dominant states. In particular, the deuteron compositeness is reasonably estimated as 0.74≤X≤10.74𝑋10.74leq Xleq 1. The importance of the range correction is found for the N​Ω𝑁ΩNOmega dibaryon and X​𝑋3872X whose compositeness is not properly estimated by the previous weak-binding relation. For the future prospects, it is important to discuss the range correction to the weak-binding relation for the unstable states because most of the exotic hadron candidates are resonances with a finite decay width. To this end, it is needed to establish the procedures of the numerical calculation for the unstable states with complex quantities. The study of the compositeness of Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc is also an interesting subject, because the magnitude of the effective range is expected to be largecitebib.bib8. While the current determination of resubscript𝑟𝑒re still suffers from the large uncertainty, we expect that the compositeness of Tc​csubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc can be determined by the weak-binding relation when the uncertainty of resubscript𝑟𝑒re reduces in future experiments. Acknowledgements. The authors thank Yusuke Nishida for the useful discussion on the weak-binding relation and the zero-range interactions during the ECT* workshop in 2019, which partly motivated this work. This work has been supported in part by the Grants-in-Aid for Scientific Research from JSPS (Grants No. JP22K03637, No. JP19H05150, No. JP18H05402, andNo. JP16K17694). This work was supported by JST, the establishment of university fellowships towards the creation of science technology innovation, Grant No. JPMJFS2139.

all_papers.title: Composite nature of $Z_b$ states from data analysis
all_papers.abstract:
We use a near-threshold parameterization with explicit inclusion of the
Castillejo-Dalitz-Dyson poles, which is more general than the effective range
expansion, to study the bottomonium-like states $Z_b(10610)$ and $Z_b(10650)$.
In terms of the partial-wave amplitude, we fit the event number distribution of
$B^{(*)}\bar B^*$ system to the experimental data for these resonances from
Belle Collaboration. The data could be described very well in our method, which
supports the molecular interpretation. Then the relevant physical quantities
are obtained, including the $B^{(*)}\bar{B}^*$ scattering length ($a$),
effective range ($r$), and residue squared ($\gamma_s^2$) of the pole in the
complex plane. In particular, we find the compositeness can range from about
0.4 up to 1 for the $B\bar B^*$ ($B^*\bar B^*$) component in the resonance
$Z_b(10610)$ ($Z_b(10650)$).

sections.31: James, CERN Program Library Long Writeup D506 , Version 94.1 (1994).

all_papers.title: Generalization of Weinberg's Compositeness Relations
all_papers.abstract:
We generalize the time-honored Weinberg's compositeness relations by
including the range corrections through considering a general form factor. In
Weinberg's derivation, he considered the effective range expansion up to
$\mathcal{O}(p^2)$ and made two additional approximations: neglecting the
non-pole term in the Low equation; approximating the form factor by a constant.
We lift the second approximation, and work out an analytic expression for the
form factor. For a positive effective range, the form factor is of a
single-pole form. An integral representation of the compositeness is obtained
and is expected to have a smaller uncertainty than that derived from Weinberg's
relations. We also establish an exact relation between the wave function of a
bound state and the phase of the scattering amplitude neglecting the non-pole
term. The deuteron is analyzed as an example, and the formalism can be applied
to other cases where range corrections are important.

all_papers.title:
How much is the compositeness of a bound state constrained by $a$ and
  $r_0$? The role of the interaction range
all_papers.abstract:
We present an approach that allows one to obtain information on the
compositeness of molecular states from combined information of the scattering
length of the hadronic components, the effective range, and the binding energy.
We consider explicitly the range of the interaction in the formalism and show
it to be extremely important to improve on the formula of Weinberg obtained in
the limit of very small binding and zero range interaction. The method allows
obtaining good information also in cases where the binding is not small. We
explicitly apply it to the case of the deuteron and the $D^{*}_{s0}(2317)$ and
$D^{*}_{s1}(2460)$ states and determine simultaneously the value of the
compositeness within a certain range, as well as get qualitative information on
the range of the interaction.

all_papers.title: Coupled-channel approach to $T_{cc}^+$ including three-body effects
all_papers.abstract:
A coupled-channel approach is applied to the charged tetraquark state
$T_{cc}^+$ recently discovered by the LHCb Collaboration. The parameters of the
interaction are fixed by a fit to the observed line shape in the three-body
$D^0D^0\pi^+$ channel. Special attention is paid to the three-body dynamics in
the $T_{cc}^+$ due to the finite life time of the $D^*$. An approach to the
$T_{cc}^+$ is argued to be self-consistent only if both manifestations of the
three-body dynamics, the pion exchange between the $D$ and $D^*$ mesons and the
finite $D^*$ width, are taken into account simultaneously to ensure that
three-body unitarity is preserved. This is especially important to precisely
extract the pole position in the complex energy plane whose imaginary part is
very sensitive to the details of the coupled-channel scheme employed. The
$D^0D^0$ and $D^0D^+$ invariant mass distributions, predicted based on this
analysis, are in good agreement with the LHCb data. The low-energy expansion of
the $D^*D$ scattering amplitude is performed and the low-energy constants (the
scattering length and effective range) are extracted. The compositeness
parameter of the $T_{cc}^+$ is found to be close to unity, which implies that
the $T_{cc}^+$ is a hadronic molecule generated by the interactions in the
$D^{*+}D^0$ and $D^{*0}D^+$ channels. Employing heavy-quark spin symmetry, an
isoscalar $D^*D^*$ molecular partner of the $T_{cc}^+$ with $J^P=1^+$ is
predicted under the assumption that the $ DD^*$-$D^*D^*$ coupled-channel
effects can be neglected.

all_papers.title: Quark mass dependence of the $D_{s0}^*(2317)$ and $D_{s1}(2460)$ resonances
all_papers.abstract: We determine the quark mass dependence - light and heavy - of the $D_{s0}^*(2317)$ and $D_{s1}(2460)$ properties, such as, mass, coupling to $DK$, scattering lengths and compositeness, from a global analysis of $DK$ energy levels from LQCD. In particular, we analyze the HSC energy levels for $DK$ scattering in $I=0$ for different boosts and two pion masses. The formalism is based in the local hidden-gauge interaction of Weinberg-Tomozawa type which respects chiral and heavy quark spin symmetries, supplemented by a term that takes into account the $D^{(*)}K$ coupling to a bare $c\bar{s}$ component. The isospin violating decay of the $D_{s0}^*(2317)\to D_{s}^+\pi^0$ is also evaluated.

all_papers.title: Weinberg's Compositeness
all_papers.abstract:
Nearly 60 years ago Weinberg suggested a criterion for particle
"compositeness", which has acquired new life with the discovery of new, exotic
hadrons. His idea resonates with model-based intuition. I discuss the role it
plays in the context of another of Weinberg's creations, the model-independent
framework of effective field theories.

all_papers.title: Compositeness of hadrons, nuclei, and atomic systems
all_papers.abstract: Recent observations of exotic hadrons have stimulated the theoretical investigation of the internal structure of hadrons. While all hadrons are ultimately composed of quarks and gluons bound by the strong interaction, quark clustering phenomena can generate hadronic molecules -- weakly bound systems of hadrons -- which are expected to emerge near two-hadron thresholds. However, it should be noted that a pure hadronic molecule is not realized, as the strong interaction induces mixing with other possible configurations. The compositeness of hadrons has been developed as a promising concept to quantitatively characterize the fraction of the hadronic molecular component. Here we summarize the modern understanding of the compositeness to study the internal structure of hadrons and review the application of the compositeness to various quantum systems in different energy scales, such as nuclei and atomic systems, in addition to hadrons.

all_papers.title: The isospin and compositeness of the $T_{cc}(3875)$ state
all_papers.abstract:
We perform a fit to the LHCb data on the $T_{cc}(3875)$ state in order to
determine its nature. We use a general framework that allows to have the $D^0
D^{*+}$, $D^+ D^{*0}$ components forming a molecular state, as well as a
possible nonmolecular state or contributions from missing coupled channels.
From the fits to the data we conclude that the state observed is clearly of
molecular nature from the $D^0 D^{*+}$, $D^+ D^{*0}$ components and the
possible contribution of a nonmolecular state or missing channels is smaller
than 3\%, compatible with zero. We also determine that the state has isospin
$I=0$ with a minor isospin breaking from the different masses of the channels
involved, and the probabilities of the $D^0 D^{*+}$, $D^+ D^{*0}$ channels are
of the order of 69\% and 29\% with uncertainties of 1\%. The differences
between these probabilities should not be interpreted as a measure of the
isospin violation. Due to the short range of the strong interaction where the
isospin is manifested, the isospin nature is provided by the couplings of the
state found to the $D^0 D^{*+}$, $D^+ D^{*0}$ components, and our results for
these couplings indicate that we have an $I=0$ state with a very small isospin
breaking. We also find that the potential obtained provides a repulsive
interaction in $I=1$, preventing the formation of an $I=1$ state, in agreement
with what is observed in the experiment.

all_papers.title: A New-Old Approach to Composite Scalars with Chiral Fermion Constituents
all_papers.abstract:
We develop a dynamical, Lorentz invariant theory of composite scalars in
configuration space consisting of chiral fermions, interacting by the
perturbative exchange of a massive "gluon" of coupling $g_0$ and mass $M_0^2$
(the coloron model). The formalism is inspired by, but goes beyond, old ideas
of Yukawa and the Nambu-Jona-Lasinio (NJL) model. It yields a non-pointlike
internal wave-function of the bound state, $\phi(r)$, which satisfies a
Schr\"odinger-Klein-Gordon (SKG) equation with eigenvalue $\mu^2$. For
super-critical coupling, $g_0 >g_{0c}$, we have $\mu^2< 0$ leading to
spontaneous symmetry breaking. The binding of chiral fermions is semiclassical,
and not loop-level as in NJL. The mass scale is determined by the interaction
as in NJL. We mainly focus on the short-distance, large $M_0^2$ limit, yielding
an NJL pointlike interaction, but the bound state internal wave-function,
$\phi(r)$, remains spatially extended and dilutes $\phi(0)$. This leads to
power-law suppression of the induced Yukawa and quartic couplings and requires
radically less fine-tuning of a hierarchy than does the NJL model. We include a
discussion of loop corrections of the theory. A realistic top--condensation
model appears possible.

all_papers.title: Inverse scattering problem with a bare state
all_papers.abstract:
In hadron physics, molecular-like multihadron states can interact with
compact multiquark states. The latter are modeled as bare states in the Hilbert
space of a potential model. In this work, we study several potential models
relevant to the bare state, and solve their inverse scattering problems. The
first model, called "cc", is a separable potential model. We show that it can
approximate (single-channel short-range) $S$-wave near-threshold physics with
an error of $\mathcal{O}(\beta^3/M_V^3)$, where $\beta$ sets the maximum
momentum of the near-threshold region and $M_V$ is the typical scale of the
potential. The second model, called "bc", serves as the bare-state-dominance
approximation, where interaction between continuum states is ignored. Under
this model, even though the bare state is always crucial for a bound state's
generation, a shallow bound state naturally tends to have a small bare-state
proportion. Therefore, we need other quantities to quantify the importance of
the bare state. The last model, called "bcc", is a combination of the first two
models. This model not only serves as a correction to the bare-state-dominance
approximation, but can also be used to understand the interplay between quark
and hadron degrees of freedom. This model naturally leads to the presence of a
Castillejo-Dalitz-Dyson (CDD) zero. We consider the energy decomposition of a
bound state. The potential ratio of the bare-continuum interaction to the
continuum self-interaction is proposed to understand how the bound state is
generated. Model independently, an inequality for the potential ratio is
derived. Based on the model "bcc", the CDD zero can be used to estimate the
potential ratio. Finally, we apply these studies to the deuteron, $\rho$ meson,
and $D_{s0}^*(2317)$, and analyze their properties.

all_papers.title: Triply charm and bottom tetraquarks in a constituent quark model
all_papers.abstract:
Singly, doubly and fully charmed tetraquark candidates, \emph{e.g.},
$T_{c\bar{s}}(2900)$, $T^+_{cc}(3875)$ and $X(6900)$ have been recently
reported by the LHCb collaboration. Therefore, it is timely to implement a
theoretical investigation on triply heavy tetraquark systems; herein, the
S-wave triply charm and bottom tetraquarks, $\bar{Q}Q\bar{q}Q$
$(q=u,\,d,\,s;\,Q=c,\,b)$, with spin-parity $J^P=0^+$, $1^+$ and $2^+$, isospin
$I=0$ and $\frac{1}{2}$, are systematically studied in a constituent quark
model. Besides, all tetraquark configurations, \emph{i.e.} meson-meson,
diquark-antidiquark and K-type arrangements, along with any allowed color
structure, are comprehensively considered. The Gaussian expansion method (GEM),
in combination with the complex-scaling method (CSM), which is quite ingenious
in dealing with either bound or resonances, is the approach adopted in solving
the complex scaled Schr\"odinger equation. This theoretical framework has
already been applied in various tetra- and penta-quark systems. In a fully
coupled-channel calculation within the GEM$+$CSM, narrow resonances are found
in each $I(J^P)$ channel of the charm and bottom sector. In particular, triply
charm and bottom tetraquark resonances are obtained in $5.6-5.9$ GeV and
$15.3-15.7$ GeV, respectively. We provide also some insights of the
compositeness of these exotic states, such as the inner quark distance,
magnetic moment and dominant wave function component. All this may help to
distinguish them in future high energy nuclear and particle experiments.

all_papers.title: Isospin violation effect and three-body decays of the $T_{cc}^{+}$ state
all_papers.abstract:
In this work, we make a study of $T_{cc}^+$ state observed by the LHCb
collaboration in 2021. In obtaining the effective potentials using the
One-Boson-Exchange Potential Model we use an exponential form factor, and find
that in the short and medium range, the contributions of the $\pi$, $\rho$ and
$\omega$ exchanges are comparable while in the long range the pion-exchange
contribution is dominant. Based on the assumption that $T_{cc}^+$ is a loosely
bound state of $D^*D$, we focus on its three-body decay using the
meson-exchange method. Considering that the difference between the thresholds
of $D^{*+}D^0$ and $D^{*0}D^+$ is even larger than the binding energy of
$T_{cc}^+$, the isospin-breaking effect is amplified by the small binding
energy of $T_{cc}^+$. Explicitly including such an isospin-breaking effect we
obtain, by solving the Schr\"{o}dinger equation, that the probability of the
isoscalar component is about $91\%$ while that of the isovector component is
around $9\%$ for $T_{cc}^+$. Using the experimental value of the mass of
$T_{cc}^+$ as an input, we obtain the wave function of $T_{cc}^+$ and further
obtain its width via the three-body hadronic as well as the radiative decays.
The total width we obtain is in agreement with the experimental value of the
LHCb measurement with a unitarised Breit-Wigner profile. Conversely, the
current results support the conclusion that $T_{cc}^+$ is a hadronic molecule
of $D^*D$.

all_papers.abstract:
A coupled channel analysis of the $D^{\ast+}D^0$ and $D^{\ast0}D^+$ system is
performed to study the doubly charmed $T_{cc}^+$ state recently discovered by
the LHCb collaboration. We use a simple model for the scattering amplitude that
allows us to describe well the experimental spectrum, and obtain the $T_{cc}^+$
pole in the coupled channel $T$-matrix. We find that this bound state has a
large molecular component. The isospin ($I=0$ or $I=1$) of the state cannot be
inferred from the $D^0 D^0 \pi^+$ spectrum alone. Therefore, we use the same
formalism to predict other $DD\pi$ spectra. In the case the $T_{cc}^+$ has
$I=1$, we also predict the location of the other two members ($T_{cc}^{++}$ and
$T_{cc}^0$) of the triplet. Finally, using Heavy-Quark Spin Symmetry, we
predict the location of possible heavier $D^\ast D^\ast$ ($I=0$ or $I=1$)
partners.

all_papers.title: Role of the effective range in the weak-binding relation
all_papers.abstract:
We study the range correction in the weak-binding relation, which relates the
internal structure of hadrons with the scattering length and the binding
energy. Utilizing the effective field theories, we show that the effective
range originates from the derivative coupling interaction as well as from the
channel coupling to the bare state, and that the different contributions are
not distinguishable. By examining the compositeness in the effective field
theories, it is demonstrated that the effective range induces the finite range
correction for the weak-binding relation in addition to the previously known
contributions. We thus propose to include the range correction in the
uncertainty terms of the weak-binding relation.

all_papers.title: Effective range expansion for narrow near-threshold resonances
all_papers.abstract:
We discuss some general features of the effective range expansion, the
content of its parameters with respect to the nature of the pertinent
near-threshold states and the necessary modifications in the presence of
coupled channels, isospin violations and unstable constituents. As illustrative
examples, we analyse the properties of the $\chi_{c1}(3872)$ and $T_{cc}^+$
states supporting the claim that these exotic states have a predominantly
molecular nature.

all_papers.title: Telling compositeness at a distance with outer automorphisms and CP
all_papers.abstract:
We investigate charge-parity (CP) and non-CP outer automorphism of groups and
the transformation behavior of group representations under them. We identify
situations where composite and elementary states that transform in exactly the
same representation of the group, transform differently under outer
automorphisms. This can be instrumental in discriminating composite from
elementary states solely by their quantum numbers with respect to the outer
automorphism, i.e. without the need for explicit short distance scattering
experiments. We discuss under what conditions such a distinction is
unequivocally possible. We cleanly separate the case of symmetry constrained
(representation) spaces from the case of multiple copies of identical
representations in flavor space, and identify conditions under which
non-trivial transformation in flavor space can be enforced for composite
states. Next to composite product states, we also discuss composite states in
non-product representations. Comprehensive examples are given based on the
finite groups $\Sigma(72)$ and $D_8$. The discussion also applies to
$\mathrm{SU}(N)$ and we scrutinize recent claims in the literature that
$\mathrm{SU}(2N)$ outer automorphism with antisymmetric matrices correspond to
distinct outer automorphisms. We show that outer automorphism transformations
with antisymmetric matrices are related by an inner automorphism to the
standard $\mathbb{Z}_2$ outer automorphism of $\mathrm{SU}(N)$. As a direct
implication, no non-trivial transformation behavior can arise for composite
product states under the outer automorphism of $\mathrm{SU}(N)$.

all_papers.title: The role of the pion in the lineshape of the $X(3872)$
all_papers.abstract:
We determine the contribution of long-range pion interactions to the
$X(3872)$ dynamics, assuming it is a loosely bound $D^0 \bar{D}^{*0}$ molecule.
Our result is based on the distorted wave Born approximation in
non-relativistic quantum mechanics. Despite their long-range nature, we find
that pion interactions cannot produce a large and negative effective range.
Nonetheless, they introduce imaginary parts. In particular, they contribute to
the total decay width of the $X(3872)$ with a term associated with, but not
precisely corresponding to, the $D^*$ width. Our approach can also be applied
to the recently discovered $T_{cc}^+$ states.

all_papers.title: Application of the weak-binding relation with range correction
all_papers.abstract:
The weak-binding relation is a useful tool to study the internal structure of
hadrons from the observable quantities. We introduce the range correction in
the weak-binding relation for the system having a sizable magnitude of the
effective range, and show that the applicability of the weak-binding relation
can be enlarged by the range correction. Thanks to the low-energy universality,
the weak-binding relation can be used to study the structure of shallow bound
states in any systems with different length scales. We apply the weak-binding
relation to actual systems, including hadrons, hypernuclei, and atoms and show
the importance of the range correction.

all_papers.title: Role of bound states and resonances in scalar QFT at nonzero temperature
all_papers.abstract:
We study the thermal properties of quantum field theories (QFT) with
three-leg interaction vertices $g\varphi^{3}$ and $gS\varphi^{2}$ ($\varphi$
and $S$ being scalar fields), which constitute the relativistic counterpart of
the Yukawa potential. We follow a non-perturbative unitarized one-loop resummed
technique for which the theory is unitary and well-defined for a large range of
values of the coupling constant $g$. Using the partial wave decomposition of
two-body scattering we calculate the phase shifts, whose derivatives are used
to infer the pressure of the system at nonzero temperature by using the
so-called phase shift formalism. A $\varphi \varphi$ bound state is formed when
the coupling $g$ is larger than a certain critical value. As the main outcomes
of this work, we estimate the influence of particle interaction on the pressure
(both without and with the bound state), and we demonstrate that the latter is
always continuous as a function of the coupling constant $g$ (no sudden jumps
occurs when the bound state forms), and we show that the contribution of the
bound state to the pressure does not count as \textit{one} state in the thermal
gas, since a cancellation with the residual $\varphi \varphi$ interaction
occurs. The amount of this cancellation depends on the details of the model and
its parameters and a variety of possible scenarios is presented. %Moreover,
even when no bound state occurs, we estimate the role of the interaction
(including a resonance in the $gS\varphi^{2}$ theory), which is in general
non-negligible. We also show how the overall effect of the interaction,
including eventual resonances and bound states, can be formally described by a
unique expression that makes use of the phase shift continued below the
threshold.

all_papers.title:
Cut structures and an observable singularity in the three-body threshold
  dynamics: the $T_{cc}^+$ case
all_papers.abstract:
The three-body threshold effect, the distinctive and intriguing
non-perturbative dynamics in the low-energy hadron-hadron scattering, has
acquired compelling significance in the wake of the recent observation of the
double-charm tetraquark $T_{cc}^+$. This dynamics is characterized by the
emergence of singular points and branch cuts within the interaction potential,
occurring when the on-shell condition of the mediated particle is satisfied.
The presence of these potential singularities indicates that the system is no
longer Hermitian and also poses intractable challenges in obtaining exact
solutions for dynamical scattering amplitudes. In this work, we develop a
complex scaled Lippmann-Schwinger equation as an operation of analytical
continuation of the $T$ matrix to resolve this problem. Through a practical
application to the $DD^* \to DD^*$ process, we reveal complicated cut
structures of the three-body threshold dynamics in the complex plane, primarily
stemming from the one-pion exchange. Notably, our methodology succeeds in
reproducing the $T_{cc}^+$ structure, in alignment with the quasi-bound pole
derived from the complex scaling method within the Schr\"odinger equation
framework. More remarkably, after solving the on-shell $T$ matrix on the
positive real axis of momentum plane, we find an extra new structure in the
$DD^*$ mass spectrum, which arises from a right-hand cut at a physical pion
mass and should be observable in Lattice QCD simulations and future high-energy
experiments.

all_papers.title:
Femtoscopic study on $DD^*$ and $D\bar{D}^*$ interactions for $T_{cc}$
  and $X(3872)$
all_papers.abstract:
We investigate $DD^*$ and $D\bar{D}^*$ momentum correlations in high-energy
collisions to elucidate the nature of $T_{cc}$ and $X(3872)$ exotic hadrons.
Single range Gaussian potentials with the channel couplings to the isospin
partners are constructed based on the empirical data. The momentum correlation
functions of the $D^0D^{*+}$, $D^+D^{*0}$, $D^0\bar{D}^{*0}$, and $D^+D^{*-}$
pairs are computed with including the coupled-channel effects. We discuss how
the nature of the exotic states are reflected in the behaviors of the
correlation results.

all_papers.title:
Productions of $D^*_{s0}(2317)$ and $D_{s1}(2460)$ in $B_{(s)}$ and
  $\Lambda_b(\Xi_b)$ decays
all_papers.abstract:
Recent studies show that $D_{s0}^{\ast}(2317)$ and $D_{s1}(2460)$ contain
large molecular components. In this work, we employ the naive factorization
approach to calculate the production rates of $D_{s0}^{\ast}(2317)$ and
$D_{s1}(2460)$ as hadronic molecules in $B_{(s)}$ and $\Lambda_b(\Xi_b)$
decays, where their decay constants are estimated in the effective Lagrangian
approach. With the so-obtained decay constants $f_{D_{s0}^{\ast}(2317)}$ and
$f_{D_{s1}(2460)}$, we calculate the branching fractions of the $b$-meson
decays $B_{(s)}\to \bar{D}_{(s)}^{(*)}D_{s0}^*$ and $B_{(s)}\to
\bar{D}_{(s)}^{(*)}D_{s1}$ and the $b$-baryon decays $\Lambda_b(\Xi_{b}) \to
\Lambda_c(\Xi_{c}) D_{s0}^*$ and $\Lambda_b(\Xi_{b}) \to \Lambda_c(\Xi_c)
D_{s1}$. Our results show that the production rates of $D_{s0}^{\ast}(2317)$
and $D_{s1}(2460)$ in the $B_s$, $\Lambda_b$ and $\Xi_b$ decays are rather
large that future experiments could observe them. In particular, we demonstrate
that one can extract the decay constants of hadronic molecules via the triangle
mechanism because of the equivalence of the triangle mechanism to the tree
diagram established in calculating the decays $B \to
\bar{D}^{(*)}D_{s0}^{\ast}(2317)$ and $B \to \bar{D}^{(*)}D_{s1}(2460)$.

all_papers.title:
The investigations of the $P$-wave $B_s$ states combining quark model
  and lattice QCD in the coupled channel framework
all_papers.abstract:
Combining the quark model, the quark-pair-creation mechanism and $B^{(*)}\bar
K$ interaction, we have investigated the near-threshold $P$-wave $B_s$ states
in the framework of the Hamiltonian effective field theory. With the heavy
quark flavor symmetry, all the parameters are determined in the $D_s$ sector by
fitting the lattice data. The masses of the bottom-strange partners of the
$D^{*}_{s0}(2317)$ and $D^{*}_{s1}(2460)$ are predicted to be
$M_{B^{*}_{s0}}=5730.2_{-1.5}^{+2.4}$ MeV and $M_{B^{*}_{s1}}=
5769.6_{-1.6}^{+2.4}$ MeV, respectively, which are well consistent with the
lattice QCD simulation. The two $P$-wave $B_s$ states are the mixtures of the
bare $\bar b s$ core and $B^{(*)}\bar K$ component. Moreover, we find a
crossing point between the energy levels with and without the interaction
Hamiltonian in the finite volume spectrum in the $0^+$ case, which corresponds
to a CDD (Castillejo-Dalitz-Dyson) zero in the $T$-matrix of the $B\bar K$
scattering. This CDD zero will help deepen the insights of the near-threshold
states and can be examined by future lattice calculation.

all_papers.title: Spectroscopic Properties of Double-Strangeness Molecular Tetraquarks
all_papers.abstract:
Inspired by recent advances in the study of the $K^{(*)} \bar K^{(*)}$
molecular tetraquarks and the $H$-dibaryon, we focus on the spectroscopic
properties of the $\bar K^{(*)} \bar K^{(*)}$ systems, which exhibit exotic
flavor quantum number of $ss\bar q \bar q$. A dynamical analysis is performed
using the one-boson-exchange model to describe the effective interactions for
these systems, accounting for both $S$-$D$ wave mixing and coupled-channel
effects. By solving the coupled-channel Schr$\ddot{\rm o}$dinger equation, we
identify the $I(J^P)=0(1^+)$ $\bar K \bar K^*$ and $I(J^P)=0(1^+)$ $\bar K^*
\bar K^*$ states as the most likely candidates for double-strangeness molecular
tetraquarks. Furthermore, we estimate their strong decay behaviors based on the
effective Lagrangian approach, with several channels exhibiting considerable
decay widths. Meanwhile, we investigate their magnetic moments and M1 radiative
decay widths, shedding light on their inner structures, within the constituent
quark model. Finally, we encourage experimentalists to focus on these predicted
double-strangeness molecular tetraquark candidates, particularly in $B$ meson
decays. Such efforts could pave the way for establishing the molecular
tetraquark states in the light-quark sector.

all_papers.title:
Higher molecular $P_{\psi s}^{\Lambda/\Sigma}$ pentaquarks arising from
  the $\Xi_c^{(\prime,*)}\bar D_1/\Xi_c^{(\prime,*)}\bar D_2^*$ interactions
all_papers.abstract:
The discoveries of the $P_{\psi s}^\Lambda(4459)$ and $P_{\psi
s}^\Lambda(4338)$ as the potential $\Xi_c\bar D^{(*)}$ molecules have sparked
our curiosity in exploring a novel class of molecular $P_{\psi
s}^{\Lambda/\Sigma}$ pentaquarks. In this study, we carry out an investigation
into the higher molecular pentaquarks, specifically focusing on the $P_{\psi
s}^{\Lambda/\Sigma}$ states arising from the $\Xi_c^{(\prime,*)}\bar
D_1/\Xi_c^{(\prime,*)}\bar D_2^*$ interactions. Our approach employs the
one-boson-exchange model, incorporating both the $S$-$D$ wave mixing effect and
the coupled channel effect. Our numerical results suggest that the $\Xi_c\bar
D_1$ states with $I(J^P)=0({1}/{2}^+,\,{3}/{2}^+)$, the $\Xi_c\bar D_2^*$
states with $I(J^P)=0({3}/{2}^+,\,{5}/{2}^+)$, the $\Xi_c^{\prime}\bar D_1$
states with $I(J^P)=0({1}/{2}^+,\,{3}/{2}^+)$, the $\Xi_c^{\prime}\bar D_2^*$
states with $I(J^P)=0({3}/{2}^+,\,{5}/{2}^+)$, the $\Xi_c^{*}\bar D_1$ states
with $I(J^P)=0({1}/{2}^+,\,{3}/{2}^+,\,{5}/{2}^+)$, and the $\Xi_c^{*}\bar
D_2^*$ states with $I(J^P)=0({1}/{2}^+,\,{3}/{2}^+,\,{5}/{2}^+,\,{7}/{2}^+)$
can be recommended as the most promising molecular $P_{\psi s}^\Lambda$
pentaquark candidates, and there may exist the potential molecular $P_{\psi
s}^\Sigma$ pentaquark candidates for several isovector $\Xi_c^{(\prime,*)}\bar
D_1/\Xi_c^{(\prime,*)}\bar D_2^*$ states. With the higher statistical data
accumulation at the LHCb's Run II and Run III status, there is the possibility
that our predicted $P_{\psi s}^{\Lambda/\Sigma}$ states can be detected through
the weak decay of the $\Xi_b$ baryon, especially in hunting for the predicted
$P_{\psi s}^\Lambda$ states.

all_papers.title:
How to unravel the nature of the $\Sigma^*(1430) (1/2^-)$ state from
  correlation functions
all_papers.abstract:
We calculate the correlation functions for the $\bar K^0 p, \pi^+ \Sigma^0,
\pi^0 \Sigma^+, \pi^+ \Lambda$, and $\eta \Sigma^+$ states, which in the chiral
unitary approach predict an excited $\Sigma^*(1/2^-)$ state at the $\bar K N$
threshold, recently observed by the Belle Collaboration. Once this is done, we
tackle the inverse problem of seeing how much information one can obtain from
these correlation functions. With the resampling method, one can determine the
scattering parameters of all the channels with relative precision by means of
the analysis in a general framework, and find a clear cusplike structure
corresponding to the $\Sigma^*(1/2^-)$ in the different amplitudes at the
$\bar{K}N$ threshold.

all_papers.title:
Reconciling experimental and lattice data of $Z_c(3900)$ in a
  $J/\psi\pi$-$D\bar{D}^*$ coupled-channel analysis
all_papers.abstract:
We study the $J/\psi \pi$ and $D\bar{D}^*$ coupled-channel system within a
covariant framework. The $J/\psi \pi$ and $D\bar{D}^*$ invariant-mass
distributions measured at 4.23~GeV and 4.26~GeV by BESIII and the finite-volume
energy levels from recent lattice QCD simulations are simultaneously fitted.
Phase shifts and inelasticities of the $J/\psi \pi$ and $D\bar{D}^*$ scattering
are predicted using the resulting amplitudes. Poles corresponding to the
$Z_c(3900)$ state are found in the complex energy plane and their couplings
with $J/\psi \pi$ and $D\bar{D}^*$ are determined. Our results indicate that
the current lattice data do not preclude the existence of a physical
$Z_c(3900)$ state.

all_papers.title: Doubly heavy tetraquark states in a mass splitting model
all_papers.abstract:
Treating the $X(4140)$ as a compact $J^{PC}=1^{++}$ $cs\bar{c}\bar{s}$ state
and using its mass as a reference scale, we systematically estimate the masses
of doubly heavy tetraquark states $QQ\bar{q}\bar{q}$ where $Q=c,b$ and
$q=u,d,s$. Their decay properties are studied with a simple rearrangement
scheme. Based on our results, the lowest $I(J^P)=0(1^+)$ $bb\bar{n}\bar{n}$
state is a stable tetraquark about 20 MeV below the $\bar{B}^*\bar{B}$
threshold. The mass and width of the low-mass $0(1^+)$ $cc\bar{n}\bar{n}$
($n=u,d$) tetraquark are compatible with the $T_{cc}(3875)^+$ observed by the
LHCb Collaboration. The location of the lowest $0(0^+)$ and $0(1^+)$
$bc\bar{n}\bar{n}$ states are found to be close to the $\bar{B}D$ and
$\bar{B}^*D$ thresholds, respectively. We hope that the predicted ratios
between partial widths of different channels may be helpful to identify compact
tetraquark states from future measurements.

core_knowledge_list.title: From the lineshape of the $X(3872)$ to its structure

qualitative: The Weinberg compositeness criterion provides a way to determine whether a bound state is elementary (with a non-zero Z) or composite (with Z=0), based on the overlap between the physical state and an elementary state in the theory.
A non-zero Z implies the existence of an elementary component in the bound state, meaning the state is not purely composite and has a compact core structure.
For a shallow molecular bound state (like a deuteron-like state), Z=0 and the effective range r0 is positive, indicating no elementary component and a purely interaction-generated bound state.
A negative effective range r0 is incompatible with a shallow molecular bound state and instead indicates the presence of an elementary component (Z>0), suggesting a compact, non-molecular structure.
The X(3872) has a negative effective range, which rules out a purely shallow D¯D* bound state and implies a compact hidden charm structure, likely a tetraquark, with short-distance QCD interactions.
The conclusion is reinforced by the general trend in exotic meson physics, including the discovery of four-quark states with hidden charm and strange valence quarks, and four-charm resonances.

The LHCb measurement of a negative effective range for X(3872) supports a structure where the state is generated by an elementary core dressed by D¯D* continuum states via short-distance color forces, rather than being a loosely-bound molecule.
quantitative: Z = |⟨𝔡|d⟩|², where Z ∈ [0,1], with Z=1 indicating a free elementary particle and Z=0 indicating a fully composite state.
The effective range r0 < 0 is incompatible with a shallow molecular bound state (which requires r0 > 0).
For the deuteron, r0 > -5.3 fm² is expected if it were elementary; a negative r0 below this threshold implies a non-molecular structure.
The Källén-Lehmann representation gives: Z + ∫₀^∞ dμ σ(μ) = 1, where σ(μ) ≥ 0 is the spectral density of multiparticle states.
The propagator of the elementary field Φ has a pole at the deuteron mass with residue Z.
The LHCb data indicate a sizeable negative effective range for X(3872), leading to the conclusion that Z > 0 and r0 < 0.
